> **Continuum Fitness;126 wrote:** I went on Ebay and got a cold water circulating machine (think igloo cooler with a water pump) and a 22 x 13 pad for 40 bucks used including shipping. Normally runs 150 -200 dollars new. It runs 40 degree water through the pad. I place it on my torso against my skin, underneath a compression shirt to hold it in place. Twice a day for 30-1 hr. works great!

What a great idea but I don't see the one for $40??

### Marsha — 2012-03-15T04:43:15+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/product-ideas-reviews-sources-for-implementing-cold-thermogenesis.38/post-465>

This might be useful for spot icing - part of protocol after/during face dunks and before total ice baths.

[http://www.bedbathandbeyond.com/product.asp?SKU=18700786&RN=200&](http://http://www.bedbathandbeyond.com/product.asp?SKU=18700786&RN=200&)

This gizmo cost ca. $10 and is said to 'melt the fat through heat that your body generates' - ya' right!...

... I don't care about that - only that it was big enough and strong enough to hold two large flexible ice bags - one for belly and one for back and still let me move around during the day. I think it works well with tights or compression hose/spanks against the skin. I zipped the thing on- to the tightest I could get, (has lots of zippers and fits up to 50" waist) then needed help to slip the ice bags in - more laughter from my husband.

### mshobbs — 2012-03-15T09:23:21+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/product-ideas-reviews-sources-for-implementing-cold-thermogenesis.38/post-632>

> My tub is so dang shallow I really need to figure out a way to cool my belly and upper half of my body. There is no way I can get completely submerged in my tub.

Trisha, I have the same problem with a shallow tub. I have found that by rolling over and lay on my tummy for a while does the trick! I prop myself on my elbows so I can adjust the amount of exposure I have. Got to the point where I do half on my back and the other half on my stomach.

### JC2K — 2012-03-15T19:15:20+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/product-ideas-reviews-sources-for-implementing-cold-thermogenesis.38/post-902>

I had looked at the ArcticHeat vest. Seemed to have pretty small amount of cooling for the cost. It's got 4 strips on the front and 3 on the back. The intent is really to keep people cool in the heat, not for weight loss though. The other downside is if you're large, and you do lose a lot of weight, the size of the vest doesn't change. So you'll need to use bands or something to keep it close to your body. So, if you've got the money to burn, it's probably ok, but I wouldn't spend the cash on it.

### grammasmitty@hotmail.com — 2012-03-16T05:25:36+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/product-ideas-reviews-sources-for-implementing-cold-thermogenesis.38/post-1288>

I plan to sew a front/back vest with velcro straps to hold it tight, with pockets to hold ice packs. I will also make a velcro wrap with ice pack pockets to wrap around my belly. Maybe something for my thighs, too. If you know someone who sews, it should be hard to do.

### Adriana — 2012-03-20T17:07:44+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/product-ideas-reviews-sources-for-implementing-cold-thermogenesis.38/post-3256>

I took a "Paleo Sabbatical" from mid-December to mid-March and come back to the CT rage...There's never a dull moment in Jack Kruse Land, is there?

After getting somewhat caught up on the topic I was at my chiropractor on the spinolator and water massage tables and wondered whether massage+cold would increase the effectiveness. As luck would have it, I went to Walmart to buy reusable ice pillow packs and compression shirts/ shorts and came across an " open box" Motor Massage Cushion for $19.99 which has settings for lumbar, back and sides. I plugged it in to a portable power pack with 12volt power. Topped it with my ice packs and here I am, chillin' in the comfort of my bed. I can lay it flat to do the front. It's not much of a massage, more of a vibration, but I'm hopping it will magnify the benefit.

### nonchalant — 2012-03-20T17:11:43+0200

### zoebarry@yahoo.com — 2012-06-02T18:41:24+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/product-ideas-reviews-sources-for-implementing-cold-thermogenesis.38/post-29071>

> **Groketteliveson;814 wrote:** Anyone have any better ways to get the water colder in the tub than just dumping bags of ice in? The ice just melts so fast. I've been using ice blocks which help but not much better. I need to buy a whole other freezer just for ice!

I dump a 20lb bag in the tub after it's filled with cold tap water. I found that if I added 2 cups of Epsom salts to the bath water as it is filling, the water stayed much colder and there were ice cubs left when I drained the tub after 55mins. Try the Epsom salts.

Grace

### jeanie@truelongmont.com — 2012-06-02T22:36:06+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/product-ideas-reviews-sources-for-implementing-cold-thermogenesis.38/post-29100>

I can't seem to get anywhere with anything but the ice cubes. The frozen blocks just don't seem to cool it the same for me.

I don't do icing anymore. I've figured out a computer/office set up for the tub. Got a $8 big shelf to go across tub.

I also don't do compression anymore. I think some of the "little" things Jack says help tend to help get you into it, but the net effect really isn't much different - so if you can do it without, go for it.

Also for plugging tub blue painters tape around the top drain. The plastic thingy in the reccomends section SUCKS.

# Thread: I'm First!
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/im-first.39/>
**Subforum:** The New Monster Thread
**Posts:** 6
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### AKMan — 2012-03-15T00:41:21+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/im-first.39/post-102>

Nothing really to say. Just wanted to be first! Ha. I figured I earned it, I have been following 'The Madman' around since early last year. One day, a person named DigitalSurgeon posted on Mark's Daily Apple, then a guy named TheQuilt started posting on PaleoHacks. I would regularly see 'Jack' or 'Dr. K.' on all the blogs I was reading. When I found out they were all the same person, I was flabbergasted. He reminds me of the chess savants you see in Central Park playing (and winning) 20 games at once, going from board to board leaving people perplexed. Well, good job, Dr. Kruse, Jack, Dr. K., Quilt, DigitalSurgeon, or whoever the heck you are today! I hope this forum takes off and you change the world!

### judithzthompson@hotmail.com — 2012-03-15T00:50:19+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/im-first.39/post-114>

I first came across Jack on Jimmy Moore's Facebook - I said way back then that this guy is going to rock the medical world. Friends and relatives think I'm nuts - do I care - NO, I shall grow old gracefully and healthfully thanks to Jack

### Birdy — 2012-03-15T00:56:12+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/im-first.39/post-124>

I first ran into a version of Jack on Paleohacks, and started the leptin reset after hearing Jimmy Moore's podcast.

### jreyn07@live.com — 2012-03-15T02:34:33+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/im-first.39/post-292>

I'm the 4th to post if I hit quick reply fast enough! So is our illustrious Dr. K going to respond to folk in this forum, as well as, in his blog?

### thomasina838@gmail.com — 2012-03-15T15:53:45+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/im-first.39/post-740>

i dont care about being first but i wanted to post the first thing i encountered from Dr. kruse that impressed me. "If you are hungry during the day then you didnt eat enough protein/fat for breakfast." easy enough to test. i cooked 12 oz of steak for breakfast the next day. couldnt eat it at once. so i cut it into little bits and ate one every few minutes until it was gone. took me a hour and a half to finish. i wasnt hungry for 10 hours. that was a huge liberation for a constant nibbler. this has plagued me my whole life and it was gone in one day. nov 10, 2011. i will never forget. doing the reset since then.

### gizmeaux@pacbell.net — 2012-03-18T04:42:54+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/im-first.39/post-2290>

Ditto, AkMan. I've been lurking here and there, following Dr. Kruse around for almost a year now. I went low-carb with Jimmy Moore in February 2010, went Paleo early last year, and am now having entirely too much fun eating well, face-dunking, spot-freezing and CTing. My husband has lost weight just by tagging along with me, though I haven't coaxed him into the water yet. We only had to lose about 25 pounds each, and we've met our weight goals. Many things have changed for the better since I found JK, and Optimum is around the corner! I really have enjoyed all the blog posts and comments, and feel like I know so many people here, even if they don't know me. Please know, everyone, that I'm urging you on even if I'm not very vocal.

# Thread: Hi
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hi.41/>
**Subforum:** Meet and Greet
**Posts:** 3
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Jane — 2012-03-15T00:43:49+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hi.41/post-107>

I'm from California. I just finished reading my way through the monster Leptin RX thread at Mark's Daily Apple, where I learned tons of info from Dr. Kruse and the stalwart members who have been my inspiration. You all rock!

I was diagnosed with asthma, high blood pressure and Type II Diabetes ten years ago. I've controlled the diabetes through diet for the last two years without meds and rarely have asthma attacks anymore. A few years ago I landed in the hospital for Atrial Fibrillation twice. Though my cardio doc says my thyroid tests look fine, I'm sure my adrenals are shot after 26 years of doing therapeutic foster care and making the hormonal roller-coaster menopause ride. I've had IBS-D dominant since I was 15, which the "specialists" have all told me to just "live" with it. I have about 100 pounds to lose. I've always been a "Night Owl" and have existed on 4 hours of sleep for eons. I believe I can correct all of these issues and get off of all of my meds through Optimal Living.

I went Gluten free 6 1/2 months ago. I'm on my 5th week of the Leptin RX and aiming for Optimal. My first goal is to heal my leaky gut. At this point, most everything is going straight through me so I've eliminated everything from my diet except GF ground beef, bacon, shellfish, fish, Coconut Mana and oil, and a few forkfuls of fermented sauerkraut along with some of the recommended supplements.

I've been Keto for awhile and have lost 20 pounds since starting the Reset and dropped two sizes. The constant pain in my upper abdomen is gone. I feel calmer even in stressful situations. My body composition is changing for the better.

Once my gut calms down and can handle more detox, I plan on adding the CT protocol. Anyway, that's my current plan. Something Dr. Kruse wrote in his last blog really hit home with me:

_If you want to know your pastÃ¢â‚¬Â¦Ã¢â‚¬Â¦..look into your present conditions.

If you want to know your futureÃ¢â‚¬Â¦Ã¢â‚¬Â¦..look into your present actions.

What you do right now determines what you will become.

_

I'm looking forward to sharing the Optimal Journey with you all!

Jane

### Lamree — 2012-03-15T00:48:49+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hi.41/post-111>

Hi Jane,

This is so encouraging and a brilliant example of what one can achieve when we take a step back, apply our intellect to a problem and take action - one of Dr. Kruse's messages which you have so poignantly highlighted in you post. Make sure you keep us all updated on your progress and good luck!

Best wishes,

Alex

### Birdy — 2012-03-15T00:52:35+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hi.41/post-118>

Welcome!!!!!!!!!!!!!!

I'm going with Factor X having something to do with our sense of smell connection to the brain that helps the creation of memories for better thinking and reasoning and needing more brain real-estate. When my kids reached the age of reason, smells that they used to ignore became more noticeable to them. As I'm aging, my once acute sense of smell seems dull.

### Souldanzer — 2012-03-15T03:06:16+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-the-h-is-factor-x-post-your-guesses.42/post-344>

> **Valorie33;175 wrote: Maybe Altitude.........
>
>
> I've been getting interested in this:
>
>
>
>
> > "**Atmospheric pressure below 760 mm Hg (1013.3 hPa) significantly affects intracranial pressure, arterial blood pressure, cerebral perfusion pressure and body temperature. Atmospheric pressure above 770 mm Hg (1026.6 hPa) does not affect intracranial pressure, arterial blood pressure, cerebral perfusion pressure or body temperature.
> >
> > Conclusions: Atmospheric pressure range of 768 mm Hg (1023.9 hPa) to 770 mm Hg (1026.6 hPa) is the border range to preserve intracranial homeostasis, below which qualitative changes of cerebral blood flow occur. **In the high range of atmospheric pressure its increase initiates biological protective mechanisms to maintain normal cerebral blood flow.** The mechanism involved in the influence of atmospheric pressure and environmental factors in general on intracranial pressure and other parameters of pressure-volume homeostasis has not been explained."
> >
> > **
>
> **
>
>
> Also, quite fascinating that there is a circadian cycle to atmospheric pressure... wonder if our bodies use this at all and if we have receptors for atmospheric pressure.
>
>
>
> Atmospheric pressure shows a diurnal or semidiurnal (twice-daily) cycle** caused by global atmospheric tides. This effect is strongest in tropical zones, with amplitude of a few millibars, and almost zero in polar areas. These variations have two superimposed cycles, a circadian (24 h) cycle and semi-circadian (12 h) cycle**.

Doubt it's Factor X, though....

### jenn — 2012-03-15T03:06:23+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-the-h-is-factor-x-post-your-guesses.42/post-345>

My guess is that Factor X has something to do with reduced oxygen during sleep, (our daily "hibernation") especially when combined with cold. My reasoning is that many species, even mammals, consume little to no oxygen during hibernation and emerge in spring healthy and with no apparent damage. With proper conditioning I think it's possible that humans could do this (some freedivers can survive underwater on greatly reduced oxygen for long periods of timed), but as a non-scientist I am unwilling to speculate on the potential benefits. Feel free to pipe up and tell me what you think!

Well I like this path, but I'm pretty sure that Factor X is going to be the reason for evolving a warm adapted metabolism. He is saying that the cold adapted metabolism is the ancient pathway. So what change in hominid evolution warranted the selective pressure to develop an entire metabolism to optimize for it? I.e. we need to be looking at causes for the warm adapted in early early hominids, not another piece to the holy trinity of optimal...

So, let's put them together. Due to \*something\* environmental, early hominids found a route in which they could rapidly produce epigenetic changes and this allowed them to prosper in the warm adapted environment, but it's at the cost of stem cells and telomere lengths due to the "hunting" and "searching" that the epigenetic changes require to find an optimal state. So then you cycle back in winter to the good old cold adapted state which lengthens the telomeres and produces autophagy to clean up the mess from summer.

???

### Souldanzer — 2012-03-15T08:03:33+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-the-h-is-factor-x-post-your-guesses.42/post-620>

Okay, I've been stuck with this for like, the last few hours

I keep coming back to atmospheric changes. Our genes evolved in the cold, that's our natural state. So we were born into an Ice Age somehow. We didn't know we were cold until we evolved into warmth (that's how I recall Dr. K explaining it to me at least ) and forgot about the ancient pathway.

So if we were born into an Ice Age, that means at some point the Ice Age was over and that resulted in atmospheric changes that can happen very rapidly according to this source:

> The carbon dioxide content of the atmosphere for the last 4 glacial cycles is known from drilling into the ice in Antarctica, where ancient air has been trapped and now can be extracted. These results show that carbon dioxide follows the change in sea level rather closely: when carbon dioxide increases, sea level rises and vice versa. A similar relationship is seen for methane. Most likely, changes in trace gases help drive sea level up and down, and the changes in sea level in turn change the content of trace gases in the atmosphere in a feedback loop. **Some of these climatic changes seem to be extremely rapid**.

This would go along with the speed of humans evolving and missing links b/c things happened so quickly. Factor X would be the change in atmospheric composition, most likely oxygen, and most likely related to hypoxia as the original human state from our present view.

I have no idea how this would have sped up evolution the way it did on a biochemical level but looks like a good explanation from the macro perspective, lol.

Okay, I'm ready to receive my "nope"  I learned a lot anyways.

Souldanzer you could be onto something!  From this art[http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/17805093](http://http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/17805093)icle, entitled, Effect of hypoxic "dose" on physiological responses and sea-level performance:

"The essence of LH+TL is that it allows athletes to "live high" for the purpose of facilitating altitude acclimatization, as manifest by a profound and sustained increase in endogenous erythropoietin (EPO) and ultimately an augmented erythrocyte volume, while simultaneously allowing athletes to "train low" for the purpose of replicating sea-level training intensity and oxygen flux, **thereby inducing beneficial metabolic and neuromuscular adaptations."**

Good question! I'm not sure yet but this may give us a clue to our mismatches. This quote is about life in the deep sea "Life appears to cope with pressure effects on biomolecules in two ways. First, their membranes and proteins have pressure-resistant structures that work by mechanisms not yet fully understood, but which also mean their biomolecules do not work well under low pressure in shallow waters. Second, some organisms external may use "piezolytes" (from the Greek "piezin" for pressure). These are small organic molecules recently discovered that somehow prevent pressure from distorting large biomolecules. One of these piezolytes is trimethylamine oxide external (TMAO). This molecule is familiar to most people because it gives rise to the fishy smell of marine fish and shrimp."

[http://marinebio.org/oceans/deep/](http://http://marinebio.org/oceans/deep/)

### Souldanzer — 2012-03-15T20:17:41+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-the-h-is-factor-x-post-your-guesses.42/post-948>

Dive Reflex...

> When the face is submerged, receptors that are sensitive to cold within the nasal cavity and other areas of the face supplied by cranial nerve V (trigeminal) relay the information to the brain and then innervate cranial nerve X, which is part of the autonomic nervous system. This causes bradycardia and peripheral vasoconstriction. Blood is removed from the limbs and all organs but the heart and the brain, creating a heart–brain circuit and allowing the mammal to conserve oxygen.

Cranial Nerve X is the Vagus Nerve....

Still sort of all comes back to atmospheric composition. Lower oxygen => body activates protective mechanisms to survive longer.

### MJ* — 2012-03-15T20:21:23+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-the-h-is-factor-x-post-your-guesses.42/post-951>

> **Birdy;908 wrote:** Do you know how long they can hold their breath? The water is freezing cold, how'd they do that?

"When you breath hold, you are not so much ‘running out of air’ as you are fighting powerful impulses to breath when you don’t really need the oxygen yet. Breath holding like Sulbin is doing is the active over-coming of automatic processes by conscious suppression; it’s ignoring pain and involuntary muscle actions because you know you don’t need to do what they’re screaming at you to do.

"Without these impulses, how long could you hold your breath? Many people may have heard that three to five minutes without oxygen will cause irreparable brain damage, so they might assume that about four to five minutes would be the maximum that a person could hold his or her breath. They would be wrong.

Breath holding is less dangerous to the brain then cardiac arrest, because during a breath hold, the brain is still connected to oxygen stores in the lungs and the body quickly starts making adjustments to stretch this oxygen store last as long as possible"

anthropology/2011/02/03/human-amphibious-model-living-in-and-on-the-water/

### MJ* — 2012-03-15T20:24:57+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-the-h-is-factor-x-post-your-guesses.42/post-956>

Also from that article "The remarkable human ability to adapt to free-diving arises, in part, from the body triggering specific nervous system responses, including a reaction to deprivation from oxygen that can be seen in walruses and other aquatic mammals. Megan Lane explains on the BBC website, quoting freediving instructor Emma Farrell, the author of One Breath, A Reflection on Freediving:

The mammalian dive reflex – seen in aquatic animals such as dolphins and otters, and in humans to a lesser extent – helps, says Farrell.

“It’s a series of automatic adjustments we make when submerged in cold water. It reduces the heart rate and metabolism to slow the rate you use oxygen.”

In fact, the human dive reflex seems to be triggered especially by apnea (the abrupt stop of oxygen intake) and by cold (see Speck and Bruce 1978). You can make use of the dive reflex, if you ever need to relax, by temporarily putting your face into cold water; your heart rate should drop, which can be a really godsend if you have to do something that’s ramping up your excitability.

The dive response is generally said to be composed of three changes:

bradycardia, or a slowing of the overall heart rate;

peripheral vasoconstriction, or the shutting of capillaries on the body’s extremities; and

shunting of blood into the torso, especially the chest, which helps to resist increased pressure.

In most people, the drop in heart rate from the dive reflex is not as great as in other mammals; while most humans decrease by about 10-25%, some mammals can drop to as little as 10% of their normal heart rate (Speck and Bruce 1978).

The human dive reflex, however, can increase with training"

### MJ* — 2012-03-15T20:46:58+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-the-h-is-factor-x-post-your-guesses.42/post-976>

Ok LOL maybe that last idea is too out there, not scientific enough for Dr. K's theories so how about this:

"without the vagus stimulation, the regulatory T cell population declined in the presence of inflammatory signals. Absence of Regulatory T Cells Can Explain Many Inflammatory Diseases

In many inflammatory diseases, e.g. celiac, Crohn’s disease, rosacea, there appears to be a deficiency of regulatory T cells. In the absence regulatory T cells, signals from vagus nerves will no longer produce anti-inflammatory suppression. In fact the same nerve signals may become inflammatory. This would explain why rosaceans will become inflamed by hot or cold stimulation that would normally lead to anti-inflammatory stimulation of regulatory T cells. Similarly, capsaicin, castor oil and menthol, which normally produce an anti-inflammatory response, produce inflammation in rosaceans." so if the dive reflex turns on the vegas nerve it could fight inflammation and save our T cells...Factor X is the Dive Reflex!

>
>
>
> Thoughts?

[http://www.fi.edu/learn/brain/fats.html](http://http://www.fi.edu/learn/brain/fats.html)

It's what they ate, a combination of Omega 3s and Omega 6 rich foods...

"The first piece of the puzzle involved discovering early human populations that demonstrated greater intelligence. They found evidence in the East African Rift Valley and on the southern Cape of South Africa. The second piece of the puzzle was the discovery that docosahexaenoic acid, (DHA), was a large contributor to brain growth. The third piece was the discovery that DHA was found in seafood. When scientists put all the pieces together they found that the early humans who lived near water sources and ate seafood experienced the big brain change!

Stone age women collecting shellfish could have easily provided themselves with a plentiful source of brain-specific nutrition, and their children would have naturally participated in exploitation of this extremely rich resource. There must have been enough omega-3 and omega-6 fatty acids available in their diet to provide many generations with fuel for fetal/infant development as well as childhood and adult needs for the cardiovascular system and the brain.

In contrast, the inland Australopithecines did not have access to omega 3 EFAs and got stuck at a brain capacity that was not much bigger than a chimpanzee for three million years."

### Souldanzer — 2012-03-16T00:28:15+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-the-h-is-factor-x-post-your-guesses.42/post-1107>

Here's one of the things that's different for us humans from other animals:

> Human brain's metabolic budget significantly different from apes. They point out that anthropoid primates use ~8% of resting metabolism for the brain, other mammals (excluding humans) use 3-4%, but humans use an impressive 25% of resting metabolism for the brain. This indicates that the human "energy budget" is substantially different from all other animals, even our closest primate relatives--the anthropoid apes.

So humans must have had an easier time attaining energy-rich, dense, nutritious food that required less energy for digesting and more energy left over for brain development.

Still can't figure out why this food source would not have been available to other animals...

### TheKid — 2012-03-16T13:01:07+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-the-h-is-factor-x-post-your-guesses.42/post-1361>

As I said on the blog, I think factor X is periodization in training. And if it isn't, then I think periodization is something that should possibly be added as part of the optimal process. Go read Tudor Bompa for more info. Swimmers and track athletes heavily rely on periodization. The idea is to allow for not just adaptation to training stimulus but to get a "super compensation" effect at the end of a season when you want peak performance and then build upon that from season to season and from year to year. I would bet that the "superhuman" results of CT fit very well with periodization the circadian method of eating. And if you add in the Tim Ferris "minimum effective dose" concept, I bet you would do certain types of training, at certain times of year, hitting very specific stimuli, which all would result in some VERY significant gains in how one "looks, feels and performs." (to quote Robb Wolf)

I've been thinking hard about this - it's driving me crazy not knowing! I think that Factor X needs to be something that differentiates us from the apes we evolved from because Dr. K keeps saying that it is something that allowed our epigenetic switches to turn on and make changes faster than evolution in conventional thinking allows - he keeps saying the reason there is no 'missing link' in the evolutionary record is because there isn't one - our epigenetic switches were turned on by something - Factor X - that allowed for a huge change from ape to human. I know one big difference is that our Brains became bigger at the expense of a shorter digestive system as the result of becoming meat eaters (see this post by Dr. Eades - [http://www.proteinpower.com/drmike/low-carb-library/are-we-meat-eaters-or-vegetarians-part-ii/](http://http://www.proteinpower.com/drmike/low-carb-library/are-we-meat-eaters-or-vegetarians-part-ii/)). ### Souldanzer — 2012-03-16T17:58:27+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-the-h-is-factor-x-post-your-guesses.42/post-1535>

> **Kirstin;1426 wrote:** I've been thinking hard about this - it's driving me crazy not knowing! I think that Factor X needs to be something that differentiates us from the apes we evolved from because Dr. K keeps saying that it is something that allowed our epigenetic switches to turn on and make changes faster than evolution in conventional thinking allows - he keeps saying the reason there is no 'missing link' in the evolutionary record is because there isn't one - our epigenetic switches were turned on by something - Factor X - that allowed for a huge change from ape to human. I know one big difference is that our Brains became bigger at the expense of a shorter digestive system as the result of becoming meat eaters (see this post by Dr. Eades - [http://www.proteinpower.com/drmike/low-carb-library/are-we-meat-eaters-or-vegetarians-part-ii/](http://http://www.proteinpower.com/drmike/low-carb-library/are-we-meat-eaters-or-vegetarians-part-ii/)). So, what goes along with this? Alternatively, what are some other big changes that separate us from the apes??! I see more research in my future

I'm still thinking it was a rapid change in atmospheric composition related to suddenly more oxygen being available in the air. It appears we already had the fast epigenetic speed control due to our diets to overcome the reproductive issue in the cold. And that was due to a diet heavily skewed towards o3/VLC. During ice ages, I _think there is less oxygen in the air b/c of how it gets bound into water/ice during cold temps. So even if it isn't Factor X, that change must have had a tremendous effect on live on earth...

I'm stuck with the issue of why this didn't happen to other animals that happened to be around. During ice ages, there really isn't much carbs around, I'm thinking, so they must have had a similar diet?? Which would have created similar epigenetic speed control... so the only explanation for this is that somehow the future humans had access to great amounts of seafood/fish, protein-rich, energy-dense, VLC foods. Which enabled them to shrink their digestive system and have more energy available for the brain... and somehow other animals couldn't do this.

I also think it is really interesting that in the recent 35,000 to 10,000 years (mostly in the last 10,000) there actually has been brain shrinkage_ observed in humans. Interesting that this coincides with the agricultural revolution... increased grain consumption, resulting in higher carb diets and lower o3 content. I think researchers are actually pretty sure that this is what is causing the brain to shrink... It also should be noted that humans overall shrunk, so the brain relative to body size is still the same ratio and we still lead the bunch in EQ but hey, that's enough reason for me personally to hit the o3/VLC, lol!!

For me, the question is right now, what could have enabled future humans to have this energy-rich brain growth-stimulating diet, and to persist throughout the atmospheric change (b/c we preserved the fast epigenetics and used them to adapt to warmth) while other animals either did not have that diet during the ice age or weren't able to sustain it after the climate change?

This is taking over my life!! I need to get some work done

### KiwiLauren — 2012-03-16T21:04:14+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-the-h-is-factor-x-post-your-guesses.42/post-1652>

> **Souldanzer;1650 wrote:**
>
> EDIT: technically, humans have a bi-phasic sleep pattern according to some info in Dr. K's blog we just don't do it

And the human sleep mechanism isn't fully mature until age 5. Prior to then (as anyone whose ever been around a baby knows), there are many much shorter sleep cycles occurring throughout the day. Not to mention taking about 12 weeks to establish synchrony with the day-night patterns of light outside the womb.

Additionally (and this is different for humans vs other apes and mammals) our infants are the most vulnerable, both physically and neurologically. Our brains are the least developed at birth yet with the most potential capacity (and as far as I know, only humans and great apes are known to possess mirror neuronal structures). But our vulnerable infants certainly make optimal survival different than for others. At least with chimps the babies can hold on!

### Souldanzer — 2012-03-17T03:16:28+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-the-h-is-factor-x-post-your-guesses.42/post-1821>

I've been doing some afternoon reading

Higher altitude increases longevity.

Here are some things that happen:

The body produces more red blood cells to carry oxygen,

The body produces more of a particular enzyme that facilitates the release of oxygen from hemoglobin to the body tissues.

The depth of respiration increases.

Pressure in pulmonary arteries is increased, "forcing" blood into portions of the lung which are normally not used during sea level breathing.

> The body naturally produces a hormone called erythropoetin (EPO) which stimulates the production of red blood cells which carry oxygen to the muscles. Up to a point, the more blood cells you have, the more oxygen you can deliver to your muscles. There are also a number of other changes that happen during acclimatisation which may help athletic performance, including an increase in the number of small blood vessels, an increase in buffering capacity (ability to manage the build up of waste acid) and changes in the microscopic structure and function of the muscles themselves.

All of this creates improved nutrient availability and makes for better performance when coming back down to sea level. This benefit is not lasting, so you have to go to altitude regularly for extended periods of time to create the effect.

Sleeping at decreased pressure or reduced oxygen is especially interesting since respiratory rate is reduced during sleep as well. Athletes are experimenting with sleeping in tents with decreased atmospheric pressure to improve performance. I wouldn't be surprised if Dr K sleeps in such a tent

So, my conclusion is that no matter what the h\*\*\* factor X is, altitude, decreased atmospheric pressure, hypoxia will be involved in our SuperHuman Training soon.

I'm sticking with my hypothesis of rapid atmospheric change and how that exponentially affected our biochemistry which was set to super fast epigenetic speed in addition to a sudden increase in performance/thinking due to more available oxygen. Now if I could just figure out why human brains took off and animals' didn't at the same speed.... could have to do with diet and sleep patterns. I'm guessing Factor X is more complex (A+B+C) than just one thing. I've got A = epigenetic speed control, B = change in atmospheric composition, and C = ? (diet and/or sleep possibly). LOL, at least it sounds good

I am spending waaaay too much time on this. But now I know that I live in the state with highest longevity (and OMG do I need that help!!) and I also know why, which is

### TheKid — 2012-03-17T14:16:40+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-the-h-is-factor-x-post-your-guesses.42/post-1931>

I can't believe my theory that Factor X is periodization in training isn't getting more love. What's up with that?

I can tell you that, as a former athlete, periodization produces some really amazing results if you do it correctly. This is from a sports perspective. I would be interested in the study of any health benefits periodization imparts.

The reason I brought it up in the first place is because animals are cyclical in their physical activity. Obviously, for those animals that hibernate, winter is a pretty lazy season. But once spring hits, those animals go through a range of changes in physical activity. For instance, the mating season (paritcularly for males) can be like hitting mutlitpe CrossFit WODs in just one day. Foraging or grazing is more low level aerobic workout. Hunting is, i suppose, more like sprinting.

And as an ex-athlete, one thing I never understood about people "trying to get in shape" was the constant effort being exerted by people. I mean, you see people ALWAYS on the treadmill, ALWAYS lifting, ALWAYS doing aerobics, etc. But as an athlete, I never trained one specific energy system for more than a month or two at a time. You do your aerobic base work, then move on to anaerobic threshold work, then on to VO2 Max, then anaerobic overload, then lactate peak, then rest.

Some of this progression has been reordered in recent years, but it still follows a cyclical approach. So even if Periodization isn't Factor X, I still think it's somethnig that should be looked at carefully in terms of living optimally.

Sex is a good possibility...hmmm maybe we can learn to activate this gene expression LoL, just be there when we need you:

Anglerfish: Let’s Me Be A Part of You. Literally.

The Prickly Deep Sea Anglerfish males becoming one with their female (Image Credit: David Paul/Mark Norman, Australian Conservation Foundation)

Anglerfish, a deep sea fish named for the spiny appendage on its head that it uses as bait to "fish" its prey, has an unusual mating habit. As it spends its time in the bottom of the ocean, finding a mate is a problem – but the species solved this evolutionary challenge beautifully.

At first, scientists were perplexed because they’ve never caught a male anglerfish. Also, all female anglerfish have a lump on their body that looks like a parasite. Only later did scientists discover that the lump is the remain of the male fish.

The tiny male anglerfish are born without any digestive system, so once they hatch, they have to find a female quickly. When a male finds a female, he quickly bites her body and releases an enzyme that digests his skin and her body to fuse the two in an eternal embrace. The male then wastes away, becoming nothing but a lump on the female anglerfish’s body!

When the female is ready to spawn, her "male appendage" is there, ready to release sperms to fertilize her egg.

1[. Vitamin D](http://http://www.news-medical.net/health/Systemic-Telomere-Length-and-Aging.aspx)

Evidence suggests that elevated levels of oxidative stress and inflammation further increase the telomere attrition rate.

Vitamin D may have an effect on peripheral blood leukocyte telomere length. ''Richards and coworkers'' examined whether vitamin D concentrations would slow the rate of shortening of leukocyte telomeres.

The authors stated that vitamin D is a potent inhibitor of the proinflammatory response and slows the turnover of leukocytes. Leukocyte telomere length (LTL) predicts the development of aging-related disease, and the length of these telomeres decreases with each cell division and with increased inflammation.

I was wondering if we are all overthinking this, and then it occurred to me that maybe \*thinking\* is Factor X. Thinking/pondering/choosing/rationalizing/experimenting.... this is something that is available to us all. Dr. K keeps saying that thoughts can change our DNA, which at first sounds like New Age woo-woo, right? But when you think about it (ha ha) before you ever DO anything, you (usually) think about it beforehand. And thinking can be good or bad.

We can rationalize bad decisions/behavior. We engage in sabotaging behaviors because we have the ability to lie to ourselves and say it doesn't matter, or we \*can't\* quit XYC or \*can't\* afford testing etc. We can daydream, in fact I have been doing a lot of daydreaming during CT, that's when I thought of this. So Factor X is, for better or worse, ideas, awareness of ideas in our head, that we can either channel toward positive thinking/action OR negative thinking/action. Does this even make sense LOL not as scientific as other ideas I've read here, but my silly $.02

I think it would be pretty awesome if it was sex, too. My DH is quite happy with this unexpected effect of CT!

They all sure do seem to know when it is time for the vet!

My dog loves the vet. She takes the leash in-between her teeth and walks me there. My dog can think

Vet = lots and lots of goodies.... and more goodies LOL

I've taught my dog crazy things. I say dinner and she gets her feeding towel out of the kitchen into the living room and throws it around until it's spread out for her raw meaty bone experience.

But the most astonished I am when she does things like take the leash to show me where we need to go... I say "where do you want to go?" and she grabs it and we switch roles. SO CUTE!!

Topping all of that, my dog can speak. I'm not kidding. One day she just started answering when we asked her something... it's not words, but it's her own personal language and I pretty much always know what she says. Crazy.

I know a lot of it is conditioning but the speaking?? She'll chime in when I'm chatting with a friend...

And she is very aware of herself... YOU have food but I (dog) don't... not okay, lol.

I'm not sure we really know what's going on in an animals mind. My dog has been my best friend ever...

but abstract thought... I don't know. I'm not sure if she "imagines" playing fetch when she brings me the chuck it... or if it's just the conditioning of something good happening when the chuck it comes out.

Me, Dog Person

### SimonM — 2012-03-17T23:35:26+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-the-h-is-factor-x-post-your-guesses.42/post-2137>

I suspect the jaw-dropping moment came when Jack explained to her his idea that diabetes (and I think he has said cancer as well) are not diseases.

> So it appears that dietary carbohydrates, which are only present in long light cycles in the summer in cold places, induce mammals to add PUFA’s to our cells to become fluid so we can function as we hibernate. Makes complete logical sense when viewed from an evolutionary stand point. I asked several mammalian vertebrate physiologist if this is how carbohydrates work for hibernating mammals and their answer was yes.
>
>
>
> This implied to me that maybe if this is how mammalian physiology was designed to work to begin with. After all they evolved in the polar environments on earth. This implied something even bigger to me……why would we need diabetes to survive. Then the answer occurred to me. **I called it Factor X.** I checked my facts and continued to connect more dots. Diabetes is required in mammals who are designed to work for optimal adaptation in cold environments.
>
>
>
> Maybe, just maybe, it has become thought of as a neolithic disease in humans because we we have simultaneously lost the ability to hibernate because we evolved the ability to control our environment completely? After all we know evolution is moving today faster than our genome can adapt Cordain and LaLonde have pointed out many times.
>
>
>
> Remember, we still have our mammalian paleolithic genes that control our use of dietary carbohydrates and the up-regulation of PUFA’s in our cell membranes in us today. These biochemical pathways remain in us. This is another well known fact in the paleosphere. What is not so obvious to most however, is that the rapidity of our brains evolution created the ultimate mismatch in this system. I believe some perceive this mismatch but no one has explained this as yet. So it then follows that when we create a biologic mismatch with our neolithic thoughts………we get something we have been socialized to believe is a disease. When after all it is not at all……it is an evolutionary novelty created by our own rapid evolution secondary to our brain amazing development in the same time period?
>
>
>
> This is how I view it from a 30,000 foot level today. The skeptics will immediately jump down my throat and point out that type one diabetics and use CW that says it is a genetic disease! I don’t and never have. I think Type 1 and 1.5 diabetes are decidely epigenetic phenomena of this mismatch that has been passed from every eutherian mammal to us today, and we remain unaware of this possibility either! Epigenetics has also speed up if evolution has. This is why humans have no ability to stop diabetes once it stops…….unless they get in a cold environment. And this is why we assume that these two conditions are diseases, rather than consequences of a “relative time frame shift” in evolutionary time, due to how fast our brains developed.

This is from [http://jackkruse.com/cold-thermogenesis-two/](http://http://jackkruse.com/cold-thermogenesis-two/)

### MJ* — 2012-03-18T03:51:14+0200

> **MJ\*;2227 wrote: From Simon From Jack ..."Why would we need diabetes to survive?" Then the answer occurred to me. I call it factor X...I checked my facts and continued to connect more dots. Diabetes is required in mammals who are designed to work for optimal adaptation in cold environments."...

**Could factor X be that the reason we needed diabetes to survive, was because we needed it to induce a state of suspended animation to reserve resources, as in a diabetic coma?

"Is it correct to refer to hibernation as a comatose condition? Is the animal in a coma? Is the hibernation state one of torpor, lethargy, stupor? These terms are frequently used by biologists in describing the hibernating condition. Coma is defined as an "abnormal deep stupor occurring in illness or as a result of it," such as alcoholic coma, apoplectic coma, uremic coma, _diabetic coma_, coma vigil, etc.** It would be interesting to know what a normal coma is. Stupor is defined as a "condition of unconsciousness, torpor, stupor. A state analogous to hypnotism, or the first stage of hypnotism." It is seen in African sleeping sickness, encyphalitis lethargica, hysteria and other pathological states. Torpor is "numbness, abnormal inactivity, dormancy, apathy." Torpid means "not acting vigorously, sluggish." Biologists use such terms as coma, comatose, lethargic, stupor, trance, etc., in describing hibernation as though there is something essentially pathological about it.

Read more: [http://chestofbooks.com/health/natural-cure/The-Hygienic-System-Fasting-and-Sun-Bathing/Hibernation.html#ixzz1pQcedMwf](http://http://chestofbooks.com/health/natural-cure/The-Hygienic-System-Fasting-and-Sun-Bathing/Hibernation.html#ixzz1pQcedMwf)

If you lapse into a diabetic coma, you're alive — but you can't awaken or respond purposefully to sights, sounds or other types of stimulation.

[http://www.cnn.com/HEALTH/library/diabetic-coma/DS00656.h](http://http://www.cnn.com/HEALTH/library/diabetic-coma/DS00656.h)

### Dextery — 2012-03-18T04:19:38+0200

I think FACTOR X in this pursuit of optimal health lies within each one of us. We must be totally and completely responsible for our own condition....whether that condition is sub par or optimal.

Most of us think we are operating sub par currently and we are desparately seeking optimal. And I think it is right in front of us. How many of you have read every one of Dr. Kruse's posts at least three times to fully absorb the essence? How many want Dr Kruse to hand you the magic bullet to solve their problems?

Are we approaching our health as if were are pursuing a PHd in our own health? The good teachers never give you all the answers to the test. They give you the basics and then you have to apply the basics to your real world. Thus he is laying out the roadmap much as a professor progresses through a semester. The foundation is layed as the semester unfolds to prepare for the next semester...leading you to writing your PHd thesis on your own health. Factor X is us. Then we take the info and pass it on.

We all have the capacity to follow Dr Kruse and he has said he will not let you down. Only you can let yourself down. And only you can let down your genes.

Do what Dr Kruse says to do...no matter how wacky or preposterous it may seem to the neolithic brain. Your ability to follow his instructions to the letter is your FACTOR X.

### MJ* — 2012-03-18T04:20:29+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-the-h-is-factor-x-post-your-guesses.42/post-2275>

Set point - the place your furnace turns on in cold! This is how you control obesity with cold therapy regulated by insulin, activate your set point by lowering the temperature!!!! FACTOR X is activating your set point!!!!!

[http://science.howstuffworks.com/environmental/life/zoology/all-about-animals/hibernation3.htm](http://http://science.howstuffworks.com/environmental/life/zoology/all-about-animals/hibernation3.htm)

"When a mammal enters hibernation, it becomes somewhat like a cold-blooded animal. Its body temperature will vary depending on the temperature around it. However, there is a minimum temperature, known as a set point. It's just like setting the temperature on your thermostat at home. When the mammal's body temperature reaches the set point, the metabolism kicks in and burns some fat reserves. This generates some energy, which is in turn used to heat things back up above the set point. Larger animals have a higher set point. If they let their temperature drop too low, it would require an enormous amount of energy to heat back up again."

### Souldanzer — 2012-03-19T05:47:31+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-the-h-is-factor-x-post-your-guesses.42/post-2621>

The reason we need diabetes (and cancer, and all neolithic disease) is b/c that is how evolution/epigenetics work. We are a species that has a primordial condition adapted to cold. We do well in the cold as it suits our genes. We became smarter and the earth became warmer and our epigenetics worked to adapt us to that change. We were able to survive as a species b/c of how fast our epigenetics were able to work b/c of o3/VLC. Any pre-human, human or other species that could not do this went extinct. There is few links to our ape-like ancestors b/c none of them ever made it. They died. Fast. Their epigenetics weren't fit enough.

Early humans with insulin-resistance in areas with warm summers were able to survive the winter. They were fitter. It's the squirrel that doesn't find enough to eat during the summer and still gets fat enough to survive the winter. Other humans survived b/c they were exposed to constant winter and cold conditions in the colder areas of the planet that being our primordial condition coupled with the proper diet available in those areas. If you transplant these humans into a warmer environment, they get all sorts of neolithic diseases fast and die quickly. Look at the Native Americans. They get huge rates of Type 2 Diabetes very fast if you take them out of their native environment. Evolution has not had a chance to create "die off" in this population. We are seeing it happening now. It's just that we have medical resources as well to "treat" them (keep them alive longer). But the ones that are not fit for adaptation will not reproduce and it will happen fast, with few links... to the "next" species that will evolve out of them.

Now is the time that we have reached the point of being able to control our environment to a huge degree. That is new for the human species and it is creating "die off". You can be smart, and follow what Dr K has found and your genes will survive. Evolution will favor your good thinking. And you can choose between being cold-adapted all year long, as that is our primordial condition, or, you can be cold-adapted in the winter and warm-adapted in the summer, as that die-off has already happened many, many years ago when the Ice Age was over. Your genes are fit to experience seasonal cycles or you would not be here. If you have neolithic disease ("die-off") you need to pretend it is winter, no matter what, b/c it is caused by endless summer. A third choice would be to stick with endless summer, but this is new to human species, and your chances to survive die-off are very. small. b/c our primordial condition is cold.

If you do all this, your epigenetics will be passed on to your children and so on, until the human species hits another major development and the process starts over again. There will be few links to ancestors during any of these big changes, as die off happens quickly, within a generation or two. We see this happening now in our children. They are sick

In many, many years, there might be two species that have humans as ancestors... one that is cold-adapted and one that is seasonally adapted. They will be smarter b/c their ancestors had good thinking. I doubt there will be a human species that is warm-adapted.

Same thing for cancer. Or any "neolithic" disease. It's all caused by mis-matches and those create die off. It's how you get rid of candida and parasites... you create an environment that they are not adapted to and it creates die off. There will be a lucky few that make it.

I _think_ diabetes is not a disease b/c it is adaptation and evolution in process.

um, just my thoughts that came to me a little while ago... they seemed to make sense so I thought I'd share them... no idea about Factor X but good for you that you made it reading to this point

Studies on the Pima Indians and diabetes are very revealing: it's not the change of environment, it's the change of diet. In the Pima's case from traditional to SAD. (It's environment in the sense that there is a change from active to more sedentary as well.)

Pretty much the same is true for the Australian Aborigines - in Cordain's new book he describes a study where they asked ten middle-aged, overweight, type 2 diabetic native Australians to revert to hunter-gatherers for seven weeks - all lost weight and in ALL of them the diabetes "effectively disappeared" as Cordain puts it. (Study by Dr Kerin O'Dea published 1984 in "Diabetes", quoted in "The Paleo Answer" by Loren Cordain (John Wiley, 2012).

To follow with Red's line of thought, I've been bouncing that one around. I've been giving my CT time thoughts to "what is different in current hominids"

Is our ability to group simply a benefit for hunting and survival - or does it also serve a biological purpose? As I woke up this morn in the "family" bed with my tribe all huddled together under the covers (windows open so room was in 50's natch) I wonder if our current child rearing dogma doesn't have a biological implication. It still drives me nuts that people insist on throwing their babies in another room to train them to be without their tribe. It's so obvious that our babies are meant to be taken care of. If they are the center of the family sleep - surely then they can relax a little with less stress knowing the predator won't get them? I think the "bond" with others and the need to do so are more than a psychological desire. I suspect it's biological too.

I have three "factor-x" lines of thought right now, and that "bond" with other humans is one.

### MamaGrok — 2012-03-19T22:54:39+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-the-h-is-factor-x-post-your-guesses.42/post-2942>

> **Lyndra;2886 wrote:** Not entirely relevant to this threat, but just had to share/vent. I read about a recent survey asking Americans about their opinion on evolution. Only 39 percent believe in it, 25 percent disbelieve, 36 percent don't have an opinion (duh!). The newspaper article did a further breakdown based on education and church attendance. In some groups only 25 percent believe in evolution. It will be a tough sell to convince those people about any health implication that involves evolutionary processes. Rather a sad state of affairs IMO... Don't get me started about a conversation I had with a relative yesterday who believes he doesn't have to worry about taking care of his body/health because he has faith that god will take care of him. But, he believes in taking modern medications. It was too much for my logic-ridden brain to handle.

Nah. Be careful not to draw too many conclusions here. It's actually somewhat surprising that the lowest groups you found had as many as 25% believing in evolution, if the number is only 39% in the general population. Do keep in mind that

\- Much within the label of "evolution" \*is\* a theory, up for debate. Most people don't know the distinction between micro and macro evolution. I personally know no Christians who dispute micro-evolution, and personally know none who believe in the 6000 year old Earth. This is across identifications from Catholic to Pentecostal. So if you just ask, "Do you believe in evolution?" someone unsure about macro-evolution (b/c there still is no direct evidence, despite many clues & much logical reason to believe in it) may say no, even if he's far from a flat-earther, and may have a lot in common with your own perception of what "evolution" means.

\- Most of the conversation you might have about evolution can be had with different terms and no disagreement. Talk about natural law and/or design and there is no dispute on religious grounds for the overwhelming majority of Christians (I can't speak to what other religions may believe on this). Dr. Kruse more and more frequently uses terms that appeal to all frameworks - evolution/design, nature/God. There is nothing in what he has uncovered that someone like me (a devout Catholic) would find objectionable.

\- There are illogical sheep among Christians and there are illogical sheep among atheists. The idea that God will take care of someone is beyond any stretch of logic or faith - clearly Christians die, and die early. This has nothing to do with faith, and everything to do with a certain individual being unable to think consistently even within the context of his own belief set.

### KiwiLauren — 2012-03-19T23:07:45+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-the-h-is-factor-x-post-your-guesses.42/post-2946>

> **Cavemam;2718 wrote:**
>
>
>
> Is our ability to group simply a benefit for hunting and survival - or does it also serve a biological purpose? As I woke up this morn in the "family" bed with my tribe all huddled together under the covers (windows open so room was in 50's natch) I wonder if our current child rearing dogma doesn't have a biological implication. It still drives me nuts that people insist on throwing their babies in another room to train them to be without their tribe. It's so obvious that our babies are meant to be taken care of. If they are the center of the family sleep - surely then they can relax a little with less stress knowing the predator won't get them? I think the "bond" with others and the need to do so are more than a psychological desire. I suspect it's biological too.

You don't have to suspect. It's absolute fact. There is a popular quote among attachment theorists: 'humans are like neurons, they both die in isolation.' 70 years of attachment theory research and 30 years of parallel neuroscience research is absolutely clear that the quality of our relationships has a direct, causal impact on our physical (and emotional/psychological) health. The HPA axis response is built in infancy through co-created regulatory states with the parent. It's all built relationally, from the moment of conception through to the first years of life. Of course, neuroplasticity occurs throughout the lifespan and it's what we're all using to harness our changes. But the foundation for optimal is from birth/conception and is decidedly relational!

### LisaAPB — 2012-03-20T04:41:35+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-the-h-is-factor-x-post-your-guesses.42/post-3109>

Control the diet, control the sleep, and controlled cold and oxygenation... that's my official guess. I keep going back to this paragraph from CT5. Oxygen behaves differently in cold... however, Russian doctors, yogi's, and world class swimmers have learned how to control blood oxygen levels through breathing training.

> In cold environments, oxygen is not released in the same fashion as it is normally in a warmed surgical environment. In capillary beds that feed cells for respiration, the oxygen is tightly bound and it is returned to the heart still oxygenated. This highly oxygenated blood was sensed as normal by our oxygen monitors giving us the false belief that everything was fine. Her blood was fully oxygenated but the cold provided us with a major issue we were unaware of. If a anesthetized patients core temp is too cold red blood cells do not release the oxygen at the right time or in the right place in a capillary bed to allow for respiration. It does not matter if your blood is well oxygenated at all. She basically suffocated because the cold starved her tissues of oxygen. The cold cause increases the binding affinity of oxygen. It also causes protein transformations of the Hemoglobin molecule. In fact, cold environments can have some rather shocking effects on human biochemistry, I learned after this case. One factor can change everything you think is true. I never forgot this lesson. If we had used slightly warmer blood, as we warmed up her core temperature she likely would have never died after this epic operative feat. We did all the crazy hard surgical things correctly but the physical and chemical effects of cold on the oxygen disassociation curve took our surgical victory to utter defeat in the matter of 5 minutes.

I finally looked at that link about the chickens and immediately it made me think of a documentary I watched "Dogs and more dogs" on The Passionate Eye a little over a month ago. Of course it's no longer available for viewing so it took a while to remember the explanation for why and how wolves had evolved into dogs... The answer apparently comes from the "Farm-fox Experiment" see [http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Farm-Fox\_Experiment](http://http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Farm-Fox_Experiment)

Here's a quote from it: "The result is that Russian scientists now have a number of domesticated foxes that are fundamentally different in temperament and behavior from their wild forebears. Some important changes in physiology and morphology are now visible, such as mottled or spotted colored fur. Many scientists believe that these changes related to selection for tameness are caused by lower adrenaline production** in the new breed, causing physiological changes in very few generations** and thus yielding genetic combinations not present in the original species. This indicates that selection for tameness (i.e. low flight distance) produces changes that are also influential on the emergence of other "dog-like" traits, such as raised tail and coming into heat every six months rather than annually." \[emphasis added\]

So, I'm asking... is it about lower adrenaline levels?

From "Thermoregulation in winter swimmers and physiological significance of human catecholamine thermogenesis"

"As evident from an increase in plasma noradrenaline concentrations, immersion of winter swimmers and control subjects in cold water activates the sympathetic nervous system considerably. There was no difference in noradrenaline production between controls and winter swimmers. In contrast, plasma adrenaline concentrations were unchanged after cold exposure (Fig. 1), in spite of a trend towards lowered adrenaline production at rest and during cooling.

Heart rates tended to increase in control subjects exposed to cold, but were significantly lower in winter swimmers (Fig. 2)."

The graph also showers winter swimmers have lower adrenaline levels than the control group, I just don't know if it's a significant amount. Sorry, only a few semesters of physiology helping me figure here.

### Lyndra — 2012-03-21T02:37:23+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-the-h-is-factor-x-post-your-guesses.42/post-3681>

> **Cavemam;3374 wrote:**
>
>
>
> "And basically what factor-x is, something that sped up hominid evolution. I think I know what that is."

I can think of two things that sped up our evolution - fire and food preservation.

Fire allowed our ancestors to stay warm during cold weather and let them cook food. Fire made them warm-adapted and diminished their cold adaptation. Cooking food made it (arguably) more palatable and digestible and broadened the foods previously eaten.

Food preservation, from low-tech (digging tubers in the fall and storing in a cool, dry place) to higher-tech (salting, drying). This allowed them to eat out of rhythm with nature - eating starch in the dead of winter, having meat available even when game was not around.

These two discoveries increased the biologic mismatch between our genes & our environment. Of course agriculture did far more and in a much shorter period of time.

Now, are they really _FACTORs_? I dunno about that. Just going off down a totally different path...

### Souldanzer — 2012-03-21T04:30:00+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-the-h-is-factor-x-post-your-guesses.42/post-3762>

Just sharing some more thoughts...

I'm wondering what factor _**Time was playing in the process of human evolution.

Time appears to be finite, either you have it or you don't, either you're young and have lots of time or you're old and don't have so much time. But then, time appears to speed up as we get older. Days go by faster and we experience us aging faster. Yet a year or a day has not changed in actual duration.

Considering evolution and epigenetics, Time can go either way... mis-matches will make you sick and unwell, and you will die faster, shortening the time you spent on this planet. It will do so exponentially, I suggest, as one mis-match will escalate another, shortening your time on earth exponentially. If one organ fails, the others are soon to fail after as the system crashes in itself.

On the other hand, look at what we are doing with the information Dr. K is giving us. We are speeding up epigenetics, that is actually our goal, in the opposite direction than we have been going, to turn on or off switches that are not serving our health. But this does not only make us healthier ... it also gives us more time on this planet, which means we can exponentially extend our life spans b/c we have more time to generate changes in epigenetics. And those changes will be passed on to our children, where the process continues... exponentially.

I guess Time is relative and not finite.

Maybe that's why we're _Chasing_** Change. B/c we've got to be faster than the mis-matches can kill us.

Maybe not Factor X but I get a point on one count: We all have it!!_

EDIT: There's a real good nugget on Time from Dr K somewhere in the abyss of the MDA Monster Thread. Might try to find it. I printed it but didn't save it on my laptop.

### MJ* — 2012-03-21T15:47:13+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-the-h-is-factor-x-post-your-guesses.42/post-3869>

> **Lyndra;3706 wrote:** I can think of two things that sped up our evolution - fire and food preservation.
>
>
>
> Fire allowed our ancestors to stay warm during cold weather and let them cook food. Fire made them warm-adapted and diminished their cold adaptation. Cooking food made it (arguably) more palatable and digestible and broadened the foods previously eaten.
>
>
>
> Food preservation, from low-tech (digging tubers in the fall and storing in a cool, dry place) to higher-tech (salting, drying). This allowed them to eat out of rhythm with nature - eating starch in the dead of winter, having meat available even when game was not around.
>
>
>
> These two discoveries increased the biologic mismatch between our genes & our environment. Of course agriculture did far more and in a much shorter period of time.
>
>
>
> Now, are they really _FACTORs_? I dunno about that. Just going off down a totally different path...

### RED — 2012-03-21T17:27:05+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-the-h-is-factor-x-post-your-guesses.42/post-3907>

I brought up parasites, bacteria, and viruses a couple of days ago and am surprised nobody responded to it at all. Even if it's not "Factor X" they have had more influence on human history than probably any other factor.

\-Our immune system developed along with the pathogens we evolved with. It must have been very active when we drank water from ponds, ate raw and rotting meat, plants with dirt, and didn't wash hands after... well anything. Now we have these active immune systems looking for something to fight, but there is not much there, so it attacks our food (food allergies) and our bodies (autoimmune disease).

\-We need healthy gut flora (bacteria).

\-There are theories that parasites can effect our behavior.

\-Pathogens have changed human genes. We are who we are today based on what we survived (and didn't survive) and adapted to.

If you spend some time thinking about this, it's huge. It's one thing for a group of humans to survive a climate change by adapting their food sources and survival skills, it's quite another for a few of them to survive a major epidemic, create antibodies or genetic change that is passed down in their DNA, forever protecting their descendants. Very cool.

### chocolate — 2012-03-22T02:40:35+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-the-h-is-factor-x-post-your-guesses.42/post-4168>

I keep thinking back to my dad being fathered by a man in his seventies and having webbing cut from between his fore and middle fingers to the first knuckle on both hands, it was perfectly symmetrical. that sounds like a sperm that swam for its life. I keep thinking about the importance of clearing when you dive and the sandy eyeballs that I had when I woke up after the storm. They wouldn't have hurt in the water, they might even have helped. And my kid loves cloudy overcast days. She says she can breathe the best and feels the best. I used to go to the ice rink to get my sinuses open. It has to do with long time cold water submersion.

I think long time submersion because I had no sense smell, and my acute sense of smell is a terrible curse. And I couldn't even feel the cold. That implies there was no value to the information. That was weird. The not being able to pee conserves heat, or reduce energy use. Or is hibernatory, like in sleep. They tell you to do that in scuba to extend your time. It doesn't matter how many gadgets you have in scuba either, if you can't clear, you can't submerge. And he talks about the freezing energy being exponential. And does anyone even know what the hell sinuses are for? And adenoids? I think I am the only one in the universe that still has them. My face used to get shut up and the doctor always said they were the right color. If the air was foul, we either went to sleep down there and didn't get eaten, or we breathed. I think we went to sleep because our babies don't seem really geared up for swimming and if you don't have your peeing function, I wouldn't think you'd be able to do any serious gestating. Also, the absence of gills, unless we dumped the salt water out our butts like those people that do the lemon cleanse.

### Clabbergirl — 2012-03-22T16:10:15+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-the-h-is-factor-x-post-your-guesses.42/post-4379>

> **MJ\*;4381 wrote: Interesting thought...I like it we are made up of mostly water, and the moon effects the tides...and this article discusses some links to the Circadian cycle
>
>
>
> [http://www.biology-online.org/articles/lunar\_cycle\_effects\_human/effects\_lunar\_cycle\_humans.html](http://http://www.biology-online.org/articles/lunar_cycle_effects_human/effects_lunar_cycle_humans.html)
>
>
>
> "Human and animal activities, physiological processes, and behavior are subject to alterations caused by circadian rhythms, lunar cycles, and seasonal changes. Circadian and seasonal rhythms are well described, but the effects of the lunar cycle on humans and animals have been much less explored."**

**

There's several connections to it. Here's [one](http://http://www.lorrieschoettler.com/2011/03/circadian-rhythms/):

"The natural rhythm of the human body over the course of 24-hour cycles is called the circadian rhythm. **This rhythm is not only regulated by lunar cycles, but also solar cycles, seasonal cycles, weather patterns, etc."**

And [this](http://http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/16407788):

"The lunar cycle also exerts effects on laboratory rats with regard to taste sensitivity and the ultrastructure of pineal gland cells. Cyclic variations related to the moon's phases in the magnitude of the humoral immune response of mice to polivinylpyrrolidone and sheep erythrocytes were also described. It is suggested that melatonin and endogenous steroids may mediate the described cyclic alterations of physiological processes. The release of neurohormones may be triggered by the electromagnetic radiation and/or the gravitational pull of the moon**."

I was thinking about this last night. I don't know if it's Factor X, but how can the moon not affect anything ancient within organisms on Earth?

### Souldanzer — 2012-03-22T19:49:31+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-the-h-is-factor-x-post-your-guesses.42/post-4555>

> **Jack Kruse;4113 wrote:** Huge......E=mc2 C2 in my formula is Factor X.
>
>
> E = mc2 where c is Factor X c = the constant of speed of light in a _vacuum
>
>
>
> _Density and temperature impact lights’ speed._
>
>
>
> c is defined as the speed of light only in a perfect vacuum.
>
>
>
>
>
> If light travels through something that has mass, such as air or glass, the velocity is reduced. The velocity of light in these materials is given as c/n, where n is called the refractive index of the material. A perfect vacuum has an index of refraction equal to unity, but almost all other materials have an index greater than one. Window glass has a refractive index of approximately n=1.5, so light travels with a velocity of about 2/3 c in glass. Air has an index of 1.0003, so the speed of light in air is nearly the same as in vacuum, but just a little slower. As a rule of thumb, materials which have higher density have larger refractive indices.
>
>
>
> If the density of a material changes with temperature, the speed of light in that material will also change._

_

Therefore, by changing the density of the body/cells_ one can indirectly affect the speed of light and with that energy.

Greater cell membrane fluidity increases signaling speed in cold.

Greater fluidity = less density = refractive index n gets smaller = speed of light in body gets closer to that of speed of light in vacuum (n closer to 1) = greater energy

Cold-adapted bodies have greater energy than warm-adapted (less membrane fluidity).

I.e. honey takes a while to flow out of the bottle. Apply heat, which reduces the honey's density, and it flows faster.

Humans flow faster when cold, lol. The left-over energy (as compared to warm-adapted human) can be applied to run faster, or lift more.

I am making the assumption that greater cell membrane fluidity equals less density. I have no idea if that is true and it appears to be beyond google's capabilities to find out  My guess is that it's possible b/c we already know that signaling speed is increased in cold.

Factor X would be the refractive index of a human, which can be influenced by various means (diet, warm or cold adaptation, and possibly some others).

It would explain why we need less calories in cold... I think, LOL. And how seafood is related to Factor X...

> **Lexi;4837 wrote: yeah MJ but HOW? what is that mechanism?**

**
LoL oh if only I knew I could end this Google fueled brain drain!!!  "The process of liver regeneration involves the triggering of active growth in the remaining liver cells, in cells of bile ductules, and in unspecialized cells called stem cells**, all of which are usually quiescent in the normal liver. Proliferation of these cells and their subsequent differentiation are key events in the process by which the missing liver mass is replaced and adequate hepatic function restored. In the musculoskeletal system, different populations of quiescent stem cells allow efficient replacement of damaged or partially removed bones and muscles."

> **MJ\*;4870 wrote: LoL oh if only I knew I could end this Google fueled brain drain!!!  "The process of liver regeneration involves the triggering of active growth in the remaining liver cells, in cells of bile ductules, and in unspecialized cells called stem cells**, all of which are usually quiescent in the normal liver. Proliferation of these cells and their subsequent differentiation are key events in the process by which the missing liver mass is replaced and adequate hepatic function restored. In the musculoskeletal system, different populations of quiescent stem cells allow efficient replacement of damaged or partially removed bones and muscles."

### MJ* — 2012-03-24T01:54:28+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-the-h-is-factor-x-post-your-guesses.42/post-5242>

It's something we can do huh?

This may confuse matters once more, maybe it's not about making oxygen more available, but using it more efficiently, check this out about the Woolly Mammoth [this:](http://http://news.discovery.com/animals/woolly-mammoth-blood-bacteria-cold.html)

"The mammoth hemoglobin cold adaptation mechanism is actually two mechanisms, as Campbell and his colleagues report in the May 2 edition of the journal Nature Genetics. Both adaptations have to do with making sure there is enough energy available to allow the hemoglobin to release oxygen. Under cold conditions, Campbell explained, hemoglobin's grip on oxygen is a lot tighter and there is less energy available to break it free and give it to cells.

"The hemoglobin can't release oxygen," Campbell said. "So these tissues would starve of oxygen and die."

The mammoth hemoglobin gets around this by lowering the amount of energy required to offload the oxygen. Specifically, it uses tiny amounts of heat given off by a couple of reactions that are also underway on the hemoglobin molecule. These other reactions relate to the binding of hemoglobin to other compounds like chloride and protons inside the red blood cells. The result is a far more energy efficient hemoglobin molecule."

What could we do to ourselves to make our hemoglobin more efficient and require less energy to operate? [Could this be it](http://http://steelcityscience.wordpress.com/2011/09/22/woolly-mammoth-blood-breathes-again/)?:

The job of real hemoglobin is to transport oxygen to where it is needed in your body. It can do this because it binds tightly to oxygen in the oxygen-rich blood of your lungs and doesn’t let go of it until it reaches the oxygen-poor blood in the other parts of your body.

That ‘letting go’ process is crucial, since the oxygen has to be released from hemoglobin to be used to produce energy. **This process is even more efficient when you are exercising hard**, because all the heat that builds up in those pumping muscles favors the deoxygenation of hemoglobin."

I know we said it before but let's say it again, but in a different way heavy exercise in this case weight lifting to release the energy needed to not freeze to death in the cold...

### Shijin13 — 2012-03-24T03:43:56+0200
