### KiwiLauren — 2012-03-27T01:53:14+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-the-h-is-factor-x-post-your-guesses.42/post-6671>

There are some interesting/critical things about water to consider....

\- 70% of the human body is water (but it's about 80%+ at birth (96% at conception) and declines as we age.... once the body drops below 50% water life ends)

\- about 70% of the planet is covered in water (and 98% of that is oceans)

\- water accepts the vibrations of everything around it

\- resonance occurs when two things vibrate at same energy level

\- normally density of substances is higher in their solid state, but with water it is not... ice is less dense than water (which is why the ice covers the top of the oceans, not the bottom of them... which is what allowed the creation of life)

\- the density of water is highest at 4 degrees Celsius (so even the bottom of the ocean is maintained at 4 degrees even in the coldest weather)

\- the surface tension of water is very strong (it produces a capillary phenomenon). This means that water can go up to the tops of tall tress against the force of gravity. Because of water, in the form of blood, the necessary nutrients can be distributed all over the body.

\- in 1990 Dr Louis Frank (who works with NASA, the National Academy of Sciences, etc) put forward a theory that humans were born from the storms of small comets poured on earth. The comets which arrive at a rate of about 20 per minute into the earth's atmosphere) arrived on te earth over 4 billion years ago and the necessary material for life were brought with them; they formed the rivers, lakes and oceans. (NASA officially announced this theory in 1997 and the Institute for Astronomy at University of Hawaii endorsed in, too.)

"Researchers also examined whether the epigenetic differences were hereditary. The answer was yes; the chickens inherited both methylation and gene activity from their parentages. After eight generations of cross breeding the two types of chickens, the differences were still evident.

The results suggest that domestication has led to epigenetic changes. For more than 70 % of the genes, domesticated chickens retained a higher degree of methylation. Since methylation is a much faster process than random mutations, and may occur as a result of stress and other experiences, this may explain how variation within a species can increase so dramatically in just a short time."

From thyme:

I know simple breath work was shot down earlier, but the mamalian diving reflex--which dr k said to start CT with, via the face dunks, but didn't talk much about--does a lot of fascinating things to the human body...it is a throwback from not just our neolithic brains, but pre-paleo, too--pre-human. It's nominally to guard against hypoxia, but I don't think that's it--it's a state of enduring hypoxia that radically resets lots of cellular networks in the body. There are some blooming idiot humans who use the diving reflex to swim more than 100M down (and sometimes get so swept into the competition that they DIE. Don't go messing with this until you know what you're doing.) It slows the heart rate, restricts blood flow, and actually lets blood into your lungs' capillaries..freedivers use the last to not be killed by the pressure differential at depths.

Polar bears & penguins breathe air; polar bears swim underwater for 6-7 minutes; penguins for 30 minutes or so. Both use a varient of the mamalian diving reflex (I know, penguins aren't mammals--this stuff is \_old\_, evolutionarily speaking, though penguins might have gotten it on their own..)

### MJ* — 2012-03-27T06:53:32+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-the-h-is-factor-x-post-your-guesses.42/post-6850>

The id is the unorganized part of the personality structure which contains the basic drives. The id acts according to the "pleasure principle", seeking to avoid pain or displeasure aroused by increases in instinctual tension.\[2\]

The id is unconscious by definition:

"It is the dark, inaccessible part of our personality, what little we know of it we have learned from our study of the Dreamwork and of the construction of neurotic symptoms, and most of that is of a negative character and can be described only as a contrast to the ego. We approach the id with analogies: we call it a chaos, a cauldron full of seething excitations... It is filled with energy reaching it from the instincts, but it has no organization, produces no collective will, but only a striving to bring about the satisfaction of the instinctual needs subject to the observance of the pleasure principle."\[3\]

In the id,

**"contrary impulses exist side by side, without cancelling each other out..**..There is nothing in the id that could be compared with negation...nothing in the id which corresponds to the idea of time."\[4\]

Developmentally, the id precedes the ego; i.e. the psychic apparatus begins, at birth, as an undifferentiated id, part of which then develops into a structured ego. Thus, the id:

"...contains everything that is inherited, that is present at birth, is laid down in the constitution â€” above all, therefore, the instincts, which originate from the somatic organization, and which find a first psychical expression here (in the id) in forms unknown to us." \[5\]

The mind of a newborn child is regarded as completely "id-ridden", in the sense that it is a mass of instinctive drives and impulses, and needs immediate satisfaction, a view which equates a newborn child with an id-ridden individualâ€”often humorouslyâ€”with this analogy: an alimentary tract with no sense of responsibility at either end, paraphrasing a quip made by former U.S. President Ronald Reagan during his 1965 campaign for Governor of California in which he compared government to a baby \[6\].

The id is responsible for our basic drives, "knows no judgements of value: no good and evil, no morality...Instinctual cathexes seeking discharge â€” that, in our view, is all there is in the id."\[7\] It is regarded as "the great reservoir of libido",\[8\] the instinctive drive to create â€” the life instincts that are crucial to pleasurable survival. Alongside the life instincts came the death instincts â€” the death drive which Freud articulated relatively late in his career in "the hypothesis of a death instinct, the task of which is to lead organic life back into the inanimate state."\[9\] For Freud, "the death instinct would thus seem to express itself â€” though probably only in part â€” as an instinct of destruction directed against the external world and other organisms."\[10\]: through aggression. Freud considered that "the id, the whole person...originally includes all the instinctual impulses...the destructive instinct as well."\[11\] as Eros or the life instincts.

from wiki: The Emperor Penguin is able to thermoregulate (maintain its core body temperature) without altering its metabolism, over a wide range of temperatures. Known as the thermoneutral range, this extends from -10 to 20 Â°C (14 to 68 Â°F). Below this temperature range, its metabolic rate increases significantly, although an individual can maintain its core temperature from 38.0 Â°C (100.4 Â°F) down to −47 Â°C (−53 Â°F).\[27\] Movement by swimming, walking, and shivering are three mechanisms for increasing metabolism; a fourth process involves an increase in the breakdown of fats by enzymes, which is induced by the hormone glucagon.\[28\] At temperatures above 20 Â°C (68 Â°F), an Emperor Penguin may become agitated as its body temperature and metabolic rate rise to increase heat loss. Raising its wings and exposing the undersides increases the exposure of its body surface to the air by 16%, facilitating further heat loss.\[29\]

### Drew — 2012-03-27T10:08:04+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-the-h-is-factor-x-post-your-guesses.42/post-6888>

This was an awesome post MJ (right or not) and makes perfect sense. What we're trying to do (whether it's factor x or not) is eliminate mismatches, therefore matching our environment to our cellular biology. This selects for a variety of extremely beneficial things.

> **MJ\*;6549 wrote: There are many times (one was my list a few days ago) where Dr. Kruse has said that something was not Factor X, and today like with Chocolate and the telomeres, **but that is was selected for by Factor X...so the obvious question should be what in nature is even able to select or is responsible for selection?
>
> **
>
>
>
> This is probably an incomplete thought but here's my start...I'll be away from all this for awhile so someone have a go at it:
>
>
>
>
>
> "[Natural selection](http://http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Natural_selection) acts on the phenotype, or the observable characteristics of an organism, but the genetic (heritable) basis of any phenotype that gives a reproductive advantage will become more common in a population (see allele frequency). Over time, this process can result in populations that specialize for particular ecological niches and may eventually result in the emergence of new species. In other words, natural selection is an important process (though not the only process) by which evolution takes place within a population of organisms. As opposed to artificial selection, in which humans favour specific traits, in natural selection the environment acts as a sieve through which only certain variations can pass."** - that's nature's law.
>
>
>
>
>
> I feel that Factor X is in our biology. It is the conditions the cells find themselves in that creates an environment that matches our ancient pathway that allows for it to be expressed. With our cells reset back to their intended natural state (through Hypoxia) allowed for by environmental conditions of cold, low light and calorie restriction, the benefits of Factor X can be seen. Factor X is nature's rule that when we match our cells with their preferred condition the cells behave optimally. The benefits of Factor X are all the cool things it has selected for like longer telomers.
>
>
>
> instincts too, can't forget those...
>
> If it's not this then we need to dig deeper into the role selection plays, or in factors of selection.

Known as the thermoneutral range, this extends from -10 to 20 Â°C (14 to 68 Â°F). Below this temperature range, its metabolic rate increases significantly, although an individual can maintain its core temperature from 38.0 Â°C (100.4 Â°F) down to −47 Â°C (−53 Â°F).\[27\] Movement by swimming, walking, and shivering are three mechanisms for increasing metabolism; a fourth process involves an increase in the breakdown of fats by enzymes, which is induced by the hormone glucagon.\[28\] At temperatures above 20 Â°C (68 Â°F), an Emperor Penguin may become agitated as its body temperature and metabolic rate rise to increase heat loss. Raising its wings and exposing the undersides increases the exposure of its body surface to the air by 16%, facilitating further heat loss.\[29\]

aha.... From: [http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Suspended\_animation](http://http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Suspended_animation)

Human hibernationThere are many research projects currently investigating how to achieve "induced hibernation" in humans.\[9\]\[10\] This ability to hibernate humans would be useful for a number of reasons, such as saving the lives of seriously ill or injured people by temporarily putting them in a state of hibernation until treatment can be given.

There are cases of accidental human hibernation. The most recent is the case of Mitsutaka Uchikoshi, a Japanese man who survived the cold for 24 days in 2006 without food or water when he fell into a hypothermic state similar to hibernation.\[11\]

**

No kidding, Cavemam! Dr. K does mention nitric oxide quite a bit [http://jackkruse.com/cold-thermogenesis-6-the-ancient-pathway/](http://http://jackkruse.com/cold-thermogenesis-6-the-ancient-pathway/), so I am very curious to see us regular folks can induce hibernation. I'll have to see if he mentions hydrogen sulfide.... no, didn't find it.

But found this: [http://thyroidbook.com/blog/nitric-oxide-modulation-for-autoimmune-disease/](http://http://thyroidbook.com/blog/nitric-oxide-modulation-for-autoimmune-disease/)

_Researchers have found that when an autoimmune reaction creates a surge in TH-17 activity, it stimulates an enzyme called inducible nitric oxide synthase (iNOS) to produce more inducible nitric oxide._

So -- How to boost the good nitric oxide and dampen the bad**So we know endothelial nitric oxide helps tame the autoimmune response and aids in tissue recovery. We know we have a “bad” nitric oxide, inducible nitric oxide, which is over active during TH-17-stimulated autoimmune attacks. We also know that taking arginine is not desirable, because although it might boost endothelial and neuronal nitric oxide, it may also boost the destructive inducible nitric oxide. These are the facts I considered as a practitioner when I began exploring these mechanisms and how to further help my autoimmune patients.

The clinical goal is to modulate the TH-1, TH-2, TH-3, and TH-17 systems (by modulate, I mean influence them to behave appropriately), increase endothelial nitric oxide, and dampen the inducible nitric oxide response.

Eventually, after my ongoing review of the research and clinical experience, it became apparent glutathione pathway integrity and nitric oxide modulation (boosting the good and dampening the harmful) offered profound responses.

### jenn — 2012-03-27T17:45:46+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-the-h-is-factor-x-post-your-guesses.42/post-7036>

Is the mismatch that Dr. Kruse talks about in our brain itself? Specifically between the newer outer cortex (the part that allows us to think, plan, and create, and interprets the world for us) and the older, reptilian brain that deals with survival? Many of the items on the list he gave us from his final chapter involve a mismatch between reality and what our brain perceives to be reality (as in pain occurring when there is no injury according to an MRI). Also, it seems that we are the only species that can choose (neolithic brain) to do things contrary to our survival instincts (paleo-brain). He did say we were the most mismatched species on the planet. Is the mismatch in our biology (brain) itself? Dr. K is a neurosurgeon, so he has brains on his mind (and in his hands) every day!

So would that make Factor X the development of our neolithic (thinking) brain, or some event that made such a development advantageous for our species' survival?

And yes, I do realize the irony of using a neolithic brain to try to figure this out!!!

### N~Doro — 2012-03-27T18:32:05+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-the-h-is-factor-x-post-your-guesses.42/post-7074>

> **Owl;7096 wrote:** Let's all remember:
>
> It's commonly known by 2 letters.
>
> It's something we all learned in school, and it's in every science museum.
>

Bounce this idea around inspired by the "mitochondrial differences of birds" part of the last paragraph of the book.

[http://www.nature.com/hdy/journal/v99/n4/full/6801034a.html](http://http://www.nature.com/hdy/journal/v99/n4/full/6801034a.html)

The evolutionary history of the human Y chromosome has been reconstructed (Skaletsky et al., 2003; Ross et al., 2005), and provides clear evidence of genetic degeneration. This uncomfortable conclusion for human males has even stimulated popular scientific accounts (Sykes, 2004). The Y chromosome's degeneration seems to be explained by its non-recombining nature. Absence of recombination in DNA has several consequences, including inefficient natural selection and low diversityâ€”the so-called Hillâ€“Robertson effects (see Charlesworth and Charlesworth, 2000 for review). These arise because an advantageous new mutation is unable to escape a poor haplotype (if that is where it first occurs), and because a disadvantageous new mutation can permanently blight the haplotype on which it arises.

Birds' sex chromosomes make a particularly interesting comparison with the XY system in humans and many other animals because, in birds, the females are heterogametic, rather than the males. In other words, the females have two distinct sex chromosomes called Z and W, whereas males are ZZ (and not XY). The avian Z and W are shared by all birds, indicating that they became established just before the bird radiation.​

Last summer I read Power, Sex, Suicide: Mitochondria and the Meaning of Life by Nick Lane.

I am wondering how the development of _two_ different sexes ie XY chromosomes plays into this

**

later in the same cite:

But then comes the question of why there should be these recurrent selective sweeps. Recurrent episodes of selection could be due to parasites that are maternally transmitted such as Wolbachia found in arthropods and nematods (Hurst and Jiggins, 2005). This kind of maternally transmitted parasites may not be present in all animals; so there must be other factors. A change in breeding system could be such a factor. Paland and Lynch (2006) have recently reported that in Daphnia, transition from sexuality to asexuality leads to accelerated evolution in mitochondrial genome**, which suggests that the lack of sex and recombination in the nuclear genome has secondary effects on the mitochondrial genome. Berlin et al.'s results suggest that W chromosome could also be a contributing factor to obscuring the correlation between mtDNA polymorphism and Ne. Birds do have a larger population size than mammals and mtDNA polymorphism is lower in birds than in mammals. This supports the general conclusions of Hurst and Jiggins (2005) and Bazin et al. (2006) that inferring Ne from mitochondrial data may be a risky business.

I also remember from the book that birds live proportionally longer than other species due to less "leaky" mitochondria.

### Lexi — 2012-03-27T18:44:16+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-the-h-is-factor-x-post-your-guesses.42/post-7085>

> **Jack Kruse;6851 wrote: to make it even more mind numbing FACTOR X goes only by two letters in many places............two letters in our alphabet.**

**

Factor X: 2 letters: to do w/ a body part: AB

as in **abdominals? something to do with the vagus nerve and how it runs from the brain to the stomach and intestines.

from wiki:

The vagus nerve ( /ˈveɪɡəs/ US dict: vā′Â·gəs), also called pneumogastric nerve or cranial nerve X, is the tenth of twelve (excluding CN0) paired cranial nerves. Upon leaving the medulla between the olivary nucleus and the inferior cerebellar peduncle, it extends through the jugular foramen, then passing into the carotid sheath between the internal carotid artery and the internal jugular vein down below the head, to the neck, chest and abdomen, where it contributes to the innervation of the viscera. Besides output to the various organs in the body, the vagus nerve conveys sensory information about the state of the body's organs to the central nervous system. 80-90% of the nerve fibers in the vagus nerve are afferent (sensory) nerves communicating the state of the viscera to the brain.\[1\]**

The medieval Latin word vagus means literally "wandering" (the words vagrant, vagabond, and vague come from the same root). Sometimes the branches are spoken of in the plural and are thus called vagi (/ˈveɪdʒaɪ/, US dict: vā′Â·jī). The vagus is also called the pneumogastric nerve since it innervates both the lungs and the stomach.**

so, submersion in cold, from head to toe, but especially the ABs, stimulates the nervous system to activate the ancient healing pathways.... for some of us they have become dormant, or lethargic, and our nervous system is waiting for some signals or messages, but there is only silence, or mixed messages. our SAD has been giving it the message of the middle finger apparently, so it is responding in kind.

> from wiki:
>
> The vagus nerve ( /ˈveɪɡəs/ US dict: vā′Â·gəs), also called pneumogastric nerve or cranial nerve X, is the tenth of twelve (excluding CN0) paired cranial nerves. Upon leaving the medulla between the olivary nucleus and the inferior cerebellar peduncle, it extends through the jugular foramen, then passing into the carotid sheath between the internal carotid artery and the internal jugular vein down below the head, to the neck, chest and abdomen, where it contributes to the innervation of the viscera. Besides output to the various organs in the body, the vagus nerve conveys sensory information about the state of the body's organs to the central nervous system. 80-90% of the nerve fibers in the vagus nerve are afferent (sensory) nerves communicating the state of the viscera to the brain.\[1\]**
>
> The medieval Latin word vagus means literally "wandering" (the words vagrant, vagabond, and vague come from the same root). Sometimes the branches are spoken of in the plural and are thus called vagi (/ˈveɪdʒaɪ/, US dict: vā′Â·jī). The vagus is also called the pneumogastric nerve since it innervates both the lungs and the stomach.**
>
>
>
> so, submersion in cold, from head to toe, but especially the ABs, stimulates the nervous system to activate the ancient healing pathways.... for some of us they have become dormant, or lethargic, and our nervous system is waiting for some signals or messages, but there is only silence, or mixed messages. our SAD has been giving it the message of the middle finger apparently, so it is responding in kind.

### Continuum Fitness — 2012-03-27T18:56:27+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-the-h-is-factor-x-post-your-guesses.42/post-7107>

Here is two more letters....CO

[http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Carbon\_monoxide](http://http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Carbon_monoxide)

In biology, carbon monoxide is naturally produced by the action of [heme oxygenase](http://http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Heme_oxygenase) 1 and 2 on the [heme](http://http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Heme) from [hemoglobin](http://http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hemoglobin) breakdown. This process produces a certain amount of [carboxyhemoglobin](http://http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Carboxyhemoglobin) in normal persons, even if they do not breathe any carbon monoxide. Following the first report that carbon monoxide is a normal neurotransmitter in 1993,\[sup\][\[4\]](http://http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Carbon_monoxide#cite_note-nytimes.com-3) as well as one of three gases that naturally modulate inflammatory responses in the body (the other two being [nitric oxide](http://http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Nitric_oxide) and [hydrogen sulfide](http://http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hydrogen_sulfide)), carbon monoxide has received a great deal of clinical attention as a biological regulator. In many tissues, all three gases are known to act as [anti-inflammatories](http://http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Anti-inflammatories), [vasodilators](http://http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Vasodilators), and promoters of [neovascular](http://http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Neovascular) growth.[\[5\]](http://http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Carbon_monoxide#cite_note-ReferenceA-4)\[/sup\] Clinical trials of small amounts of carbon monoxide as a drug are on-going.

### MJ* — 2012-03-27T19:08:48+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-the-h-is-factor-x-post-your-guesses.42/post-7130>

atmospheric composition + speed up epigenetics = change in DNA

How is it that the largest flying animals of the cretaceous were able to attain so much greater size than modern birds? There are severe physical limits associated with flight. It is difficult for large birds to generate enough lift to take off. Consider the well-known square-cube law: if you double the size of a bird by simple scaling, its wing area and associated lift go up by 22, or a factor of 4, while the body weight that must be lifted goes up by a factor of 23, or a factor of 8. When an evolving flying animal species increases in size the basic design must be altered to accommodate the reduced lift-to-payload ratio. But if anything, the pterosaurs were less well designed than modern birds. They lacked the birds' efficient keelbone muscle structure and the aerodynamic advantages of feathers. How, then, could pterosaurs have grown so large?

A missing piece of this puzzle may have been discovered. There are indications that the cretaceous atmosphere may have been much richer in oxygen. Today, Earth's atmosphere contains about 77% nitrogen, 21% oxygen, 1% water vapor, 0.9% argon, and 0.03% carbon dioxide, with traces of about a dozen other gases. It's been commonly assumed that earth-air stabilized at about this composition a few million years after life evolved on our planet when the early plants photosynthesized most of the primordial carbon dioxide into free oxygen, and the early bacteria converted most of the primordial ammonia to free nitrogen and water.

But now there is evidence that the cretaceous atmosphere may have been very different.

### MJ* — 2012-03-27T19:38:52+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-the-h-is-factor-x-post-your-guesses.42/post-7167>

Just want to visit this abstract one more time...look at the bold part

[http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/18754702](http://http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/18754702)

Abstract

Hydrogen sulfide (H2S) is recognized as a neuromodulator as well as neuroprotectant in the brain. H2S can be produced from cysteine by enzymes such as cystathionine beta-synthase. However, a mechanism for releasing H2S under physiologic conditions has not been identified. Here we show that H2S is released from bound sulfur, an intracellular store of sulfur, in neurons and astrocytes of mice and rats in the presence of physiologic concentrations of endogenous reducing substances glutathione and cysteine. The highest pH to release H2S from another sulfur store, acid-labile sulfur, which is localized mainly in mitochondria, is 5.4. Because mitochondria are not in the acidic condition, acid-labile sulfur may not be a physiologic source of H2S. Free H2S is immediately absorbed and stored as bound sulfur. Our novel method, using silver particles to measure free H2S, shows that free H2S is maintained at a low level in basal conditions. A**lkalinization of the cytoplasm is required for effective release of H2S from bound sulfur, and this condition is achieved in astrocytes by the high concentrations of extracellular K+ that are normally present when nearby neurons are excited.** These data present a new perspective on the regulation of H2S in the brain.

How do we excite our neurons??? Nothing dirty folks...wait...then again Dr. K says sex is very good for us!!!

> **chocolate;7206 wrote: [http://www.google.com/url?sa=t&rct=j&q=&esrc=s&source=web&cd=10&ved=0CHgQFjAJ&url=http%3A%2F%2Fwww.freelancetraveller.com%2Ffeatures%2Fscience%2Fxbio101%2Fxbio2.html&ei=F-xxT9XOCsWwiQKpt\_SkDA&usg=AFQjCNHPDgw80m-tlyeSpWeoZ7VpKOnqRA&sig2=MSstMrAO6C448y7bE4eOVA](http://http://www.google.com/url?sa=t&rct=j&q=&esrc=s&source=web&cd=10&ved=0CHgQFjAJ&url=http%3A%2F%2Fwww.freelancetraveller.com%2Ffeatures%2Fscience%2Fxbio101%2Fxbio2.html&ei=F-xxT9XOCsWwiQKpt_SkDA&usg=AFQjCNHPDgw80m-tlyeSpWeoZ7VpKOnqRA&sig2=MSstMrAO6C448y7bE4eOVA)

>

> I was focusing on the potassium.....hm....not the excited cells
>
>
>
> this talks about a North Star type body and the evolutions of breathing.**

**

"Metabolism : What does it mean to be an 'oxygen breather', anyway?

The sum total of the chemical reactions taking place in an organism is termed metabolism.

Reactions are of two broad types: catabolic, where energy is liberated in a series of small usable steps via the breakdown of complex molecules, and anabolic - the synthesis of complex molecules from simpler substrates, which costs energy.

In Earthly life, there are two important chemical reactions that illustrate both processes:

The (catabolic) aerobic breakdown of glucose (C6H12O6 + 6O2 -> 6CO2 + 6H2O + 3085kJ/mol glucose - 50% of the energy released is stored in the high energy phosphate bonds of 38 molecules of adenosine triphosphate, ATP ; the remainder is 'wasted' heat), and the reverse anabolic reaction, photosynthesis - this allowed the replenishment of organic molecules from atmospheric gases.

Photosynthesis is the reason why worlds have oxygen in their atmospheres. Oxygen is difficult to liberate from rocks, etc. without extensive violent geological activity.

Early in life's history, it is presumed that energy requirements were met by the anaerobic degradation of glucose (glycolysis) as it forms the "backbone" of most contemporary organisms' metabolic pathways: glucose -> lactic acid + 2 ATP

The capability to use oxygen came later when it became more abundant. Aerobic respiration \[the citric acid or Krebs cycle and the electron

transport chain\] is based entirely within mitochondria, which are symbiotic bacteria which merged with our distant ancestors early in the story of life.**

Chloroplasts, the organelles of photosynthesis, also appear to be endosymbionts."

### Adriana — 2012-03-27T19:45:07+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-the-h-is-factor-x-post-your-guesses.42/post-7179>

> **MJ\*;7205 wrote: Just want to visit this abstract one more time...look at the bold part
>
>
>
> [http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/18754702](http://http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/18754702)
>
> Abstract
>
>
>
> Hydrogen sulfide (H2S) is recognized as a neuromodulator as well as neuroprotectant in the brain. H2S can be produced from cysteine by enzymes such as cystathionine beta-synthase. However, a mechanism for releasing H2S under physiologic conditions has not been identified. Here we show that H2S is released from bound sulfur, an intracellular store of sulfur, in neurons and astrocytes of mice and rats in the presence of physiologic concentrations of endogenous reducing substances glutathione and cysteine. The highest pH to release H2S from another sulfur store, acid-labile sulfur, which is localized mainly in mitochondria, is 5.4. Because mitochondria are not in the acidic condition, acid-labile sulfur may not be a physiologic source of H2S. Free H2S is immediately absorbed and stored as bound sulfur. Our novel method, using silver particles to measure free H2S, shows that free H2S is maintained at a low level in basal conditions. Alkalinization of the cytoplasm is required for effective release of H2S from bound sulfur, and this condition is achieved in astrocytes by the high concentrations of extracellular K+ that are normally present when nearby neurons are excited.** These data present a new perspective on the regulation of H2S in the brain.
>
>
>
> How do we excite our neurons??? Nothing dirty folks...wait...then again Dr. K says sex is very good for us!!!

### N~Doro — 2012-03-27T19:51:55+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-the-h-is-factor-x-post-your-guesses.42/post-7192>

> **MJ\*;7223 wrote: Oh, yes that's what we are trying to solve for.....**

**

Hyperventilation? Something we can do at home.

In normal breathing, both the depth and frequency of breaths are varied by the neural (or, nervous) system, primarily in order to maintain normal amounts of carbon dioxide but also to supply appropriate levels of oxygen to the body's tissues. This is mainly achieved by measuring the carbon dioxide content of the blood; normally, a high carbon dioxide concentration signals a low oxygen concentration, as we breathe in oxygen and breathe out carbon dioxide at the same time, and the body's cells use oxygen to burn fuel molecules, making carbon dioxide as a by-product. Normal minute ventilation is generally 5-8 liters of air per minute at rest for a 70-kg man.

If carbon dioxide levels are high, the body assumes that oxygen levels are low, and accordingly, the brain's blood vessels dilate to assure sufficient blood flow and supply of oxygen. Conversely, low carbon dioxide levels cause the brain's blood vessels to constrict, resulting in reduced blood flow to the brain and lightheadedness.

The gases in the alveoli of the lungs are nearly in equilibrium with the gases in the blood. Normally, less than 10% of the gas in the alveoli is replaced with each breath taken. Deeper or quicker breaths as in hyperventilation exchange more of the alveolar gas with ambient air and have the net effect of expelling more carbon dioxide from the body, since the carbon dioxide concentration in normal air is very low.

The resulting low concentration of carbon dioxide in the blood is known as hypocapnia. Since carbon dioxide is carried as bicarbonate in the blood, the loss of carbon dioxide will drive bicarbonate to combine with hydrogen ions (protons) to form more carbon dioxide. The loss of hydrogen ions results in the blood becoming alkaline**, i.e. the blood pH value rises. This is known as a respiratory alkalosis.

This alkalization of the blood causes vessels to constrict (vasoconstriction).

### Patty Cakes — 2012-03-27T19:57:15+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-the-h-is-factor-x-post-your-guesses.42/post-7200>

I was googling dinos and extinction and came across this:

For the animals species, the major factors correlating with survival seem to be:

\* Small size

Cold-bloodedness

Burrowing adaptations

Flight adaptations

Deep-sea adaptations

The biological advantage that all of the above are associated with is lower oxygen requirements. Smaller sized animals have lower environmental oxygen requirements than large one. Cold-blooded animals have slower metabolisms and thus lower oxygen requirements than warm-blooded animals.

> Chloroplasts, the organelles of photosynthesis, also appear to be endosymbionts."
>
> . . . . . . .

MJ, your posts are way over my head . . . but I was just thinking . . .

Don't we burn a greater percentage of our energy in the from of fat when we have higher circulating oxygen levels? like at rest (when we should not have an oxygen deficit) as a percentage (not total) we are burning more calories from fat than we are (again as a percentage) during intense, anaerobic activity . . . . so maybe more circulating oxygen would mean more fat burning . . .

>

After impact, the asteroid would cause a conflagration of biblical proportions, burning everything. Survivors would need to be able to survive dark, cold, and low oxygen. The planet would be covered with a dark atmosphere blocking out the sun. Life in the oceans would be best adapted to survive ( bigtime users of the ancient pathway) and only those on land that still carried that pathway and could adapt. We are descended from them via the process of evolution. So, we have the ancient pathway--we are from the survivors. The oxygen in the atmosphere would be less, carbon dioxide and monoxide higher along with numerous other normally toxic gases. The survivors could adapt to the this set of gases.

### MJ* — 2012-03-27T20:16:21+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-the-h-is-factor-x-post-your-guesses.42/post-7227>

[http://jeb.biologists.org/content/201/8/1223.full.pd](http://http://jeb.biologists.org/content/201/8/1223.full.pd)

adaptations at stable low oxygen levels:

" Thus, the three modes of

adaptation to the oxygen minima which have been proposed

are as follows. (1) The development of mechanisms for the

highly effective removal of oxygen from water, allowing an

animal to regulate its rate of oxygen consumption down to low

partial pressures. (2) The reduction of metabolic rates so that

the organism can live at lower oxygen partial pressures with a

given set of uptake mechanisms. (3) The use of anaerobic

metabolism to make up the difference between aerobic

capacity and total metabolic demand.

### hellojtm — 2012-03-27T20:48:29+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-the-h-is-factor-x-post-your-guesses.42/post-7263>

Some more info from the science daily series:

"Professor Andy Purvis from Imperial College London's Division of Biology explains: "Our research has shown that for the first 10 or 15 million years after the dinosaurs were wiped out, present day mammals kept a very low profile, while these other types of mammals were running the show. It looks like a later bout of 'global warming' may have kick-started today’s diversity – not the death of the dinosaurs."

So there was a double dipping effect, first the KT wiped dinosaurs (except those that could retreat to water like crocodiles and the Loch Ness monster), but then there was a large global warming that wiped another set of mammals out. Leaving who? Our ancestors, able to survive extremes.

### hellojtm — 2012-03-27T22:13:09+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-the-h-is-factor-x-post-your-guesses.42/post-7325>

> **Souldanzer;7349 wrote:** whatever happened inside of us is an effect b/c it comes after the atmospheric change
>

>
> I'm not so confused about what exactly it is, I'm confused why one post got a full nope and the other one a very close?? When they are so closely related. I'm not sure I even care anymore. I think I know what it is but will probably leave this thread with a never mind.

Hi Souldanzer,

I think "climate change" was just too vague. Specifically the climate change caused by KT (the cretaceous extinction) and the following actions of mammals to:

a) thrive in the cold environments away from the toxic post-asteroidal environment

b) find high omega-3 foods there

c) down-regulate their metabolism etc.. in a hypoxic environment

d) rely on anaerobic metabolism (possibly using some fatty acids in a novel way that we aren't aware of)

and then

d) survive the global warming of the next 10-15 million years to develop the warm adapted state that we all live in now.

So, why is it optimal? I think it's optimal seasonally as an intense catabolic phase with reduced oxidation of the body and unregulated fatty metabolism. The cold triggers the ancient pathway and the KT explains why we have preserved it. All those mammals that didn't preserve it eventually died out either during the KT or the 10-15 million year later warm adaptation period.

And of course we know there were many ice ages after that and any non-oceanic species that survived those must contain both a warm and cold adapted metabolism.

So claiming atmospheric change is just too vague, IMO, or else we would have had it a week ago.

jesse

### jumcc — 2012-03-28T02:19:27+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-the-h-is-factor-x-post-your-guesses.42/post-7445>

my guess is it is the use of hydrogen sulphide to induce a state of hibernation.

'With H2S, a mammal can be turned functionally into a cold-blooded animal and cooled far lower than could otherwise take place \[to slow down the progression of injuries\]. This could save a lot of lives \[in a medical crisis\]. Each cell (already naturally) produces a minute amount of hydrogen sulfide, and it causes that cell to reduce activity. So when you're in a crisis, it's as if \[the cells and body\] come to the conclusion, "I'm in a crisis; I can't be expending energy, I better go into reserve \[mode\]."

Mother nature gave us this ability to use hydrogen sulphide as a way to slow us down and hibernate because of the mass extinctions that took place on our planet. She gave us this ability so that we could survive these events. Evolution was speeding up because species were dying too fast when earth encountered a major event causing extinction. She gave us the ability to be able to withstand these events and flourish. Interestingly enough Hydrogen Sulphide is what was the killer of species in up to 12 major extinctions. Mothernature gave us the ability so survive with it.

'It's believed that hydrogen sulfide was the cause of up to 12 major extinctions. Every mass extinction except the dinosaur extinction seems to have been caused by this. It's all about when the Earth decides to spit out these big burps of magma that come to the surface. But a big mass extinction from global warming has not happened in 100 million years. Those past episodes were from very rare flood basalts. There may not be another of these, as the Earth is cooling as it ages. But we've had these mass extinctions \[from hydrogen sulfide\] when carbon dioxide has hit 1,000 ppm. We have not hit that \[level\] for 100 million years. But we are currently at 380 ppm -- and climbing rapidly at 2 ppm a year and accelerating -- this is the highest CO2 I think in the last 40 million years. The only time \[these extinctions\] ever happened in the past is when these big flood basalts happened. But now we're making it happen far faster than the flood basalts ever did. This is a unique event in the history of the planet'

And they possessed those additional characteristics that made them adaptable to a radically different environment. The event selected for those characteristics as "the ticket" to move forward. Having this duality provided the survivors with the power of a power law to evolve faster (against a much more varied backdrop of environments)--a doubling characteristic, not linear. From Dr. Kruse's equation--the epigenetic factor is the square of factor x! Evolution did not just continue, it exploded because of the duality of characteristics to survive against a plethora of environments (temperature, altitude, O2 concentration, wet, dry, etc.). It's how Nature works. E=mcSQUARED!!!!!

Hope that helps. And I hope I don't get a nope.

### LisaAPB — 2012-03-28T06:36:40+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-the-h-is-factor-x-post-your-guesses.42/post-7596>

The game does not have to stop here, we haven't even talked about camels or Canadian frogs.... there is still lots to investigate. In that vein, here's a little tidbit about wood frogs, a hardy little amphibian that freezes in winter and later thaws hail and hearty and ready to breed:

> One response promoting freeze tolerance in freeze-tolerant frogs is the redistribution of up to 60% of the water normally found in tissues. Dissecting a frozen wood frog reveals that much of the ice is sequestered within the lymph system and in the coelom, where it may form without damaging delicate tissues and organs (Lee et al. 1992).
>
>
>
> Freeze tolerance is also promoted by the rapid synthesis of glucose from liver glycogen and the distribution of this cryoprotective agent to cells throughout the body. The accumulated glucose apparently enhances the survival of cells, tissues, and organs because experimentally administering additional glucose to the frog increases its tolerance to freezing (Costanzo et al. 1993). One of the primary functions of glucose is to raise the osmotic pressure of the body fluids, which in turn reduces the amount of ice that forms at any given temperature. Glucose transported into cells acts as an osmolyte, decreasing the degree of cell shrinkage during freezing, and also serves as a fermentable fuel that can be metabolized in the absence of oxygen. The wood frog also uses urea as a cryoprotectant. Unlike glucose, urea is accumulated during autumn and early winter, and is already localized within cells when freezing begins. Some evidence suggests that urea is more efficacious than glucose in preventing cryoinjury (Costanzo and Lee 2005).

[http://www.units.muohio.edu/cryolab/projects/woodfrogfreezing.htm](http://http://www.units.muohio.edu/cryolab/projects/woodfrogfreezing.htm)

### phillipsgeorge@hotmail.com — 2012-03-28T08:54:21+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-the-h-is-factor-x-post-your-guesses.42/post-7626>

I'm so slow. I was so mesmerised with the 'what' and not the why! What and how was he doing/using not why he was doing it. NO was an obvious guess, JK being a dentist as well.

Then I just woke up this morning at sun rise and up popped one of my slow thoughts; Darwininan evolution is so slooow but epigenetic adaptation can be so quick:

You can't evolve to survive the 'deadly fallouts' of a 'instant explosion' (there isn't time) but you can adapt if you already have a mechanism available.

A long time ago Darwinian evolutionary mechanics gave some Earthlife (by chance) an ancestor with DNA 'reprogrammable' in its own life time. Since then some Earthlife has had the ability to survive sudden Extinction Events that Darwinian genetics (chance mutations and survival of the fittest) wouldn't have time to cope with.

All you have to do is recreate an 'Extinction Event' in your cellar, live in it and wait for, epigenetically 'all the leads on your moog synthesiser to be pulled out and rearranged.' Then you can play a different tune!

Lamarck had it right all along.

Now its time for breakfast and getting ready to talk to a class of 5 year olds about how helicopters work!

I've enjoyed this thread! MJ\*, you really are a star!\*

### TheKid — 2012-03-28T16:53:47+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-the-h-is-factor-x-post-your-guesses.42/post-7716>

Watched Mark Roth's video. Interesting. It brings some points to mind:

1\. Are we really talking about some sort of actual hibernation/suspended animation, or will Factor X simply bestow some of the amazing benefits (do we know what they are?) that can be had from hibernation/suspended animation?

2\. Dr. K has mentioned before that in terms of human evolution, he believes there is no "missing link." Could this be because some sort of hibernation that significantly prolonged the duration of the generations? Roth talks about "immortality." Could there have been generations of hominids that were quasi-immortal, thereby sleeping through so much time that no missing link would be necessary to continue the hominid line?

3\. Could this all be related to the stories we find in the cultural and religious record about various long-lived individuals e.g. Rip Van Winkle, Methuselah & Noah in the Bible, or the People of the Cave in the Koran?

4\. Does hibernation and/or Factor X have healing or restorative properites beyond what we get from CT and Paleo eating? If it does not, then wouldn't hibernation just be a zero sum game? You'd either live a life in one straight shot, or you'd break it with extended periods of sleep. But you're waking time would be the same.

### LisaAPB — 2012-03-28T17:54:23+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-the-h-is-factor-x-post-your-guesses.42/post-7741>

OK, so the KT event is two letters. I guess I am having trouble understanding what would be so jaw dropping to an educated medical doctor/reporter. There was an event, which caused tremendous climate change (first heat, then cold) and the survival of the fittest in that event were those who took to aquatic or burrowing activities. Furthermore, some of the tiniest life, like bacteria, inserted itself into symbiotic relationships (ie: mitocondria) in order to survive. That is not new information, nor is it "jaw dropping". I'm still missing something.

What I've discovered thus far is that heat, and certain elements, maybe light too, signal that cold is coming. Many life forms use glucose as a form of "antifreeze" to survive the coming cold. Modern man continues to use glucose except the cold never really comes, hence diabetes would be helped with cold therapy.

### AmyK — 2012-03-28T19:17:51+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-the-h-is-factor-x-post-your-guesses.42/post-7781>

> **TheKid;7757 wrote:**
>
> 2\. Dr. K has mentioned before that in terms of human evolution, he believes there is no "missing link." Could this be because some sort of hibernation that significantly prolonged the duration of the generations? Roth talks about "immortality." Could there have been generations of hominids that were quasi-immortal, thereby sleeping through so much time that no missing link would be necessary to continue the hominid line?

Epigenetics is brand new to me, so I am still learning and may be way off on this.

What if, rather than beings dying out, the climate/environmental changes induced an extended state of suspended animation. While in that state, as metabolic processes were slowed, epigenetic changes sped up…so much so that when they finally emerged back into an animated state, they were unrecognizable in many ways? There would be no missing fossils accounting for the progression because the "original" hadn't died.

### TheKid — 2012-03-28T20:37:51+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-the-h-is-factor-x-post-your-guesses.42/post-7843>

With regard to factor X, it seems we're now leaning towards respiration. Maybe Hypoxia was nixed because maybe were not looking to achieve a true hypxic state. Rather I would imagine it is just greatly reduced respiration.

As I know, some of the animals who live the longest breath the slowest, such as the Gallapagos giant tortoise. And I personally use a device called Resperate to treat high blood pressure by slowing the rate of breathing to I think under 10 breaths per minute.

### phillipsgeorge@hotmail.com — 2012-03-28T20:59:36+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-the-h-is-factor-x-post-your-guesses.42/post-7854>

Every EE (e**Xtinction event) (and I include rapid onset climate change) requires single generation survivors. We don't have those ancient pathways left over simply as relics of previous phenotypes. We are here now because those pathways protected us in the past.

Darwinian mechanics (time, randon mutation, competition) slowly adapts life. Sudden stressors and the resultant epigenetic adaptation can quickly reselect older programs and adapt individuals to rapid changes in environment.

The ancient pathways are not relics of our evolution, like 'ontology recapitulating phylogeny' They are the** reason we are still here now.

### QuantumRadical — 2012-03-28T21:47:19+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-the-h-is-factor-x-post-your-guesses.42/post-7880>

> **localad;7896 wrote: Every EE (e**Xtinction event) (and I include rapid onset climate change) requires single generation survivors. We don't have those ancient pathways left over simply as relics of previous phenotypes. We are here now because those pathways protected us in the past.
>
>
>
> Darwinian mechanics (time, randon mutation, competition) slowly adapts life. Sudden stressors and the resultant epigenetic adaptation can quickly reselect older programs and adapt individuals to rapid changes in environment.
>
>
>
> The ancient pathways are not relics of our evolution, like 'ontology recapitulating phylogeny' They are the** reason we are still here now.**

**

Very insightful. Dr. Kruse did give us the answer in his opening remarks on his website.

_"Our biggest attribute is the ability to think. This allows us to radically change the environment that we are ideally adapted to. It has allowed us to dominate all habitats and create havoc in most of them as well. The real human miracle of our minds is not that we can see the world as it isÃ¢€Â¦Ã¢€Â¦but that we can see it as it is not, and then change it. If we think and act incorrectly, we can quickly recalibrate and overcome it. Conversely, we seem to be a prisoner to our paleo-cortex (older less evolved brain), and resist change even when we know it must occur. Many times we will subjugate the best interests of our survival to suit our emotional needs or desires. The real paradox of humanity is that our reasons for the things we do are often weak but our sentiments to do them remain quite strong. Interestingly, we have overcome that liability as a species many times so far. Some of us even find comfort in that ability at times."

If you really look at what he is saying, we not only "_can adapt to new environments quickly**" (using technology if we must, and we will because we can), but as he says, we are also prisoners to our past (paleo-cortex). So our evolutionary chain goes all the way back and has had to survive not only the slow march of time, but also the dramatic planetary level changes that can happen instantly (like the KT eXtinction event) or over geologically speaking short time periods (like the start of an ice age, or the end of one).

But the even more dramatic insight that I have gathered from reading everything on Dr. Kruse's website (blogs, comments, forums, etc.) is that we are now confronted with yet another dramatic (and planetary level) Factor X which is being caused by the very technology that has in the past, paradoxically, helped us adapt to our environments. And that Factor X is the speeding up of the epigenetic price we are paying living in modern society that is clearly mismatched to our long term healthy survival. So, the Doc has found a way to march us back to prison (paleo-cortex) as a path to overcoming the current Factor X. The path is in all of us, so adapt or die--it is Mother Nature's way.

I am mucho excited about THAT!

### LogicalLoser — 2012-03-28T22:12:49+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-the-h-is-factor-x-post-your-guesses.42/post-7894>

Just thinking outloud based on everything:

It seems whatever survived the KT had to go without O2 for a period and aerobic energy consumption. I.e it had to be able to go without breath for a period of time while the atmosphere was in conflagration and quickly switch to anaerobic energy. To survive it would have had to rapidly adapted or turn up this system.

It seems like it is intertwined with breathing or the ability to not have to breathe and survive. Two energy systems aerobic and anaerobic. We breathe now and have both capabilities. Can we turn off the Neolithic breathing and survive on the old for some extended time? If you close you mouth under your nose you force the issue. Perhaps you need cold to also make it efficient. Also oxidation damage could be the a huge mismatch if we evolve from anaerobics.

Am I crazy or is this logical. It seems to fit what I have seen on surviving the KT.

LL

### jeanie@truelongmont.com — 2012-03-28T22:13:39+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-the-h-is-factor-x-post-your-guesses.42/post-7895>

> **janagram;7904 wrote:** ENFP here.....yeah, I spend lots of time on forums and such, also have a high degree of introversion...myers briggs allows for high levels of opposing factors, they don't cancel each other out.
>
> This forum is full of brainiacs.....exciting to watch it unfold.

I think the concept of alternating behavior modes (sometimes E, sometimes I) is something overlooked by theorists - certainly not by those that apply the concepts as a means to understanding actual behavior. I am a very high I - yet I chose career paths rarely seen by I's (marketing/publicity 1st, and Real Estate Agent now) - I also experienced a very large shift in T/F after having a child. Pre child I was a strong INTJ, post child I am relishing my role as INFX - when it comes to my child and clients.

For practical reasons, with MB, you have to look at other things besides tested type - it's expression of behavior along with actual positive/negative experiences. Once you are able to have a real grasp of that, predicting behavior is scarily accessible.

### hellojtm — 2012-03-29T01:08:12+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-the-h-is-factor-x-post-your-guesses.42/post-7985>

> **LogicalLoser;7936 wrote:** Just thinking outloud based on everything:
>
> It seems whatever survived the KT had to go without O2 for a period and aerobic energy consumption. I.e it had to be able to go without breath for a period of time while the atmosphere was in conflagration and quickly switch to anaerobic energy. To survive it would have had to rapidly adapted or turn up this system.
>
>
>
> It seems like it is intertwined with breathing or the ability to not have to breathe and survive. Two energy systems aerobic and anaerobic. We breathe now and have both capabilities. Can we turn off the Neolithic breathing and survive on the old for some extended time? If you close you mouth under your nose you force the issue. Perhaps you need cold to also make it efficient. Also oxidation damage could be the a huge mismatch if we evolve from anaerobics.
>
>
>
> Am I crazy or is this logical. It seems to fit what I have seen on surviving the KT.
>
>
>
> LL

Hi LogicalLoser,

In order to "go back" to our anaerobic ancestors I'm pretty sure we'd have to become single cell organisms or amoebas. I don't think you can live entirely without oxygen unless you have, for all practical purposes, stopped living. I'm basing this on the notion that ~20% of our brain requires glucose to burn and that glucose requires oxygen to metabolize. If I'm wrong about that then there are probably 10 more reasons that I'm not even aware of.

Limited oxygen I can understand... perhaps down to 20% of what we currently use. OR, like people keep mentioning gases there may be a way to extract oxygen from gases, but I don't know enough about physiology to even imagine... for all I know the lungs would have to handle that?

jesse

### LisaAPB — 2012-03-29T05:54:28+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-the-h-is-factor-x-post-your-guesses.42/post-8122>

I'm going to start at zero, assuming it's the KT event, and imagine. At the time of the KT event, there were mammals, but they were very tiny. Anything that survived the KT event had to be able to take to water or underground burrowing, until the fires and heat died down. (Note: water and dark, to escape heat). Even the tiniest organisms like bacteria and virus' were thrown into an adapt or die situation. Many formed symbiotic relationships for mutual survival (Note: mitochondria). The impact of the meteor would have at first, released oxygen trapped in the rock and ore, but the fires would have consumed a lot of oxygen eventually, and the gaseous cloud enveloping the earth would have changed, and it would start to get dark and cold because no sunlight could get through. That was the foundation of the ancient pathway.

When you expose the body to cold for long periods of time, it forces the body to use less oxygen because the outer layers constrict and limit oxygen to the extremities. It will protect the core organs, heart, lung, kidney, liver, brain... all of which contain a heck of a lot of the body's mitochondria. Also most people naturally catch or hold their breath, and slow their breathing when exposed to cold, reducing the amount of oxygen. Mitochondria produces ATP by oxidization of glucose through aerobic respiration, however anaerobic respiration happens outside of the mitochondria. What does that mean, outside of the mitochondria?

I've been thinking about this entirely too much, my brain hurts and I still don't know exactly what Factor X is.

### AmyK — 2012-03-29T06:56:09+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-the-h-is-factor-x-post-your-guesses.42/post-8147>

> **August;8173 wrote:** No, I was more thinking about the posts re: something like cryogenically freezing or the video re: that goes to flat ling . . I'm not sure that's likely . . . . I have to admit though that I would be surprised if hypoxia it is either though . . . . it would seem surprising to me if a neurosurgeon favored hypoxia . . . . maybe I'm not understanding correctly . . . .

I'm not sure, and this may be too simplistic in reasoning, but I was thinking about oxygen in the way he talks about leptin. Sensitivity to leptin and oxygen are of upmost importance, but excessive amounts of either are not ideal.

My take on the freezing and flatlining, in a nutshell, is that he is wanting to prove that the body is capable of enduring a period of suspended animation, perhaps to a point where vital signs cannot be detected by our current methods. This would suggest that our ancestors, in whatever form they were in at the time of this event, also could have endured a period of climate-induced suspended animation. This would explain a lot. While in that state, their metabolic processes would have slowed and epigenetics would have compensated by speeding up. This would have led to exponential changes. If the suspended animation state was endured long enough, the being would emerge significantly changed. Not only might this explain why there are no "missing link" fossils, if the being emerged into an environment with different conditions than it had adapted to, perhaps because of the relatively rapid climate/environmental changes, it would be mismatched.

I could be mistaken, but I'm pretty sure that if this is indeed what he did, he would say "Don't try this at home."

### AmyK — 2012-03-29T14:01:41+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-the-h-is-factor-x-post-your-guesses.42/post-8189>

I woke up this morning remembering that, as a child, I used to love to exhale as much as I possibly could so I could sink to the bottom of the deep end of the pool and walk around for a while. It was the most peaceful and comforting feeling. Of course, I would have to eventually push myself back up for air, but I had the thought this morning...that if there was no other way to survive...if I instinctively knew that my body would know what to do and I would be okay, I could let myself stay there...

I don't know for sure if this is relevant to where Dr. K is going with all of this, but it filled me with a deep sense of reverence.

### Shijin13 — 2012-03-29T18:00:57+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-the-h-is-factor-x-post-your-guesses.42/post-8307>

> **AmyK;8169 wrote:** I don't know...even though Lance Armstrong and Michael Phelps use hypoxic tents and/or some sort of altitude training, I don't remember hearing that Wim Hof does. (If that was the sort of thing you were thinking about.)

FWIW the US Olympic committee's HQ isn't in Colorado Springs (7250ft above sea-level - actually higher in altitude than Denver!) by a random happen stance! Training at altitude increases red blood cells allow you to carry less O2 more efficiently.

Living yr round in the Springs and training - lets just say I wouldn't have to taper for National qualifying swim meets - Just step down in altitude. Every year we'd go to a BIG swim meet in December - lots of college teams will do short taper for meets in December to start qualification process for nationals. USAFA swimming was the only team at the meets we went too, where we didn't taper, shave, or wear the "fast suits" and we'd all go faster than our D1 competitors - We were D2. We were more efficient w/our O2 - b/c of training at altitude and in the cold pool. from what was explained to me by Richard Quick - Stanford & USOC womens swim coach - those extra blood cells will stay in your system for 3-4wks when you come down from altitude and can help you expand your VO2Max.

If I had $$ I'd seriously buy a well bred throughbred - and find an awesome trainer and train the horse at altitude, using slow long gallops, and water therapy; and grazing on wild grasses only; guarantee that horse (regardless if female/male) would be the next triple crown winner.

### differentstory — 2012-03-29T18:55:20+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-the-h-is-factor-x-post-your-guesses.42/post-8336>

> **August;8372 wrote:** Wow! I think speaking as someone with PCOS, I find that's pretty offensive!!!! . . . I've done varying degrees of carb restrictions for years (from extremely low carb to moderate) . . . I don't sunbath (although I do live in a warm climate . . . and I have been known to take a hot bath) . . . Based on my experiences and kind of of general layperson type of knowledge that I have had access to, thus far . . I would have to tell that PCOS is a very hard bell to unring . . .Maybe CT will be a game changer for PCOS . . . I hope . . but I don't know . . . . I don't believe that I've read the article that you are referring to . . . but it seems somehow unkind to me (and perhaps even a sign of ignorance or bigotry) to assert that women with PCOS who were not able to cure themselves with a low carb diet are either liars . . . or doing something else wrong. . . . and are therefore to blame for their condition . . . .
>
>
>
> I hope that I am misunderstanding what you are saying . . . I have to admit that I am very sensitive about the subject of PCOS . . .

I agree. I am sensitive as well about the subject. That said, I do understand chocolate's point somewhat because the way I got to JackKruse was via PCOS and along the way, I saw PCOS diagnoses being handed out like candy (have you see the soulcysters messageboards? There are THOUSANDS of women on them!!). It's a bit depressing to me.

And on those boards, I've seen a WIDE varieities of symptoms that encompass many different problems. I often find myself wondering if half of those of the women could just have weight/hormone issues? I'm not blaming the patients in the least - really I am blaming the doctors. I have found PCOS to be like the bucket at the end of the conveyor belt - a catchall if you will. Doctors rule out a variety of other things and then shrug their shoulders and say, "Hm. Must be PCOS." But maybe I've just had some pretty crappy doctors.

It just really got me thinking after I discovered all of the other diagnosed with PCOS. . .no offense.

### chocolate — 2012-03-29T19:26:25+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-the-h-is-factor-x-post-your-guesses.42/post-8357>

I am sorry, You are right. I am coming from a natural perspective of livestock and conditions. Two horse in one stall, one's ass gets very large the other gets very small. The food is double rationed. If the horses are separated, rations being equal, the skinny assed one grows and the fat assed one shrinks. The grain situation is deadly for horses pent up and even running turn out. Most of my opinions have come from horses. They have a cardiovascular system like ours, they sweat like we do, and they don't twin well, if you overgrain them, they'll shed out two eggs and miscarry twins. I have the most fabulous horse ever that was given to me by a gal that couln't not give her horse sweet feed and it happened two years in a row. Lots of horse owners think they are being nice to their horse and are killing them. I did that to my poor goats. I used to believe that people didn't eat very much and got fat... that's true for about a year. I gave credence to about one per cent. I used to work in a bakery and saw people closet eating, I have seen oreos discreetly placed in mouths like little mints. I find it exhausting to spend a lot of time with large eaters. I don't know. I personally know of one person that was going to an infertility doctor and paying the big bucks. I posted the study on facebook she quit going to the infertility doctor and went to a cakery , but she was honest with herself. Some people can't afford denial. And then to went get her face waxed. If you went to a horse track and told a trainer your story, the trainer would be polite and not believe you. And they would also tell you the healthiest place is outdoors. No respiratory infections, no hoof infections, they soak up the sun, even when they have shade as an option in the most miserable sun ever. I am sorry that you are struggling, for what ever reason. I got fat for the first time in my life ever, and thinking back, it was because I had an injury and stayed inside where it was warm. All that said, I had PCOS and didn't know what I was doing "wrong". I had a hysterectomy just from a chronically enlarged, not pathological (shrug) uterus at 34. I got pregnant twice on the pill. Once with a one day miss and once with a four hour gap. I had no reproductive control. Then it turns out my kid, who has text book measurements has symptoms and the diet makes them go away. Its a chronic undiagnosed problem for bipolar gals, only my kid isn't bipolar. She just ate grains. In the animal kingdom if you give too many grains, the animals over shed eggs. That's how you try to bump up your heard. About 4 months before breed season, you start giving them grain. Otherwise, you'll waste their muscles, make them get inflammed joints and lots of other bad stuff. I learned the hard way lots of things. I started with a few goats for weed control. I was giving them grain and they were popping out 5 and 6 babies. I wound up with them in my bathroom bottle feeding them and taking them to trader joes and mooching goats milk. I missed as much sleep as if they were my own babies. And god help you if you leave the door just slightly cracked. You will wake up with evidence of them having slept with you. All of the reading and clues and confidence I have gotten in no grain have come directly from livestock studies. If we are suspended animation, we are vegetables and our sexuality is changeable through carbs. In utero, somehow it is determined. Maybe our fertility issues come from the mismatch, we are supposed to be like frogs and lay lots of eggs and expel our placenta, then being fat and making lots of eggs is good. Because when your eggs hatch, you can't hunt for food, you'll be like a chicken stressing and watching and herding and protecting and nursing and losing a third of your weight while not hunting for food. But I'm ok with thinking that's where I came from and I am offended that no one told me sooner.

### chocolate — 2012-03-29T19:31:31+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-the-h-is-factor-x-post-your-guesses.42/post-8358>

[http://www.livestrong.com/article/473118-foods-that-fight-pcos/](http://http://www.livestrong.com/article/473118-foods-that-fight-pcos/)

[http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sex\_hormone-binding\_globulin](http://http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sex_hormone-binding_globulin)

However, recent evidence suggests that it is the liver's production of fats that reduces SHBG levels,\[1\]\[2\] not any direct effect of insulin and specific genetic mechanisms that do this have been found.

unbound hormones are all about the liver and the fat it must produce.... unbound hormones are from the liver making fat.... MAKING FAT!

Cold burns the fat out of the liver first, then moves along. The minerals that are dumped because of the sugars eaten or produced by the body are how hormones are regulated. You don't make more hormones, you either bind them or unbind them. If your body has control of the sugars, then there are reasonable expectations of certain things. If you shove fruits, sweets, and grains in... you can reasonably expect wacky hormones...no matter what time of year. I think its awesome that you have control by burning out the fat and the circadian clock will decide about when to dump minerals, if you let it.

Wow! That gives me SOOOOO much to think about! I think you have just totally changed years of thinking for me! I had to go back and read your thread a few times - there was so much info.  I have been diagnosed with PCOS and personally believe my menstrual/infertility issues are a result of my weight. I have seen it over and over. Each time I diet, I get my period within just a few days of starting the diet. This time, though, I seem to be a little more screwed up. One month in and I haven't gotten my period back. That's okay though, one day it will come. I am confident.

Apology accepted.

### LinD — 2012-03-29T21:16:14+0300

**I wish I could link to this study....

Integrating nitric oxide, nitrite and hydrogen sulfide signaling** in the ...[www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1095643312000219You](http://http://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1095643312000219You) +1'd this publicly.

by A Fago - 2012

Jan 31, 2012 – Hydrogen sulfide (H2S), nitric oxide (NO) and nitrite (NO2−) are formed in vivo and are of crucial importance in the tissue response to hypoxia, ...

This is cool:

[http://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0960894X09012876](http://http://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0960894X09012876)

Abstract

Hydrogensulfide has recently been considered to have an important role as a gasotransmitter in the cardiovascular system as well as in the central nervous system, but its action seems directly related to the presence of nitricoxide/nitricoxide-derivatives. We report here chemical evidence that emphasizes a prominent role of the hydrogensulfide as cofactor of NO-derivatives in inducing nitricoxide release.

\--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

### LinD — 2012-03-29T21:30:27+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-the-h-is-factor-x-post-your-guesses.42/post-8419>

Somebody stop me and tell me I'm way off track.....

[http://www.canadafreepress.com/index.php/articles-health/44936](http://http://www.canadafreepress.com/index.php/articles-health/44936)

New hybrid “NOSH aspirin” as possible anti-cancer drug

Scientists have combined two new “designer” forms of aspirin into a hybrid substance that appears more effective than either of its forebears in controlling the growth of several forms of cancer in laboratory tests. Their report on the new NOSH-aspirin, so named because it releases nitric oxide (NO) and hydrogen sulfide (H2S), appears in the journal ACS Medicinal Chemistry Letters.

Khosrow Kashfi, Ravinder Kodela and Mitali Chattopadhyay point out that NO and H2S are signaling substances produced in the body that relax blood vessels, reduce inflammation and have a variety of other effects. Scientists previously developed designer aspirin that releases NO in an effort to reduce aspirin’s potential adverse effects in causing bleeding in the gastrointestinal tract. Another designer aspirin that releases H2S was developed which also has anti-inflammatory properties and appears safe to the stomach.

Since NO and H2S are gases with physiological relevance, and Kashfi’s group had previously shown beneficial effects with both NO- and H2S-aspirins, they postulated that a new hybrid that incorporated both of these entities might be even more potent and effective than either one alone. Their hypothesis has proved to be correct.

They found indications that the new hybrid inhibits the growth of breast, colon, pancreas, lung, prostate and some leukemia cancer cells in laboratory tests. Some of the NOSH-aspirins tested were more than 100,000 times more powerful against cancer cell growth than aspirin alone. Promisingly, the group reported that their hybrids did not damage normal cells.

The authors acknowledge funding from the National Cancer Institute.

### LinD — 2012-03-29T21:51:47+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-the-h-is-factor-x-post-your-guesses.42/post-8435>

Interesting note by 'Hip' - [http://forums.phoenixrising.me/archive/index.php/t-10907.html?](http://http://forums.phoenixrising.me/archive/index.php/t-10907.html?)

HipApril 10th, 2011, 04:21 PM

L-arginine increases NO production, and it's cheap. It also increases stomach acid production.

Yes, it does. The enzymes that make NO (namely iNOS, eNOS, nNOS) do so using arginine directly. So if you increase arginine levels, you will get more NO. At least initially.

But unfortunately Whitlock thinks that these simple methods of boosting nitric oxide will not work in the long term, because NO levels are VERY tightly regulated in the body by many feedback systems (this tight control is necessary as NO is such a fundamental molecule in the body). So you may get a temporary NO boost by L-arginine, but the feedback systems will soon kick in, and undo your gains.

No harm in experimenting though. As Freddd points out, L-arginine can cause herpes outbreaks, so a better NO booster to try might be citrulline malate, which has equal if not better NO boosting potency. The malate is good in CFS anyway. There are also NO raising herbs like Gynostemma pentaphyllum (used in bodybuilding circles), which increase NO levels by reducing the NO-destroying free-radicals in the blood.

I have tried all the above, and experimented with taking them together in various combinations too, and though I often get immediate benefits, the benefits unfortunately seem to quickly disappear (after a day or so), which I guess is a result of these regulating NO feedback systems kicking in and bringing NO levels down again.

So the trick is not just raising NO, but raising NO in such a way that circumvents the NO level regulating mechanisms bringing NO levels back down again.

Interestingly, when I did manage to produce what seemed like a boost in nitric oxide, I temporarily lost all those slightly autistic traits that you get in CFS (the social withdrawal, and needing your own space more, needing solitude more, etc, etc). Propelled by a NO boost, I suddenly found myself wanting to speak to people, make friends, create rapport, feel for other people, etc, etc - all the natural human responses that seem to get much diminished in CFS (well, at least for me).

So this makes me think that there is something to this low NO theory of CFS and autism.

### chocolate — 2012-03-30T02:09:33+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-the-h-is-factor-x-post-your-guesses.42/post-8512>

I am sorry also if you felt that gay remark was insensitive. I was the only girl in my entire town of 500 in my age group and played with boys all the time, I was in the science classes and there were no other girls in them either. I was the stick you measured toughness against. I never learned to get along with catty women. I live/own in a gay neighborhood and the thought of waking up from nuclear winter and it not being gay would seriously depress me. However, I feel very lucky for my children to have grown up in such a fine neighborhood, without bratty kids to bully them or teach them bad habits. My kids were "the kids." I went through basic training one of the only two years that women did exactly what men did. There were accommodations made and heads turned for women falling short. I led the twenty mile road march at 118 pounds with 70 lbs of gear. Two women out of forty made it. The women would cry when they got a blister. At the end of the hike when the others were trucked in already setting up their tents, a sergeant brought me a cup of cold water and the other gals started bitching and saying I was the pet. I zeroed my weapon, I was the pet. I did everything. My dad was special forces and we never stopped moving when we were kids. We ran fence lines, we fed stock... we didn't eat junk. When we were in the mess hall the drill sergeants would take cake off the larger ladies plates and put it on mine and say things to them like, "you're fat... you don't need that." And tell me to eat it. I didn't initiate it, but the ladies would somehow get mad at me. The skinny guys got the same treatment, but the heavy guys didn't hate on them. They somehow could separate it, the cake was there for everyone .... So yeah, I wouldn't apologize for saying that I felt like a guy that likes guys trapped in a woman's body. I was laughing at the fact that it was circadian and I could have just as easily been born a guy or gay. And the andro gynic thing makes sense to me. I wore boys sized clothes. Its whatever the universe is calling for. I'm not sure its x or y any more....so confused. The scn decides or does it? It was more a question. But if you tell me specifically what it was...maybe I can clear things up.
