### Inger — 2012-03-30T08:16:51+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-the-h-is-factor-x-post-your-guesses.42/post-8660>

> **LinD;8467 wrote:** Somebody stop me and tell me I'm way off track.....
>
>
>
> [http://www.canadafreepress.com/index.php/articles-health/44936](http://http://www.canadafreepress.com/index.php/articles-health/44936)
>
> New hybrid “NOSH aspirin” as possible anti-cancer drug
>
>
>
> Scientists have combined two new “designer” forms of aspirin into a hybrid substance that appears more effective than either of its forebears in controlling the growth of several forms of cancer in laboratory tests. Their report on the new NOSH-aspirin, so named because it releases nitric oxide (NO) and hydrogen sulfide (H2S), appears in the journal ACS Medicinal Chemistry Letters.
>
>
>
> Khosrow Kashfi, Ravinder Kodela and Mitali Chattopadhyay point out that NO and H2S are signaling substances produced in the body that relax blood vessels, reduce inflammation and have a variety of other effects. Scientists previously developed designer aspirin that releases NO in an effort to reduce aspirin’s potential adverse effects in causing bleeding in the gastrointestinal tract. Another designer aspirin that releases H2S was developed which also has anti-inflammatory properties and appears safe to the stomach.
>
>
>
> Since NO and H2S are gases with physiological relevance, and Kashfi’s group had previously shown beneficial effects with both NO- and H2S-aspirins, they postulated that a new hybrid that incorporated both of these entities might be even more potent and effective than either one alone. Their hypothesis has proved to be correct.
>
>
>
> They found indications that the new hybrid inhibits the growth of breast, colon, pancreas, lung, prostate and some leukemia cancer cells in laboratory tests. Some of the NOSH-aspirins tested were more than 100,000 times more powerful against cancer cell growth than aspirin alone. Promisingly, the group reported that their hybrids did not damage normal cells.
>
>
>
> The authors acknowledge funding from the National Cancer Institute.

Hmmm... Aajonus Vonderplanitz ( [http://www.wewant2live.com/](http://http://www.wewant2live.com/) ) healed himself from cancer (and if I remember correctly he was autistic too) with raw, rotten meat (high meat)... and have helped many other heal from different contions with the same approach..

Wasn't nitric oxide builded up when meat etc rot? What this says about natural tribes using rotten fish, meat eggs... for health and curing disease?

Hmmm.... my brain hurts too...

**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-the-h-is-factor-x-post-your-guesses.42/post-10232>

I always wake up just before dawn these days. Often with unbidden, random thoughts. (Small mind so only small ideas) This morning I was thinking about storage processes.

Remember when computers had magnetic tape storage and you could 'wipe the drive clean' by using a powerful magnetic pulse? It reset the whole tape so you could set off from 'square one' again.

Made me think of the effect cold shock seems to have on the bio systems.

'The Man Who Reformatted His Hard Drive.'

### Jack Kruse — 2012-04-02T12:28:26+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-the-h-is-factor-x-post-your-guesses.42/post-10243>

exactly what winter does for mammalian biochemistry.......it is our reset button and we never truly see a winter any longer.......instead we see a perennial summer.......artificial lights and carbohydrates.......that speed up our chemical clocks and shorten our telomere. Therefore the solution to repairing many neolithic diseases is to face a winter in winter and maybe a few induced winters during other seasons to reset what is broken........the more broken one is the longer their winter exposure needs to be.

### Melinda — 2012-04-06T02:43:09+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-the-h-is-factor-x-post-your-guesses.42/post-12009>

In fish, the Rete mirabile: A countercurrent exchange system is utilized between the venous and arterial capillaries. By lowering the pH levels in the venous capillaries, oxygen unbinds from blood hemoglobin. This causes an increase in venous blood oxygen concentration, allowing the oxygen to diffuse through the capillary membrane and into the arterial capillaries, where oxygen is still sequestered to hemoglobin. The cycle of diffusion continues until the concentration of oxygen in the arterial capillaries is supersaturated.......The Rete Mirabile allows for an increase in muscle temperature in regions where this network of vein and arteries is found. The fish is able to thermoregulate certain areas of their body. Additionally, this increase in temperature leads to an increase in basal metabolic temperature. The fish is now able to split ATP at a higher rate and ultimately can swim faster.

In mammals: Vascular retia mirabilia are also found in the limbs of a range of mammals. These reduce the temperature in the extremities. Some of these probably function to prevent heat loss in cold conditions by reducing the temperature gradient between the limb and the environment. Others reduce the temperature of the testes increasing their productivity. In the neck of the dog, a rete mirabile protects the brain when the body overheats during hunting; the venous blood is cooled down by panting before entering the net.

Retia mirabilia also occur frequently in mammals that burrow, dive or have arboreal lifestyles that involve clinging with the limbs for lengthy periods. In these cases, the rete mirabile may lower limb temperature and therefore the metabolic requirement for oxygen and nutrients in the tissues of the limb. This would be desirable when the blood supply to the limb is limited as a result of a "diving reflex" diverting blood away from the limbs during diving or burrowing or because of postural restrictions on blood supply in the case of clinging, arboreal animals. In the last case, slow-moving arboreal mammals such as sloths, lorises and arboreal anteaters possess retia of the highly developed type known as vascular bundles. The structure and function of these mammalian retia mirabilia are reviewed by O'Dea (1990).\[2\]

These quotes are from Wikipedia...I need to study about how far back in evolution this showed up...still on the hunt for X

The last link, AmyK, says it all.... Except Factor X, of course... Or does it? LOL

" But could there be a simpler solution? According to Professor Cawthorne, the brown fat levels of our hunter-gatherer ancestors would almost certainly have been more highly activated than our own.

Warmer temperatures, abundant food and too little activity have effectively switched off its usefulness in the modern world.

'Even 30 years ago, it was more difficult to stay warm than it is now,' says Professor Cawthorne. 'Today, our homes, cars, offices, shops and almost everywhere we go are warm.'Just turning off the central heating could help spur brown fat into action. 'If we were to expose ourselves to cooler temperatures more often, then a lot of people would probably lose weight,' he says.'Either that or have a daily sauna and plunge into icy water afterwards.

Don't have the heating on in the car and spend more time outdoors, especially in winter. We need to activate brown fat and there are simple ways to do it that may have some benefit.' "

**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-the-h-is-factor-x-post-your-guesses.42/post-12600>

Here's some more that I copied from Wikipedia:

VNS may also be achieved by one of the\*vagal maneuvers: holding the breath for a few seconds, dipping the face in cold water, coughing, or tensing the stomach muscles as if to bear down to have a bowel movement.\[3\]\*Patients with\*supraventricular tachycardia,\[3\]atrial fibrillation, and other illnesses may be trained to perform vagal maneuvers (or find one or more on their own).Vagus nerve blocking (VBLOC) therapy is similar to VNS but used only during the day. In a six-month\*open-label trial\*involving three medical centers in Australia, Mexico, and Norway, vagus nerve blocking has helped 31 obese participants lose an average of nearly 15 percent of their excess weight. A year-long 300-participant\*double-blind,\*phase IItrial has begun.\[4\]

Also see: http://newsblog.mayoclinic.org/2008/06/23/device-jamming-stomach-nerve-signal-shows-promise-in-obesity/

### Jim — 2012-04-09T01:55:59+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-the-h-is-factor-x-post-your-guesses.42/post-12827>

OK I must admit that I have not read this entire thread so hopefully I am not repeating anything. Here goes.

I went back to the Quilt and looked at Level #1 - the most important one. Here it is in it's entirety

> Current cellular homeostasis determines cellular fates all of the time. This is the cornerstone of my theory of longevity. We must stop focusing in on disease states and instead think about what happens to the cellâ€™s environment that dictates its fate. The cellular terroir are what cause the disease states. This is my cellular theory of relativity. My theory of aging is that the environment the cell finds itself in is directly proportional to outcomes the cell may face.
>
>
>
> In my mind, these levees will uncover a new paradigm that requires the â€œpatientâ€￾ and physician to forget about the disease states and think first about the normal cell. This theory will require you, the reader, to embrace paradox and ambiguity at every turn.

Then I read this article in Time Magazine on epigenetics [http://www.time.com/time/magazine/article/0,9171,1952313,00.html](http://http://www.time.com/time/magazine/article/0,9171,1952313,00.html)

Very interesting that the example they used was from a group of folks living in Northern Sweden and spoke about the cold and starvation.

Putting it all together. basically I believe that if we change our nutrition and our terrior (our environment) to the way our ancestors lived i.e. cold adapted and nutritionally "in the moment", then we can drive our personal epigentics in reverse to free us from our neolithic diseases.

### jeanie@truelongmont.com — 2012-04-09T02:13:34+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-the-h-is-factor-x-post-your-guesses.42/post-12831>

> **Jack Kruse;12733 wrote:** except byncritics with small minds.......

Critics are such an important symbol in progress. NO great ideas are met with immediate approval from everyone. If the idea/concept/new thing is bad or worthless, people don't get worked up and bother rejecting.

Next time your in a committee/group. Propose something really boring and lame and simply not true. Watch how it kind of dies in their minds. It must have some kernel of relevance - even if it's unconscious to a person, it forces priority in subject hierarchy in the brain. Their body forces it to be relevant, thus behaviors are altered because of this.

So, when someone has a need to outwardly criticize (without a deliberate motivating factor of changing something), it means you got them. It's defensive. It's a threat. Animals cornered with a threat are feral-behaving.

So it would literally be IMPOSSIBLE, no matter what the evidence/proof etc, for you to make this kind of change in something without eliciting a feral response in others.

It's more of that balance of nature stuff. Wish I could have understood all this long ago, but here we are - hopefully I can impart some wisdom to the next gen.

as a slight aside: I've really enjoyed reading all this tapestry and threads of thought from the Doc and from you all. I've been to places/ideas (thanks to the web) I'd never even dreamed of looking at a few months ago.

I've even just resolved a long standing difference of opinion between my wife and I that, years ago, even threatened (because of my bull headedness) our marriage. One sentence I read here a few hours ago completely changed my viewpoint of human life. It was a thought, an observation on human potential that suddenly made sense to me. Perhaps the insight was made clearer to me thanks to the ongoing enlightenment (thanks to this blog) on what can be acheived by people.

My wife was a primary school teacher; she has always held that every child has the potential (given the opportunity) to acheive greatness. I was of the darwinian, bell shaped curve, what we inherit is what we get, IQ type of school. We would argue to near divorce over intelligence potential. For years it has been a subject we have always avoided!

Well, very recently a very short quotation I read here gave me a sudden 'blindingly obvious' paradigm shift on human potential. So now the one hidden difference that we agreed to bury long ago rose to the surface of my thoughts and was rubbed out for ever.

So thankyou for that Doc! (and my marriage is now perfect!)

and what was so blindingly obvious I just couldn't see it?...............

> Einstein didn’t wait until he was Einstein to perform like an Einstein.

### LisaAPB — 2012-04-12T07:36:21+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-the-h-is-factor-x-post-your-guesses.42/post-14114>

I've been considering UV radiation and Electromagnetism and thought this was interesting:

> The risks of damage to our bodies due to radiation exposure certainly give us good reason to be very wary of unnecessary exposure to nuclear radiation. However, it is important for us to keep things in perspective. We are constantly bombarded by nuclear radiation. The stars in our galaxy (as well as all other shining stars, and including our own sun) shine by virtue of nuclear reactions primarily in their cores. Particles created in these nuclear reactions can escape the stars’ surfaces and travel through space, some of which can eventually hit the earth’s surface (and us!). In addition, supernova explosions which occur during the death-stages of massive stars result in the emission of many high-energy particles.
>
>
>
> The net result of all of this is that we are constantly bombarded by particles (mostly protons, electrons, and gamma rays, but also various other nuclei) from outer space. These particles are referred to as cosmic rays. In addition, these cosmic rays can interact with molecules in the earth’s upper atmosphere to create even more particles (known as secondary cosmic rays) which shower down on us here at the earth’s surface. It is estimated that approximately one high-energy (up to about 1020 eV!) cosmic-ray particle strikes the earth’s atmosphere per square-centimeter per second, and about two secondary cosmic ray particles reach the earth’s surface per square-centimeter per second. **It has been proposed that approximately 2% of all genetic mutations of organisms on Earth are due to biological damage caused by cosmic rays. Since cosmic rays can be stopped by the earth’s atmosphere, it follows that the higher your altitude above the earth’s surface, the greater your exposure to cosmic radiation. For example, mountain climbers or airline passengers will have a slightly higher rate of exposure to cosmic radiation than people on the earth’s surface.
>
>
>
> In addition to receiving radiation from above us, we are also constantly bombarded by radiation from beneath our feet. Naturally occurring radioactive elements in rocks in the earth’s crust are constantly emitting radiation in all directions, including upwards towards us.**

[Radiation](http://http://frank.mtsu.edu/~phys2020/Lectures/Part_2__L6-L11/L11/Sources_of_Radiation/sources_of_radiation.html)

But, for some really interesting stuff, go to this link... starting around page 4, the electromagnetic properties of organisms. [Earth Electromagnetism AC/DC](http://http://www.hese-project.org/hese-uk/en/niemr/natural_emr.pdf) Wow.

### Pardus — 2012-04-12T23:18:36+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-the-h-is-factor-x-post-your-guesses.42/post-14396>

> **Owl;14296 wrote:** Factor X is the third thing, in addition to the Leptin Reset and CT, that we need to become optimal.

According to Dr. Kruse:

"All this has happened as our brain underwent explosive growth due to power laws after a a biologic event (FACTOR X) that caused a relative acceleration of evolution but a faster one of epigenetic control. this time frame shift with the simultaneous increase of primate evolution created the perfect storm for humans."

He also mentioned earlier that Factor X was the evolutionary bottleneck that caused epigenetics to speed up in mammals (humans).

I would think that this is referring to an event, such as a world wide scale natural disaster or radical change in climate.

What would "bottleneck" the genepool and thus select for ONLY cold-adapted mammals to survive into the future?

I would guess that it was some sort of mass extinction resulting from a rapid and sudden decrease in temperature and light (hence, cold, dark and sleepy) where only warm-blooded animals who were able to induce adequate thermogenesis, or hibernate and widthstand the cold would survive through to the future.

I think I read somewhere that during suspended animation, the slowed bodily functions speed up epigenetic signalling. I would guess Factor X is some sort of ice age, or extinction event, which completely eliminated non-cold-adapted, non-hibernating mammals (if they even existed in the first place), ensuring that any surviving remaining mammals were cold adapted, hibernating mammals which lived in the ancient pathway.

Furthermore, due to the suspended animation or hibernation they used to endure this extinction event, their epigenetics sped up. Either that, or only the animals with sped up epigenetics would survive the harsh, rapid/sudden-changing, unforgiving conditions of the extinction event.

These ancient-pathway mammals were the only ones that survived the extinction event and we are direct descendants from them, therefore we have inhereted their cold-adaptation pathways, as well as their sped-up epigenetics.

If factor x is something we can walk on, why not snow or ice? (as in ice age). We also learn about the extinction of the dinosaurs, as well as ice ages in early elementary school, so it fits the bill as well. If not, then it could also be the ground, because in order to survive the extinction event, the mammals would have to retreat underground and enter hibernation, where it was cold, dark, and sleepy. I'm sure snow/ice/ground are 2 letter words in several languages around the world. If not, other guesses would be rocks (as in, the great meteor which supposedly set off an extinction of the dinosaurs) or magma (ie, ancient active volcanos worldwide spewing ash, dust, and certain types of gasses into the air rendering the planet cold, dark, and very inhospitable for a period of time).

Hence, we (all mammals) are all descended from these cold adapted, warm-blooded mammals, and thus we should all have the genes necessary to induce extreme thermogenesis in freezing cold temperatures, along with most other amazing abilities that stem from the ancient pathway (ie, ridiculous immunity for fighting infections, curing disease, etc).

I doubt factor X will add any more "optimizing" protocols, rather it will simply put them into proper perspective and context. After you learn of factor X, you will want to become cold, dark, and sleepy, because it mimics the same extinction event which directly selected for the survival of your ancient eutherian mammal ancestors millions of years ago. It really is like nature's reset button.

### pthomaslandry@gmail.com — 2012-04-13T14:35:35+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-the-h-is-factor-x-post-your-guesses.42/post-14652>

Right under our noses... Or perhaps another clue might be the noses themselves?

I was thinking about our relative hairlessness, and then our other more obvious feature differences to our closest evolutionary cousins. I thought that it would be a good adaptation for being in water and diving...

From wikipedia:

The aquatic ape hypothesis relates the nose to a hypothesized period of aquatic adaptation in which the downward-facing nostrils and flexible philtrum prevented water from entering the nasal cavities.\[10\] The theory is not generally accepted by mainstream scholars of human evolution.\[11\]

Also allows us to live in colder/drier climates by moistening and warming air.

Low body hair is attributed to either a cooling effect, but also reduces drag in the water, and has little benefit to burrowing animals like the naked mole.

KT or a similar event lead our mamillian ancestors to the cold ground/water for food, as terrestrial life was mostly killed off. This, I believe is factor X.

The reason that we still have the ancient pathway is that DNA is additive, (Fibonacci) so the event that caused evolution to speed up granted us the gift of this enhanced and most efficient pathway.

I'm still on the electromagnetic kick (E-M). Here's something to think about: the force that keeps you from falling through the floor is the electromagnetic force which causes the atoms making up the matter in your feet and the floor to resist being displaced. I found that on a kids science page called The Particle Journey. E-M is temperature sensitive, and consider this (from wikipedia): Ordinary matter takes its form as a result of intermolecular forces between individual molecules in matter. Electrons are bound by electromagnetic wave mechanics into orbitals around atomic nuclei to form atoms, which are the building blocks of molecules. This governs the processes involved in chemistry, which arise from interactions between the electrons of neighboring atoms, which are in turn determined by the interaction between electromagnetic force and the momentum of the electrons.

### hellojtm — 2012-04-13T22:19:36+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-the-h-is-factor-x-post-your-guesses.42/post-14841>

What major atmosphere changes have happened in mammalian history? I'm not asking what the possible events were, but what were the changes. We have:

ice ages

heat waves?

gas changes?

Of those, what are the specifics underlying the change, i.e. did CO2 go up, down, nitrogen up, down, etc... Was there more or less sunlight... etc..

Widdle those down to some set of factors that could produce a selectable advantage for a warm adapted mammal, i.e. a selection against cold adapted organisms. In that corner case any land based organism had to be warm adapted or die and all oceanic life was able to remain cold adapted.

Bottleneck

[http://www.scientificamerican.com/article.cfm?id=when-the-sea-saved-humanity](http://http://www.scientificamerican.com/article.cfm?id=when-the-sea-saved-humanity)

Specifically, MIS6, which left us with only hundreds of H.sapiens, who survived on the southern coast of Africa by supping on the bounty of shellfish and living in caves.

We have all descended from this group of survivors, and we have their epigenetics.

It's not a question of evolving from an aquatic ape, but rather into an aquatic ape. Epigenetics and factor X made it happen much more rapidly than what we typically look for in the fossil record.

### AndrÃ©s — 2012-04-15T21:33:56+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-the-h-is-factor-x-post-your-guesses.42/post-15368>

I jump to this thread since this post is not thyroid related.

[After some clues Dr. Kruse has put forward](http://http://forum.jackkruse.com/showthread.php?773-CT-for-hashimoto-s-\(Scared-\)&p=14769&viewfull=1#post14769), I think Factor X could be slight hypoxia, or we could talk about O2 restriction. I have found that fetuses has [65% saturation on left atrium](http://http://ceaccp.oxfordjournals.org/content/5/4/107.full) while usual adult levels seems to be [95%](http://http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Pulse_oximetry) (my biology background is from when I was 16 years old). It should be the reason for Dr. Kruse to be so sure that everyone of us has the ancient pathway still intact: we would have been naturally aborted in the womb if not.

Nevertheless, there are some interesting links about hypoxia AND cold: [effect on rats hearts](http://http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/20801191), [the relation of eutherian hibernation biology with other ancient mechanisms (_anoxia tolerant turtles and molluscs, estivating toads and snails_)](http://http://www.cnslab.carleton.ca/~kbstorey/pdf/451.pdf) --I would bet that eutherian survived the meteor crash event while hibernating on winter--, [or the unusual high results of asthmatic winter athletes (Figure 4)](http://http://ep.physoc.org/content/95/3/422.full) (I am not sure this is related or just it looks like it).

Cheers!

### chocolate — 2012-04-17T01:11:35+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-the-h-is-factor-x-post-your-guesses.42/post-15747>

I think it is some odd stinky breathing. I keep thinking about that bear in captivity eating her baby. I had hamsters once that were supposed to be like sexed and weren't. The normal thing happened. The mommy had a litter with one kind of slow viable runt that she buried in the deep moist litter. They need their own waste for antibodies, like probiotics, from what I understand.( I know for sure chickens do. You have to bury the moisture and let it cure. They scratch and inhale probiotics and it is their colostrum.)I dug it out and tried to latch it on. She buried it again. I dug it out again, she buried it again. I dug it out again, she ate it. She had noble intentions and good instincts. I didn't mind my own business.

### pthomaslandry@gmail.com — 2012-04-17T15:38:50+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-the-h-is-factor-x-post-your-guesses.42/post-15866>

> **janagram;15233 wrote:** Tom. Now you've got me interested; I'm really fascinated by borderline theories...can you share yours, or maybe if I pm you..

Off topic, but somewhat more relevant given the context of CT 10

My short story career wise

Chemical Engineer/hobbiest musician

To software/systems engineer/hobbiest musician with horrible sciatica

Enter the work of F.M. Alexander

(here is Nobel laureate Nikolaas Tinbergen's acceptance speech where he devotes a substantial amount of time to the Technique [http://www.youtube.com/watch?v=XXr-9kQZ0ow&feature=youtube\_gdata\_player\[/video\]\]\[video=youtube\_share;XXr-9kQZ0ow\]http://www.youtube.com/watch?v=XXr-9kQZ0ow&feature=youtube\_gdata\_player\[/video\]](http://[video=youtube_share;XXr-9kQZ0ow)). Other proponents of his work include Aldous huxley and George Bernard Shaw to name a few.

My sciatica went away just by becoming more aware and using my whole self better and thus not interfering with the reflex system that coordinates human movement (he named it the primary control).

I realized immediately that the implications of the Alexander technique across multiple fields are massive, and that the technique has never been understood by more than several students who have n-1 changes to awareness and function. Wanting to push and better understand the mechanisms involved I gave up the engineering and became an Alexander technique teacher and pro musician.

The work essentially boils down to awareness and allowing human movement (and I believe thought) to operate using its optimal design. But there is a mechanism here and it needs to be better explored. It is not steeped in anything mystical, FM was a guy who had a problem, traditional medicine did not help his problem, so he observed himself and figured it all out. i.e. he used the scientific method on himself to solve a much larger human problem (sounds a little familiar right?).

Understanding that there were more inputs to the human system than the psyco-physical applications of awareness, I started using a paleo diet about 7 years ago, and became a personal trainer/fitness nutritionist so I could help even more people who know that they need positive change, but are typically misguided about how to get there (low fat sad, calorie counting, wods or chronic cardio...)

I am excited that dr. Kruse opened my eyes to more variables and helped me better understand the mechanisms from both a historical and practical view.

I agree completely with his moderation statement, my clients frequently use this as a mantra or excuse. I typically tell them it's a great idea, as they can get every disease of civilization in moderation that way too. A little cancer, a little diabetes, hell lets throw in some moderate arsenic and heroin too, just in case... moderation.... How blind people are when there's pleasure involved...

...was thinking about recent evolution/adaptation in response to the environment. Lightbulb moment\*; the obesity 'epidemic' isn't an illness; its an adaptation!

Grain agriculture, night lighting and central heating are like the fallout environment from an extinction event, only in SLOW MOTION! (and this time we set it off and the fallout is now pretty much finally worldwide) It's been slow enough spreading for a bit of actual 'evolution' in places (eg lactose tolerance) but the rest (eg.metabolic syndrome) has just been short term epigenetic switching to stay 'enjoying' the comfort zone of wheat, sugar and warmth.

The trick is now to carefully pick and enjoy the useful 'fruits of civilisation' (applemac?) while convincing (tricking?) our body biochemistry that the environment we live in is still that slightly older one we had beautifully evolved to just before the slow bomb went off.

And now it's time for my morning hot bath, hair shampoo and bowl of Kelloggs.

Wheat is pretty much in EVERYthing these days and modern wheat is totally unfit for human consumption due to hybridization that has turned those "amber waves of grain" into a stunted dwarf grass that is resistant to drought, bugs and digestion. The sugars in wheat will spike your blood glucose levels higher than simple table sugar! and the protein in wheat crosses the blood/brain barrier!!!! and attaches to opiate receptors in the brain. Which, in plain English, makes it as addictive as, well, opiates. The end result is, studies have shown, people who indulge in wheat-containing foods will eat, on average, 400 calories a day MORE than those who do not.

Let's not educate people to the hazards of wheat. let's give em another prescription drug to buy at a hyper inflated price.

These factors combined with research developed food production create a synergistic epigenetic pathway altering our health.

### DarleenMB — 2012-04-18T20:17:14+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-the-h-is-factor-x-post-your-guesses.42/post-16275>

Hmm. where the heck did my sense of humor and irony disappear to? I could have sworn I had it right here in my back pocket. OH WAIT. I was SITTING on it. Silly me.

And DonKjelberg you are absolute correct. Obesity rates didn't begin that climb into double digits until well into the '70's. Prior to that people were mostly normal weight (whatever that might be) and NO one (except athletes) was out exercising like a maniac. We can thank Jane Fonda and "feel the burn!" for that. /sarcasm

When I was 14 we went to CA to visit relatives. We went to Pacific Ocean Park one night and as we walked in saw a couple walking out. EVERYONE, and I mean everyone, was staring. The woman was huge (for the times). Today she'd be considered "average." But she was an anomaly back then. And I am not exaggerating when I say people literally stopped in their tracks when they saw her.

And btw, wheat has NOT been genetically modified. It has been hybridized. Two entirely different things. Davis's book "Wheat Belly" has a lot of interesting info in that regard. Today's post on his [blog](http://http://www.wheatbellyblog.com) is timely as well.

Off to see if I can revive my sense of humor. I might have kilt it when I sat on it.

### Guest — 2012-04-19T02:15:51+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-the-h-is-factor-x-post-your-guesses.42/post-16396>

I never did get that moderation is the key. I laugh cause it's true, and it use to make me wonder what was wrong with me that I just couldn't do moderation or get that it was so important, like people made it out to be. I know frustration would set in whenever people mentioned it to me--I wanted to tell them to "shut the hell up already"--heard it once to often. Anytime I did try to conform, it got me no where fast and I felt more out of sync with life and those around me. I have wasted so much time trying to conform and fit in. I learned it was easier to stay away from people because, I felt, they never listened and really didn't care what I thought. I decided to become indepentent from others and self-governing (secluded/a hermit). I am not anti-social and most hermits aren't but they are just tired of people. I chose it and have become quite comfortable with it. I live in close proximety to others so I had to build a wall, a sanctuary within which does not isolate me but rather steels me for daily life until something better comes along.

I see nothing wrong with practice being largely an internal work, a work of changing self from caring about the anger and resentment to caring about nothing in particular, then letting that empty space fill only with blessed things.

### QuantumRadical — 2012-04-19T15:33:37+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-the-h-is-factor-x-post-your-guesses.42/post-16562>

> **Jack Kruse;16602 wrote:** this is the closest you've come to Factor X my friend......and you did not even try to. Think about that.

Localad is essentially describing the "BUTTERFLY EFFECT" in complexity theory. Biology and evolution are nonlinear, and thus small changes in initial conditions can manifest downrange in time as huge changes. It is not a straightline, predictable course of cause and affect, but very possible, and even likely, to see "jumps" (step functions). Exponentially progressing changes can and do occur. Missing link? Nope, we skipped that step (a jump)! As biology manifests, it becomes more COMPLEX, no longer predictable in a Newtonian physical description, but more problematic as in a Quantum Physical description. Thus, epigenetic expression accelerates well ahead of any DNA changes needed to adapt to changing conditions-- like diet, or cold. Just as in QP where an electron can seem to be in two places at once, so too in biology we can seem to have two ( or more) expressions at the time in the same species due to accelerating epigenetic changes, and that is Mother Nature's hedge on adapting to environmental changes-- it is all about selecting for survival-- the more expressions, the more likely at least one has the right stuff to carry on.

### QuantumRadical — 2012-04-19T17:19:30+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-the-h-is-factor-x-post-your-guesses.42/post-16594>

> **MJ\*;16653 wrote:** Randy what do you do for a living?

Hi, MJ. I work for a defense contractor involved in building the toys that our boys can use to do bad things to bad people--mostly in the very advanced technology R&D side. I am a retired Air Force Colonel. I have a BS in Physics (my favorite topic was Quantum Physics) and highly advanced mathematics comes naturally to me--a gift. I attended the National War College in the mid 90's and wrote a paper on how physics, and other sciences, change our language about ourselves and our universe, and how Quantum Physics would have the same affect on our culture, over time, that Newtonian Physics had in terms of the Renaissance, the Industrial Age, the conduct of war and peace, etc. My thesis was that Quantum Physics, along with the other sciences, would usher in its (and their) own version(s) of change. No longer do we solve problems in a scientifically stovepiped fashion, but the real breakthroughs occur in the synthesis, the combining of ideas. I truly believe that we are just now beginning to see the Revolution--robotics, DNA manipulation, genetic manipulation, artificial biological life, artificial machine intelligence, etc. The first indications of this Revolution were the near simultaneous creation of atomic energy (reactors and bombs) and computers--that was no coincidence by the way. With the discovery of what DNA looked like (double helix) and the subsequent ability to leverage computer technology has resulted likewise in the evolutionary medical revolution that Jack Kruse is talking about. It all started back at the end of the 19th century and the beginning of the 20th century with the fact that Newtonian based science was an inadequate description of the world, subsequently replaced by quantum physics, the theory of relativity, et al. The Revolution could span several hundreds of years, but it has definitely started. I am starting my own Blog to talk about this New Next Revolution--quantumradical.com--and hope to have my first blog up by the end of this weekend. These are very exciting times, ripe with opportunity and risk! (yes, I could not believe that that domain for my blog was actually still available--fate, maybe?)

Remember, a cold adapted human is just another epigenetic expression of himself or herself in time. Choose wisely! )

NS?

It's not that obvious is it? The step functions in evolutionary history explained by sped up **natural selection** due to rapidly changing environmental factors. Our epigenetic responses to them brought to us courtesy of our genetic ancestors (Fibonacci sequence) were pre-selected for us... So we are reacting/(mis)evolving to our mismatched environment and while our reaction is based on our DNA, our ability to bring back our environment can bring us back to optimal.

We don't control the mechanism of reaction, we control the variables.

Now add these 'hedge better' letters as well: QM, Quantum Mechanics.

OK: I'm going 'QM'.

### pthomaslandry@gmail.com — 2012-04-19T23:46:05+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-the-h-is-factor-x-post-your-guesses.42/post-16752>

> **villjamur\_stevenson;16808 wrote:** It's gotta be the Triassicâ€“Jurassic extinction event - everyone learned about that in 6th grade

No, that wasn't the bottleneck, the bottleneck was only about 200k years ago when there were less than 1000 humans who dove for shellfish on the tip of Africa.

The Jurassic event explains some of our adaptations, and why the pathway still exists. It started with those mammals, then it was utilized multiple times (each ice age).

The MIS6 ice age is what drove us to the oceans, cold, dark calorie restricted and caves. Because our population was so small, we sped up our natural selection.

(we became aquatic apes)

I'm almost at that point myself! This thread has been pretty good for piecing together a lot of horizontal thinking.

The problem I'm having is defining the question in way that it can be solved. We know that it's an evolutionary bottleneck and an evolutionary reason why natural selection has chosen the natural pathway. (according to ct-8)

The bottleneck would probably be an event (like MIS6, as in the scientific American article about the sea saving humanity). The reason is most likely an environmental factor, or maybe the environment itself. Just not really understanding what can speed up our evolution from a nuts and bolts point of view.

What variables do we have? Temperature, Light, Magnetic fields, electrical conductivity/impedance, grounding, resonant frequencies, all of the chemical interactions...

Add more...

Hydrocarbons? I should have taken organic chemistry back in the day.

Maybe the horizontal approach covers up or stifles us from looking a bit more vertically. We are letting our minds mask what JK is really trying to say. What would we be thinking looking at The statue of David if we were in ill health? Something like " i could/should look like that? I wonder what it would take. What was different in our lives causing such a different expression of humanity?"

Nakedness to the environment. Completely different diet. A lot more daily physical activity. Better genes? What is the Factor X that synthesizes all of this and with polar bears swimming 9 days? Certainly survival of the fittest, procreation, relentless adaptation or death. Complex and unpredictable nature, night and day, four seasons, weather, climate change, geological change, the relentless arrow of time.

Or is it something really simple and just hiding in the open?

### Trolando — 2012-04-20T16:24:57+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-the-h-is-factor-x-post-your-guesses.42/post-17022>

Well, maybe first or second grade Chem classes include the periodic table.

Maybe they've done basic experimentation with various compounds, just for fun.

Copper comes to mind, lots of fun colors to play with, just to get the hang of playing with chemicals in a slightly controlled way.

Still, as far as I'm aware of the factor X hints, it involves something "new". Something that wasn't available before, but was available after. Either due to the organism exhibiting different behavior for a reason (for example, nasty animals driving us out of the water) increasing the availability of the material, or a nasty event or chain-of-events modifying our environment. For example, a vulcano eruption greatly increases certain compounds in the air/environment. Ash... sulphur... darkness particles... things like that. Oh, except for darkness particles, because they only exist in Discworld. Duh.

etc etc etc

This would select for cold, since the ocean is cold.

And we stand on soil everyday, which has minerals in it.

speaking of taking tests - I had an instructor in college who told us - its cheating when you look on someone else's paper/test - but its research when you get up and walk around...so for one of the test - as a class we assigned everyone 1 question - and each of us answered the same question on every one's test - we'd finish the question - get up, move to the next seat - until our instructor asked us what we were doing - we told him research... the entire class got an A for that test...

This is also simple, i just love simple by the way. Just a couple of years ago we found apes that adapted to extreme winters by gathering around streams. Since food during the winter is hard for them to get they found that open water contained life. In a short period of time they started to turn rocks to get small waterbased insects, next up they learned to dive and catch even more nice stuff. Today they even dive for fun. Evolution in constant work.

//Peter

Amazing, in a field that supposedly teaches and allows one to think.

### Birdy — 2012-04-20T17:44:19+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-the-h-is-factor-x-post-your-guesses.42/post-17078>

I am certain I know factor X , and have known for quite some time, and am probably already patterning my activities around it. I am just getting hung up on this logical game. Once I attended a seminar and there was this word puzzle. It was a creativity thing, were we were supposed to think outside the box and all that.... Anyway, I glanced at the paper and blurted out the answer, just as a joke really. I was right. The instructor was annoyed cause she thought I had seen it in another seminar and I just wrecked it for her, and the students kept asking, "How did you DO that??" Truth is, I don't know, but it happens alot. Dr. K, I think posted a link recently about people using their intuition. You could tell the author didn't have any. People who say you can't rely on intuition don't have any, and for them, it is probably a really good idea that they not rely on it.

### villjamur_stevenson — 2012-04-20T18:05:17+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-the-h-is-factor-x-post-your-guesses.42/post-17100>

> Human fossils and the genetics of extant human populations indicate that living people derive primarily from an African population that lived within the last 200,000 years. Yet it was only ≈50,000 years ago that the descendants of this population spread to Eurasia, where they swamped or replaced the Neanderthals and other nonmodern Eurasians. Based on archaeological observations, the most plausible hypothesis for the delay is that Africans and Eurasians were behaviorally similar until 50,000 years ago, and it was only at this time that Africans developed a behavioral advantage. The archaeological findings come primarily from South Africa, where they suggest that the **advantage involved much more effective use of coastal resources.** Until now, the evidence has come mostly from deeply stratified caves on the south (Indian Ocean) coast. Here, we summarize results from recent excavations at Ysterfontein 1, a deeply stratified shelter in a contrasting environment on the west (Atlantic) coast. The Ysterfontein 1 samples of human food debris must be enlarged for a full comparison to samples from other relevant sites, but they already corroborate two inferences drawn from south coast sites: (i) coastal foragers before 50,000 years ago did not fish routinely, probably for lack of appropriate technology, and (ii) they collected tortoises and shellfish less intensively than later people, probably because their populations were smaller.

From: [The Ysterfontein 1 Middle Stone Age site, South Africa, and early human exploitation of coastal resources](http://http://ukpmc.ac.uk/articles/PMC395859/reload=0;jsessionid=9HHsQybkWrjwoe5XJUt7.6)

Michael Phelps didn't become cold adapted and then just start swimming.

Swimming lead to Cold Adaptation. Same process, just 50,000 years ago.

### AKMan — 2012-04-20T18:12:09+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-the-h-is-factor-x-post-your-guesses.42/post-17105>

> **MamaGrok;17166 wrote:** It's been obvious for a long time that this part is dinosaurs. What next? (KT)

You know, what got me when I went to the Museum of Natural History as a youngster, was the huge plants and bugs. Foot long cockroaches, 30 foot tall ferns. It was explained that it was because 'back then' the atmosphere was like a green house, very rich in oxygen. The young-earth people like to say that when the world was created, it was surrounded by a wall of water which filtered the sunlight and increased oxygen--their explanation of the big bugs in the fossil record. I also remember seeing a guy, who heard all that, and grew 30 foot tomatoe vines with tomatoes the size of basketballs (I maybe exagerating a bit) in a hyperbaric chamber. But my vote for factor X is still tied to oxygen.

**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-the-h-is-factor-x-post-your-guesses.42/post-17174>

I know this was turned down several pages back in the thread, but it does appear that the

[Toba catastrophe](http://http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Toba_catastrophe_theory) forced early humans to turn to the ocean for food - which led to cold adaptation. This may not be THE bottleneck, but it certainly was a major bottleneck - since it severely reduced the population and forced humans to fish.

### Doug — 2012-04-21T03:21:08+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-the-h-is-factor-x-post-your-guesses.42/post-17290>

> **Trolando;17311 wrote:** But the question is what it would do, why it would be useful, what it would add if you ingest it or put it on your skin or something like that. At least, I still expect it to be something that has a biological effect. So how would clay have a biological effect? Or asphalt.

Honestly, my brain just aint that big to figure it out on my own (that's an indirect compliment to JK), therefore i am using the hints, my own knowledge, and google&wiki to connect the dots.

Here is an idea i was pursuing with the clay hypothesis before the infamous "nope" (similar.to trimp's "you're fired")......

... Bentonite clays contain montmorillonite which can carry RNA on micelles, and in acidic conditions incorporate inside the resulting vesicles & promote cell growth and division without biochemical machinery....thus low energy process that helps speed up epigenetics when low temps are trying to slow down reactions.

### AndrÃ©s — 2012-04-21T03:24:13+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-the-h-is-factor-x-post-your-guesses.42/post-17292>

After the K/T event it must have been quite dark and cold, so in this case eutherian cold adapted would be efficient on their use of the few vitamin D available given the almost complete shutdown of UVB, as Dr. Kruse has pointed out elsewhere (enhanced affinity of VDR, I think). Also, [low vitamin D levels seems to upregulate UCP1 expression](http://http://ajpendo.physiology.org/content/296/4/E820.full). The only thing that doesn't match is that vitamin D is almost the only thing Dr. Kruse wants us to supplement after being cold adapted, but I suppose that there is always a frigid enough temperature to _compensate_ for a high level of vitamin D . So, vitamin D is my second guess, although it is not the reason (if I have grasped it correctly) to the ancient pathway being still active (BAT, sympathetic nervous, noradrenaline release and so on) in newborns. Nevertheless it is coherent with MJ's shots .

### Doug — 2012-04-21T03:59:34+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-the-h-is-factor-x-post-your-guesses.42/post-17309>

> **Doug;17360 wrote:** Honestly, my brain just aint that big to figure it out on my own (that's an indirect compliment to JK), therefore i am using the hints, my own knowledge, and google&wiki to connect the dots.
>

>

Bentonite clays contain montmorillonite which can carry RNA on micelles, and in acidic conditions incorporate inside the resulting vesicles & promote cell growth and division without biochemical machinery....thus low energy process that helps speed up epigenetics when low temps are trying to slow down reactions.

More ideas...but irrelavant now...

Graphite & Clay

first, from your hints... -it's found in 2nd and 3rd grader's pencils, with the symbols HB -grounding can "lead" us to it....("lead" being a play on words which is confused for graphite in pencils) -we walk on it everyday -sleep on slate reference -wines from specific regions with bentonite!!! clays, or use bentonite in fermentation process. -many other more deep hints

thoughts on where you go from here...

1) if graphite is included: may help with signaling within the human body, specifically due to with aromatic ability, however only conducts well within the tightly bound planes, not across them. I'm curious if this aids in leptin's ability to be an electron transfer counter, to aid in Cold receptors signaling, or aid in cell signaling combined with omega3s & lower activation energy. (Note: The acoustic and thermal properties of graphite are highly anisotropic.... Anisotropy is the property of being "directionally" dependent)

2) clay: looking up Medicinal Clay on Wikipedia there are tons of applications, but no known explanations....I think you know why...I don't yet but my guess so far is: - Montmorillonite the main constituent of the volcanic ash weathering product, bentonite. - Montmorillonite is also known to cause micelles (lipid spheres) to assemble together into vesicles. These are structures that resemble cell membranes on many cells. It can also help nucleotides to assemble into RNA which will end up inside the vesicles and, under the right conditions, will replicate themselves. This process may have led to the origin of life on Earth. -(Note: I have also heard some pregnant mothers crave dirt or clay and some have eaten it during their pregnancy)

3) clay: depending on the cation exchange capacity, reacts with byproducts of metabolic reactions... maybe absorbs H+, NAD/NADH ions to free up leptin or help lepton count elections....or maybe contributes to a reaction? just starting on this path

4) clay: just read a study that states the following... "During mechanistic studies of lauryl nicotinate, we discovered that niacin causes the release of leptin and downstream signaling of leptin has profound effects on epidermal renewal, wound healing and hair follicle biology in skin".....another rabbit hole to go down.

“NAD in Skin: Therapeutic Approaches for Niacin” Claudia A. Benavente, Myron K. Jacobson and Elaine L. Jacobson)

5) the processes above may also tie into the ultimate goal of becoming cold resistant by creating anti-freeze like a Canadian frog you mentioned in interview. One idea is during Lipolysis fat (triacylglycerol) is cleaved to provide 3 fatty acid chains and 1 glycerol molecule....does this glycerol molecule act like antifreeze if not wasted during hibernation? if so it should mix with water in cytosal to help lower the freezing point to protect cell from formation of ice crystals.

### AndrÃ©s — 2012-04-21T14:56:34+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-the-h-is-factor-x-post-your-guesses.42/post-17353>

Interestingly, [it is retinoic acid (vitamin A) which seems to activate UCP1 transcription](http://http://m.jbc.org/content/270/10/5666.long) (protein that drives uncoupling of the mitochondria from ATP production, that is, direct thermogenesis), but it seems that we may rely on a paleolithic diet to get it (I eat a minimum of two eggs a day, butter every day, beef liver once every two weeks, mussels at least once a week). Perhaps we are not going to guess correctly Factor X, but we are learning a lot of curious facts about our body . Nevertheless I haven't been centering my reading on hormones but in mitochondria, so I will have to go back to Dr. Kruse's old blog posts. (Note: The acoustic and thermal properties of graphite are highly anisotropic.... Anisotropy is the property of being "directionally" dependent)
>

These are structures that resemble cell membranes on many cells. It can also help nucleotides to assemble into RNA which will end up inside the vesicles and, under the right conditions, will replicate themselves. This process may have led to the origin of life on Earth. -(Note: I have also heard some pregnant mothers crave dirt or clay and some have eaten it during their pregnancy)

### chocolate — 2012-04-21T18:36:08+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-the-h-is-factor-x-post-your-guesses.42/post-17446>

[http://phys.org/news162738601.html](http://http://phys.org/news162738601.html)

Ancient volcano may have caused mass extinction

May 28, 2009

A previously unknown giant volcanic eruption that led to global mass extinction 260million years ago has been uncovered by scientists at the University of Leeds.

"When fast flowing, low viscosity magma meets shallow sea it's like throwing water into a chip pan - there's spectacular explosion producing gigantic clouds of steam," explains Professor Paul Wignall, a palaeontologist at the University of Leeds, and the lead author of the paper.

**The injection of sulphur dioxide into the atmosphere would have lead to massive cloud formation spreading around the world - cooling the planet and ultimately resulting in a torrent of acid rain. Scientists estimate from the fossil record that the environmental disaster happened at the start of the eruption.

"The abrupt extinction of marine life we can clearly see in the fossil record firmly links giant volcanic eruptions with global environmental catastrophe, a correlation that has often been controversial," adds Professor Wignall.

Previous studies have linked increased carbon dioxide produced by volcanic eruptions with mass extinctions. However, because of the very long term warming effect that occurs with increased atmospheric carbon dioxide (as we see with current climate change) the causal link between global environmental changes and volcanic eruptions has been hard to confirm.**

The paper 'Precise coincidence of explosive volcanism, mass extinction and carbon isotope fluctuations in the Middle Permian of China' is published in Science.

Source: University of Leeds

### Iceman — 2012-04-22T15:02:07+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-the-h-is-factor-x-post-your-guesses.42/post-17689>

Was stuck awake last night. Was one of the wife's 2 nights where we leave the lights on (still trying to get the point across that in evolution, there is no room for mediocre.) JK mentions that factor x is two letters. After the last hint my guess would be 02. I went back and reread another clue that fascinated me. It was the chicken article. [http://www.sciencedaily.com/releases/2012/02/120229091844.htm](http://http://www.sciencedaily.com/releases/2012/02/120229091844.htm) it was about how domestication was a massive "bottleneck" in the evolution of chickens. Methylation is a chemical alteration of a DNA molecule that affects its expression. Jack says that our biochemistry is rewireable. As people have domesticated themselves becoming warm adapted we are "evolving." Our neolithic disease process is a result of epigenetic switches getting turned on and off due to domestication. Grains/farming/agriculture is the biggest dumb mistake mankind has ever done in that it is speeding up our evolution in a bad way. You always hear that a generation ago, diabetes was a disease of the elderly, yet now we see it in teens. Heart disease in kids.... We are constantly in an "ON" summer state. We are overclocking our engines and burning them out quickly. Don't we consume less 02 in sleep? Are we consuming more 02 being awake ALL the time? Does running and doing WODs burn out our telomeres because of increased oxygen metabolism? Is this why our Biologic ages are so much worse than our chronological ages because we are burning out our bodies more and more and then passing these epigenetics down to future generations? JK is taking advantage of the methylation process. Stress induced methylation. The cold, decreased 02 ( he said higher elevations are good) are turning on epigenetic factors that make us the opposite of our warm, fat, increased aging, grains eating counterparts. We are splitting the evolution process here and now with a single thought. These are genetic adaptations that get switched in you today and hopefully in our subsequent generations. Evolution has no place for mediocre. Right now, making a single mistake and not being as optimal as you can is decreasing your life span as you turn on and off the epigenetic switches to aging and disease. We sleep, we cold, we eat fat ( burn sugar you burn out your engine) we slow down need for 02 ( Wim holds his breath for 6 minutes), we are walking suspended animations, stronger, faster, less disease, live 150 years or longer and all the while passing to future generations. Or you could warm, fat, burn sugar, burn 02, increase disease, decrease life span as our modern drugs and medicine keep you alive but miserable, and pass this on to our kids.

### AndrÃ©s — 2012-04-22T15:11:49+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-the-h-is-factor-x-post-your-guesses.42/post-17691>

I don't know how it ties with everything else, but one thing that fat metabolism gets in respect to carbohydrate one is a lower [respiratory quotient](http://http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Respiratory_quotient) (precisely carbo_hydrates_ have as much oxygen themselves to give rise to the water part of the reaction and only need as much oxygen as to produce the CO2 part) . Since breath is driven by CO2 blood level and not by O2 one, this should give rise to [lower O2 blood levels](http://http://www.springerlink.com/content/f13g65x33h421x85/), if I am not misguided. Although Factor X is not hypoxia, I would bet it has to be related to this lower level of oxygen in blood.

On the other hand, [hypothermia seems to increase O2 blood levels (page 30) due to a shift to the left in the oxyhemoglobin curve (page 28)](http://https://docs.google.com/viewer?a=v&q=cache:QcO94UmsMeIJ:www.cardionursing.com/pdfs/Ventilation-Perfusion-Diffussion-and-More.pdf+&hl=es&pid=bl&srcid=ADGEESir-mMIVfozukdPERQYDiuIeryrbQP_1QnkSAHFx5UcBk6v-4l0JUCM0za5ecbE5lucG0aeE5tyWu_O8VmZgOGaMtbh8Lr_GQdunOI2HFdCwqOgKSH5c_UtPPLrRTVUmrtVXyKV&sig=AHIEtbScW_IyfGujcBmPoW1-cSu5LNtMzA). It keeps looking like cold and slight hypoxia go hand in hand.

### Trolando — 2012-04-22T16:26:49+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-the-h-is-factor-x-post-your-guesses.42/post-17717>

> **Jack Kruse;17781 wrote:** No.....but a sped up epigenetics is the reason why you have an organ in your head that allows you to change your environment for the good or for the bad......modern humans only focus on thoughts and never qualify how they affect their biology. That is why a neolithic thought can subjugate a paleolithic gene and lead to disease.

Apparently hypnosis can cause biological changes this way. Some even claim they can changes hormone levels using hypnosis.

I do not know whether this is true, but I can see at least two mechanisms:

1) Thinking modifies behavior which in turn affects biology.

2) Thinking trains neural networks which in turn affects biology.

In hypnosis, it is sometimes assumed that you have an unconscious that knows or is capable of certain things that your conscious doesn't, especially how to control certain biological processes. At the very least, muscle control is tied to this, but I'm unsure if epigenetics, hormone balances, etc, can also be controlled this way. You can influence stress levels in a subject, but whether this controls their perception of stress or really changes their cortisol levels is something I do not know.

It depends on whether certain neural networks that you can (un)consciously control connect to those reponsible for hormone management...

Also, hypnosis is not mysterious, mystical, or unnatural. Many animals can be easily hypnotized. It is an automatic reaction to certain natural threats.

Species using hemocyanin for oxygen transportation are commonly crustaceans living in cold environments with low oxygen pressure. Under these circumstances hemoglobin oxygen transportation is less efficient than hemocyanin oxygen transportation.

(from Wikipedia)

Like the Horseshoe crab... (which used to fascinate and kinda scare me since I saw them on a 2nd grade field trip to the beach- the long island meet up thread got me thinking about them)

Who is also found to have a receptor for short neuropeptide F's, which are linked to ucp1 in this paper:

[http://www.sdbonline.org/fly/hjmuller/neuropep-f2.htm](http://http://www.sdbonline.org/fly/hjmuller/neuropep-f2.htm)

This stuff most likely goes back to the first animals that had nervous/endocrine systems...

### jeanie@truelongmont.com — 2012-04-23T22:05:04+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-the-h-is-factor-x-post-your-guesses.42/post-18157>

Plants utilize the green pigment chlorophyll to absorb energy from sunlight to breakdown carbon dioxide into sugars. This is well known, and taught in probably every elementary school across the world. What is not as well known, is that the dark colored pigment called melanin found in the skin of humans and other mammals can absorb various types of electromagnetic radiation. In fact, melanin appears capable of absorbing a much broader spectrum of such radiation than chlorophyll. This was proven when scientists noticed various fungi growing in radioactive environments actually seemed to be thriving. Testing of these fungi proved the melanin producing their dark color was absorbing gamma radiation and converting it to energy. The article, "Ionizing Radiation Changes the Electronic Properties of Melanin and Enhances the Growth of Melanized Fungi" published by Ekaterina Dadachova reviews this discovery.

### villjamur_stevenson — 2012-04-24T18:49:42+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-the-h-is-factor-x-post-your-guesses.42/post-18397>

In researching Factor X guesses, I found this free article, in case anyone else wants to read it:

[http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC3268579/?tool=pubmed](http://http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC3268579/?tool=pubmed)

_

> Abstract
>
>
>
> The prevalence of obesity continues to increase particularly in developed countries. To establish the primary mechanisms involved, relevant animal models which track the developmental pathway to obesity are required. This need is emphasized by the substantial rise in the number of overweight and obese children, of which a majority will remain obese through adulthood. The past half century has been accompanied with unprecedented transitions in our lifestyle. Each of these changes substantially contributes to enhancing our capacity to store energy into adipose tissues. The complex etiology of adiposity is critical as a majority of models investigating obesity utilize a simplistic high-fat/low-carbohydrate diet, fed over a short time period to comparatively young inbred animals maintained in fixed environment. The natural history of obesity is much more complex involving many other mechanisms and this type of challenge may not be the optimal experimental intervention. Such processes include changes in adipose tissue composition with time and the transition from brown to white adipose tissue. **Brown adipose tissue, due its unique ability to rapidly produce large amounts of heat could have a pivotal role in energy balance and is under epigenetic regulation mediated by the histone H3k9-specific demethylase Jhdma2a.** Furthermore, day length has a potential role in determining endocrine and metabolic responses in brown fat. The potential to utilize novel models and interventions across a range of animal species in adipose tissue development may finally start to yield sustainable strategies by which excess fat mass can, at last, be avoided in humans.

_

Should egg eating be seasonal?

Right now, making a single mistake and not being as optimal as you can is decreasing your life span as you turn on and off the epigenetic switches to aging and disease. We sleep, we cold, we eat fat ( burn sugar you burn out your engine) we slow down need for 02 ( Wim holds his breath for 6 minutes), we are walking suspended animations, stronger, faster, less disease, live 150 years or longer and all the while passing to future generations. Or you could warm, fat, burn sugar, burn 02, increase disease, decrease life span as our modern drugs and medicine keep you alive but miserable, and pass this on to our kids.

You just blew my mind. This is an epic thread. I too recall reading about yogis and eastern thought and metabolism.... A fast metabolism just accelerates your death. As an obese woman I've often thought to myself, I want a fast metabolism, so I can lose this weight (calories in calories out) and then later I want a slooooow one so I can live a long life. ### pthomaslandry@gmail.com — 2012-04-24T22:53:13+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-the-h-is-factor-x-post-your-guesses.42/post-18448>

Norwegian researchers at the University of TromsÃ¸ have shown that some Arctic animals (ptarmigan, reindeer) show circadian rhythms only in the parts of the year that have daily sunrises and sunsets. In one study of reindeer, animals at 70 degrees North showed circadian rhythms in the autumn, winter, and spring, but not in the summer. Reindeer at 78 degrees North showed such rhythms only autumn and spring. The researchers suspect that other Arctic animals as well may not show circadian rhythms in the constant light of summer and the constant dark of winter.\[19\]\[20\]

Wikipedia

The Hayden planetarium is at the American museum of natural history.

I'm starting to think this has more to do with the bodies on which we are riding through and interacting with in space. Kids learn about the solar system in 2nd/3rd grade, it's under our noses and controls our natural environment and all of its cycles.

I kept thinking, what set and keeps all of this spinning in motion... what controls the frequency, what sets our clocks? Light, gravity, volcanic events, tides, meteors, ice ages, even the food that's available to us. It's our spaceship, and the path it takes.

Earth.

SS?

Solar system?

My heart was broken once when this poor mommy chicken had to abandon a makeshift nest because of a freak snowfall. All night she made it almost and then had to hike away before the snow got too deep. I think it broke her little chicken spirit some. She was one of those hens that the slut chickens would run and lay eggs in her nest while she was out and she had to sit on all of the eggs, and herd around 14 bitties that weren't even hers. Anyway, they reproduce as long as there is food. Consequences of free unfettered.

### Melinda — 2012-04-25T01:02:53+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-the-h-is-factor-x-post-your-guesses.42/post-18504>

Atmospheric concentrations of CO2 vary annually, rising in the fall as shortening days stop photosynthesis. I wonder if we can sense this rising CO2 and eat more carbs and fatten and prepare for winter. The total ppm of CO2 has been rising over the last century and more steeply recently. It has done that in lock step with rising temperatures in the past until an abrupt drop into an ice age, over and over again. This latest rise mirrors the rise in the human obesity epidemic. What if we as a species can account for the increased atmospheric CO2 levels and are fattening up for the next ice age, epigenetically passing easy fattening on to future generations that are going to need it? Factor X is atmospheric levels of Carbon Dioxide.

**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-the-h-is-factor-x-post-your-guesses.42/post-18665>

> **chocolate;18560 wrote:** Chickens laying eggs is food based. If you feed them year around, they lay year round. My heart was broken once when this poor mommy chicken had to abandon a makeshift nest because of a freak snowfall. All night she made it almost and then had to hike away before the snow got too deep. I think it broke her little chicken spirit some. She was one of those hens that the slut chickens would run and lay eggs in her nest while she was out and she had to sit on all of the eggs, and herd around 14 bitties that weren't even hers. Anyway, they reproduce as long as there is food. Consequences of free unfettered.

Chickens do not lay year round if they receive no supplemental light whether you feed them or not. If you let your laying hens live with the circadian cycle and free range them they will live twice as long as hens in year round production. I have Americauna hens that just started their sixth year of laying. Six years is unheard of in the egg industry. What's so funny is I let my chickens live a healthy circadian life and didn't think that applied to humans. Now I know different.

### prAna303 — 2012-04-25T21:46:49+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-the-h-is-factor-x-post-your-guesses.42/post-18810>

More brainfood...

> If some clock-less individuals managed to survive and reproduce every year, and if it was engineered (genetically?) in such a way that the progeny were also clock-less, would such a population manage to establish itself in the wild? What alternative mechanisms it would evolve to decrease risk from predation? Would other correlated aspects of timing (e.g., photoperiodism, developmental timing, ultradian rhythms) additionally reduce their fitness? If we are removing a mechanism that was inherited for billions of years, why would we expect that an alternative mechanism would exist to protect the animals in the absence of the clock?

[http://blogs.scientificamerican.com/a-blog-around-the-clock/2012/04/17/clock-evolution/](http://http://blogs.scientificamerican.com/a-blog-around-the-clock/2012/04/17/clock-evolution/)

//Peter

### phillipsgeorge@hotmail.com — 2012-04-27T01:28:18+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-the-h-is-factor-x-post-your-guesses.42/post-19178>

> our perspective (point of reference) is minute, compared to the cycles of the earth

This is our problem: we think the world **as it is now** is the one that was created for us.

"The onset of the deep cold of prior glaciations would cripple agriculture and cause mass starvation worldwide. No doubt, civilization as we know it would be greatly altered, if not lost."

[http://westinstenv.org/wp-content/postimage/Five\_Interglacials.jpg](http://http://westinstenv.org/wp-content/postimage/Five_Interglacials.jpg)

"cores have revealed that global climate—long thought to change only very gradually—can shift with frightening speed, in some cases in a matter of years. As this graph shows, one such jump occurred about 12,000 years ago, as the last glacial period (the Pleistocene) was giving way to our current warm "interglacial" period (the Holocene). Suddenly, possibly in less than five years, average temperatures, which were slightly cooler than today's, plunged by about 27Â°F, returning the world to near-glacial conditions. The Younger Dryas, as this freak period is known, lasted about 1,300 years before it returned—just as abruptly—to the temperatures typical of the period immediately preceding it.'

Handy to have some quick reacting ubergenes!

### villjamur_stevenson — 2012-04-27T15:42:31+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-the-h-is-factor-x-post-your-guesses.42/post-19303>

Through diversity of organisms. Throw a massive monkey wrench in the system, and you might wipe out entire families of organisms, but some will survive. At the very least we can count on bacteria and viruses to survive and start over at square one.

### QuantumRadical — 2012-04-27T16:37:57+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-the-h-is-factor-x-post-your-guesses.42/post-19319>

> **villjamur\_stevenson;19376 wrote:** Through diversity of organisms. Throw a massive monkey wrench in the system, and you might wipe out entire families of organisms, but some will survive. At the very least we can count on bacteria and viruses to survive and start over at square one.

I think this is right, but it doesn't give Mother Nature enough credit. The real power of a Quantum Physical world--and biology and evolution are yoked to QP implications--is that the tradespace is whatever is possible. So, diversity is but one dimension of the multi-dimensional tradespace. Another could be that some forms of life are adaptable across the extremes. Or, some forms of life could become smart enough to change the environmental factors in their favor (mankind, for example). So, that is at least a three-dimensional tradespace--diversity across organisms, diversity of adaptation within a species across environmental factors, and diversity in the ability to actively change the natural environment. Nature selects for survivability, always! I am sure there are other dimensions possible--maybe silicon-based lifeforms rather than carbon. The universe and Mother Nature love complexity.

### pthomaslandry@gmail.com — 2012-04-27T18:14:58+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-the-h-is-factor-x-post-your-guesses.42/post-19353>

Back to those monks... (or where they lived)

What causes ice ages?

Why only topical ice?

Plate tectonics seem to be the prevailing accepted theory...

In addition, the collision of India and Asia led to the uplift of the Tibetan Plateau and the Himalayas. While topography may not be enough to explain the cooling trends, another mechanism may account for changing climate. The uplift may have caused both an increase in the global rate of chemical erosion, as well as erode fresh minerals that are rapidly transported to lower elevations, which are warmer and moister and allow chemical weathering to happen more efficiently. Through these mechanisms, then, it has been hypothesized that the tectonically driven uplift of the Tibetan Plateau and the Himalayas is the prime cause of the post-Eocene cooling trend.

[http://www.pbs.org/wgbh/nova/earth/cause-ice-age.html](http://http://www.pbs.org/wgbh/nova/earth/cause-ice-age.html)

What is released from underwater vents?

Back to sulfur?

So if we adapt, we survive. If we can adapt massively and quickly, we survive massive and quick changes. If the world is going to plummet into another ice age in 5 years, that rules out (I think) the ability to think our way out of it, so it must be within us.

Now where I live, the dead of winter can be 0 degrees C and the height of summer can be 28 degrees C. That's not a 37 degree drop in temperature, but it's still a pretty massive swing and it occurs within 6 months. It is, however, gradual. But if I yoked my survival to those outdoor temps then I'd be on the road to being prepared if it dropped another 10 degrees all of a sudden.

This is that binary - now not dormant - pathway. But this is what we already know, not factor X.

[http://pasteur.crg.es/portal/page/portal/Internet/06\_NOTICIAS/HIDE-PRESSRELEASES/The%20molecular%20mechanism%20that%20links%20temperature%20with%20sex%20determination%20in%20some%20species%20has%20been%20found](http://http://pasteur.crg.es/portal/page/portal/Internet/06_NOTICIAS/HIDE-PRESSRELEASES/The%20molecular%20mechanism%20that%20links%20temperature%20with%20sex%20determination%20in%20some%20species%20has%20been%20found)

Yes, though the studies I've been looking at indicate faster repair, and gene expression at higher rather than lower temperatures. Doesn't point to mechanism.

This again indicates that the natural state is one of cold, and the switches to adapt epigenetically get triggered when exposed to heat. (and different diet and light, and probably some other factor(s--x?))

With CT we are turning off all the epigenetic switches we shouldn't have had on in the first place.

Our use of fire/heat may have allowed for faster gene expression while we were still spending a lot of time diving for shellfish during the bottleneck. Also this might explain the change in teeth and larger brain.

Factor x- fire?

In England we term a design suitable for a challenging environment as being "built like a brick sh\*t house"

### QuantumRadical — 2012-04-28T18:09:17+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-the-h-is-factor-x-post-your-guesses.42/post-19585>

> **Tom;19630 wrote:** [http://pasteur.crg.es/portal/page/portal/Internet/06\_NOTICIAS/HIDE-PRESSRELEASES/The%20molecular%20mechanism%20that%20links%20temperature%20with%20sex%20determination%20in%20some%20species%20has%20been%20found](http://http://pasteur.crg.es/portal/page/portal/Internet/06_NOTICIAS/HIDE-PRESSRELEASES/The%20molecular%20mechanism%20that%20links%20temperature%20with%20sex%20determination%20in%20some%20species%20has%20been%20found)
