### Entelechy — 2012-05-17T21:31:45+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-the-h-is-factor-x-post-your-guesses.42/post-24974>

> **Optimalbound;24921 wrote:** Correct. Normal air has 21% O\[sub\]2\[/sub\] content, while Enriched Air Nitrox, as it is called, typically has either 32% or 36% oxygen content. I am a Nitrox certified scuba diver and my experience with it, apart from the incease in dive time, is that am less tired afterwards. Not sure if it is relevant in ths context, but I thought I'd mention it.

Nitrox is a generic term for diving gas mixes.

You were using a mixture of N2 and O2 gasses. This mixture is not the same a NO, NO2, or N2O (nitric monoxide, nitric dioxide, and nitrous oxide)

NO is unstable, quickly becomes toxic smog(NO2) and causes acute lung damage in doses greater than parts per million.

If you used N2O, aka laughing gas, it would be worse than the worst nitrogen narcosis you've ever experienced. lol

### Optimalbound — 2012-05-17T22:57:36+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-the-h-is-factor-x-post-your-guesses.42/post-25001>

> **Entelechy;25056 wrote:** Nitrox is a generic term for diving gas mixes.

No, Nitrox is a term used _only for the mix of nitrogen + oxygen. (In practice, Nitrox is made by adding oxygen to air.)

Heliox and Trimix are examples of diving gas mixes that are not_ Nitrox.

There is a very strange sense of entitlement happening in this world lately.

If someone can afford something, they probably have put in the work to get there. I can't afford everything, in fact, most of what I can afford is b/c of very good money management skills. Life just hasn't taken me to the point of financial freedom yet. Never would I point my finger at the people who are already there. Good for them and an inspiration for me to work harder and learn more about how to succeed.

If you approach life this way, your cup will always be half empty.

### DarleenMB — 2012-05-18T18:23:44+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-the-h-is-factor-x-post-your-guesses.42/post-25208>

You know, Jack gives and gives and gives. He is a busy neurosurgeon yet he still finds time and generosity of heart to come in here (and on his blog posts) and answer questions, both group questions and individual ones. You show me another physician who would do that. FOR FREE.

I do NOT have a problem with paid memberships. I had originally decided I would just stick with the free one but my curiosity, my biggest failing, got the better of me. I may or may not continue the paid membership after this month. I don't know. It depends on what Jack has in store next. I know I cannot afford to do it indefinitely. But this is all MY CHOICE.

It saddens me to see the complaining going on and the sniping.

Too many people think that if it's on the 'net it's supposed to be free.

I'd remind those of that temperament that there really is no such thing as a free lunch.

Jack, thank you for all you do here. Your efforts are greatly appreciated whether they come free (which is most of it) or one pays for the privilege.

### shilohman — 2012-05-18T19:33:27+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-the-h-is-factor-x-post-your-guesses.42/post-25235>

Think I have figured out Factor-X. If not, I am very close You can figure it out for yourself. Just follow these simple steps:

1\. Read Cold-Thermogenesis-5 and mull over in your mind this quote:

> Since the cell cycle was slowed by cold it sped up epigenetics to compensate for the slower growth.\* That is the basis of FACTOR X.\* It is the most important part of my theory because it is why the human brain was naturally selected for in a mismatched environment.

2\. While you are doing your CT just keep mulling this quote over in your mind and try to fit that in to everything you have heard Dr.

Kruse say/write about Factor-X.

3\. Once you have finished you CT, pour yourself some Bulletproof coffee

4\. Do whatever you do now to warm up from the CT i.e. hot shower, hot tube, etc.

If you do those four steps, then you have figured out/participated in Factor-X. If you are still confused, then think about these

things:

1\. the Chinese concept of ying/yang

2\. the coming and going of the ice ages

3\. why the human brain is so adaptable to its environment and how you just used your brain with Factor-X

Dr. K dropped so many clues in his blogs about Factor-X, I just had to read/search them many times.

Can't wait for his webinar to find out if this is correct or just close.

### jeanie@truelongmont.com — 2012-05-18T20:22:48+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-the-h-is-factor-x-post-your-guesses.42/post-25261>

> **Anna K.;25189 wrote:** this is becoming a bit annoying

Well, I've been on the losing side of "fair" so many times in my life - no way am I going to not take advantage of being able to enjoy the rewards when I can. So I'm a webinaring and consulting. I grew up the fat-kid and I well know what being excluded feels like.

Anna, I know there is going to be some heat for you guys (and if one speaks up, there are more feeling the same way) for feeling this way, but seriously - it makes perfect sense to feel that way! I would be super annoyed in your shoes, and I bet the others on here would too for the most part. Being excluded always sucks. Even if you didnt' care one bit about the topic, It would have to rub you the wrong way.

Feelings are never always wonderful and benevolent and take into account reason. I think it's ok to have AND EXPRESS them. I'm pretty sure that's why the internets have taken over socializing for many. It's easier to type words about feelings than say them. Try it. Try to verbalize to an empty room your feelings. Then let your fingers do the talking. Different paths in the brain. If you get mad on here at those sharing their feelings - time for a bath. It's human nature, ain't gonna change. Fighting it causes a mismatch!!

Jack is the one taking all the risk on Factor-X. I would be very surprised if he didn't realize denying some would cause some animosity. Or at minimum if his wife didn't tell him that.

I'm sure the marketing professionals (that he is paying) told him to "screw those ungrateful people, you can't please everyone and you have to spend marketing capitol on where it will pay off. Those that only use the free stuff will never pay. Focus on those that pay. ROI 101."

Fortunately for me, Jack doesn't seem to listen to the experts, and he's bent over to take abuse and paid for the privilege. I think because he IS benevolent and tries to help people. We will never ever know all his internal reasons, but really when judging others all you can go on is their actions. No way to know what's in someones heart. (unless you get an ekg).

Will all the hype for Factor-X be a boon or a bust for him? Remains to be seen, but it was his idea and his risk to take and his earned reward if it pays off.

If factor X doesn't deliver and his membership peters out and book sales falter, all of this hype could be a negative for him. I'm not going to second guess his reasons, and I don't think he owes me a peek at his business plan. If he did let me consult on it, I would charge him even more $ out of pocket.

So although you are stuck in the VERY SUCKY position of having to wait for the info, while others get it before you - you also don't take the risk of spending all that $ and then feelin like a fool if it doesn't pan out.

Yin/Yang

Ok, so anywhoo - Anne (and other) I'm with ya, IT SUCKS FOR YOU. You have my sympathy and empathy - but I'm an animal and going to enjoy my spoils anyway.

ROI 101. Well said, Cavemam.

I began this trek the day I began LR (02/25/12). Who knew that my hobby would become Evolutionary Molecular Biology in Hominids? My brain has bent and hurt alot over the past three months. Nowadays I wake up before my alarm goes off thinking about BAT and what I can do to make my CT more efficient. Sheesh!

This same coursework, if it were even available at my local college, would cost hundreds of dollars in tuition and texts. Dr. Kruse is making it freely available to anyone who wants to read all the blogs, comments and forum posts. I am proud to say that I can now read scientific abstracts with a reasonable understanding of what's being said and without having to look up every other term. Yay Me!

One of the greatest ideas ever comes from the Bible: "Give a man a fish, you feed him for a day; teach a man to fish, you feed him for a lifetime." Dr. Kruse is teaching us to fish. We've been Noped and encouraged, had arguments and made up, and in the process significantly expanded our knowledge. The bottom line is, all of us will remember this trip twenty years from now, because we’ve had to earn that knowledge just like we earned our grades in school… by doing our homework and connecting those dots.

I am not "entitled" to anything Dr. Kruse chooses to offer. He's done the homework for the past five years - lived it actually - so if he wants me to pay $38 to find out one more piece of the puzzle, that's okay by me. I cannot afford grass-fed, pastured, organic, bulletproof anything. I do what I can on my budget and hope for the best. I robbed Peter to pay Paul to be able to attend this webinar. It's that important to me.

Will knowing what Factor X is rock my world? I don’t know. But LR did and so has CT. I’m going for three in a row.

Just my two cents…

Elin

### freezin4areason — 2012-05-19T22:33:43+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-the-h-is-factor-x-post-your-guesses.42/post-25564>

I didn't watch the webinar (I'm considering buying the month membership for DL), so I went to the FTA blog.

I read what Richard wrote about Factor X; he said Dr. Kruse told him a meteor struck the earth and started an ice age.

Meteors can contain iron.

Dr. Kruse's blog post said that: 1. iron aids bacteria growth when they can get it, 2. people with the hemochromatosis gene can fight off bacteria better because less iron is available for the colonizing bacteria, 3. type I diabetes is probably a mutation that was used to survive cold winters.

So from this I'm guess that factor x is just iron. The meteor introduced a lot of iron into the environment and encouraged bacterial growth, and for humans to survive, they needed a way to store iron from bacteria. At the same time, the meteor kicked up a lot of dust, blocked the sun, and started an ice age. Type I diabetes was selected for so that humans could survive.

What I'm getting from this is that we need to optimize our iron levels. I already knew that high iron is bad because it is a GREAT reducing agent.

Am I close?

### QuantumRadical — 2012-05-20T18:53:10+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-the-h-is-factor-x-post-your-guesses.42/post-25732>

> **Jack Kruse;25761 wrote:** If you dont think it should change your protocols then you missed the point big time.......

It seems much clearer to me now. We have evolved from the mammal line that had it in them (biochemically, DNA, epigenetics, etc.) to adapt very rapidly to very rapid environmental changes. And we still have that "ancient pathway." With the KT event, if your species couldn't adapt like RIGHT NOW, you perished.

Therefore, knowing that we are a species that can adapt fast, and knowing that our lineage had to adapt to a cold and probably hypoxic environment, and knowing that we need to be yoked to the natural circadian rythms, and knowing that cold helps correct our mismatches--opening up the ancient pathway--then we can choose to change our protocols and make those adaptations that result in health and fewer mismatches. We can choose our diet, we can choose hot or cold, we can choose the style and manner of our exercise, and we can choose to live a more natural circadian profile--get better sleep--all resulting in an N=1 member of the species far healthier than the "average" person who is unaware.

So, a ketogenic paleo diet, CT, Circadian sleep cycles yoked to natural light cycles, and lift heavy things once in awhile, goes a long way to being optimally healthy. The right protocol can lead to a positive (from a health perspective) epigenetic change within one's own lifetime. Start now! That is the whole point of evolving from a line of mammals that nature kicked to ground--but they got up immediately and ultimately thrived! Nature forced evolution to speed up!!!!

Same reason dogs--same species--can be so different. They also came from the same "surviving" lines. Environmental pressures cause changes within just a couple of generations.

Flying dinosaurs, who escaped and then had to have it in themselves to rapidly adapt, and the small eutherian mammals that were in hybernation. Keys to the puzzle. Awesome stuff to ponder further.

We can "force" ourselves to revert to a healthier (or not) epigenetic profile especially based on mothers and grandmothers, and that is why the current new young generation is getting sicker because their mothers and grandmothers were trapped/yoked to the environmental pressures leading to neolithic diseases-- artificial lights, artificial food! They were just unaware of even the possibility of mismatches. Nature continues to accelerate evolution. More pieces to the puzzle.

### QuantumRadical — 2012-05-20T19:42:57+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-the-h-is-factor-x-post-your-guesses.42/post-25747>

I just had an interesting thought. All that Nature is looking for to select for survival is a minimum of one (or one pair male/female fertile enough to procreate) N=1 to survive and continue the "adapted" species to the future. The junk DNA is so complex with so many potential variations, that perhaps each of us is a "special case" of epigenetic change based on our own individual environmental pressures. I am an identical twin, but my brother and I still have our differences. Look at Wim Hof. He is certainly his own N=1. Mother Nature's hedge on the future is to always have as many options available as possible because you never know what Black Swan event is coming next or when! The more I get into this evolutionary biology, the more it is simply brilliant. The wider the trade space to select from, the more likely at least one set is adapted/adaptable for the uncertain future. Life goes on--it finds a way!

This is a Borat/Ali G-style question:

mammals that survived KT, did so because they were cold adapted. Cold adaptation was built into their genes. We, at the end of a long line, are their offspring. As such, we are cold adapted, by default. Therefore, I should be able to run into the Baring Sea for a swim right after stepping of a plane from say Cairo. No?

Perhaps, also, it would be good to have a thread with definitions. What is cold adapted, for example.

Cold adapted animals are those with a lot of insulating blubber.

Cold adapted human members of this forum are those without insulating blubber, or at least those aiming to shed it.

Then the animal/ human distinction is very neolithic. We are all mammals at heart. So what is cold adapted?

### Entelechy — 2012-05-21T03:33:25+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-the-h-is-factor-x-post-your-guesses.42/post-25861>

not to detract from the intent of the message, but this is not a fair comparison in any way.

A single cell to TRex is a way bigger change than little mammal to big mammal.

with this time frame. (4.5 Bya) it's not even a eukaryotic cell, but an ancestor of an ancestor of a cell!

it took 2 billion years to get from bacteria to eukaryote.

one should at least choose a biologically comparable differential to make the point.

[Raptorex kriegsteini](http://http://news.discovery.com/animals/t-rex-dino-tiny.html), 125mya is an ancestor of TRex. (but not as dissimilar as we are from purgatorius)

the very first dinosaurs showed up about 230mya.

TRex appeared on the scene about 85 mya

first mammals showed up 200mya.

pick your point on the scale to pin the tail.

### QuantumRadical — 2012-05-21T07:02:36+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-the-h-is-factor-x-post-your-guesses.42/post-25901>

> **ruby;25946 wrote:** This is a Borat/Ali G-style question:
>
> mammals that survived KT, did so because they were cold adapted. Cold adaptation was built into their genes. We, at the end of a long line, are their offspring. As such, we are cold adapted, by default. Therefore, I should be able to run into the Baring Sea for a swim right after stepping of a plane from say Cairo. No?
>
> Perhaps, also, it would be good to have a thread with definitions. What is cold adapted, for example.
>
> Cold adapted animals are those with a lot of insulating blubber.
>
> Cold adapted human members of this forum are those without insulating blubber, or at least those aiming to shed it.
>
> Then the animal/ human distinction is very neolithic. We are all mammals at heart. So what is cold adapted?

Ruby, i think the mammals that survived may or may not have been cold adapted, but instead, cold adaptable, and quickly. A nuance, yes, but I think what is the more important concept is the ability to quickly adapt to a very rapidly changing environment. The specific adaptation was certainly into a cold environment, and those mammals that adapted into COLD WATER developed blubber, and those that adapted to a cold land environment didn't adapt via a blubber path--they leveraged a completely different metabolic pathway. Apples and oranges. Just goes to show the diversity of possible adaptations from just a single event. You adapt based on your particular circumstances at the time in a manner that is selectable for survival at that time. At the next frame in the picture show, what worked before may turn into a liability.

### QuantumRadical — 2012-05-21T07:20:46+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-the-h-is-factor-x-post-your-guesses.42/post-25904>

> **Randychaps;25992 wrote:** Ruby, i think the mammals that survived may or may not have been cold adapted, but instead, cold adaptable, and quickly. A nuance, yes, but I think what is the more important concept is the ability to quickly adapt to a very rapidly changing environment. The specific adaptation was certainly into a cold environment, and those mammals that adapted into COLD WATER developed blubber, and those that adapted to a cold land environment didn't adapt via a blubber path--they leveraged a completely different metabolic pathway. Apples and oranges. Just goes to show the diversity of possible adaptations from just a single event. You adapt based on your particular circumstances at the time in a manner that is selectable for survival at that time. At the next frame in the picture show, what worked before may turn into a liability.

Ruby, on the otherhand, nothing says we didn't evolve from a water-based survivor of the KT event. We do have that layer of fat different from other primates. Maybe we are yet another one of those complex matches and expressions of junk DNA. I'll have to think about this more.

### neaderthal — 2012-05-21T08:05:52+0300

> **Jack Kruse;25972 wrote:** yeah it is.......because you missed the point.......time. 4.5 billion yrs compared to 67 million yrs is not even close. Moreover we came from animals that survived the KT event and not those before it and we know from the fossil record they were pretty small and simple. We are not simple at all. In fact what is in our skulls alone compared to our nearest relative is nothing short of shocking. Compared to a T Rex it is even more amazing. Their brain was the size of a walnut. I guess my sense of relativity in biologic terms is quite different than yours.

I agree with Entelechy regarding the lack of equivalency between the comparisons b/c a rodent is much more similar to a human in terms of complexity than T. Rex is to a single cell; so, one should expect the number of years for a rodent to evolve to a human to be significantly shorter.

However, I guess what the take home message is---look how long it took evolution to produce a moron like T. Rex compared with the significantly shorter length of time it took to produce a complex human, capable of debating the legitimacy of this comparison, once the speed of epigenetics increased following the KT event.

At first glance at the comparison, & before I read Entelechy's comments, I had the same thoughts as Entelechy regarding the comparison, but perhaps I'm one of the outliers whose moronic ancestors survived despite adversity

### ruby — 2012-05-21T08:37:24+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-the-h-is-factor-x-post-your-guesses.42/post-25907>

Thanks for commenting Randychaps.

Sorry, did not express myself clearly. yes, I agree that they may not have entered the game already cold adapted (BUT that raises another ugly question - if they were not cold adapted then were they, heaven forbid, warm or even hot adapted?). Chicken or egg....

But, yes, they may have had an inherent ability to adjust quickly and before anybody else.

Jack mentions brain size --hmmm. Just by looking around - size doesn't seem to be everything. Even among people I know, who I presume are all homo sapiens, the same size brains do not produce the same quality output.

For example, Neanderthal brains were bigger but did they serve them well?

The only Neanderthal hybrid I know is myself I am a proud owner of Rhesus negative blood which means that if I mate with a 100% homo sapiens (as defined by Rh+) my body will attempt to destroy the embryo, surely, in an attempt, by none other than Mother Nature, to keep our superior bloodline untainted

So here I am, with big-brained Neanderthal ancestry but asking dumb questions.

Jack says "Moreover we came from animals that survived the KT event and not those before it" -- surely the animals that survived KT came from somewhere. That adaptable ancestor who came equipped with this inherent ability to adapt that served him so well during KT must have had parents.

I hope, his ability to adapt, since it was so advantageous in evolutionary terms, has been preserved and will serve us well in all future events when our little niche gets obliterated. Which is now.

We can "force" ourselves to revert to a healthier (or not) epigenetic profile especially based on mothers and grandmothers, and that is why the current new young generation is getting sicker because their mothers and grandmothers were trapped/yoked to the environmental pressures leading to neolithic diseases-- artificial lights, artificial food! > **Randychaps;25831 wrote: I just had an interesting thought. All that Nature is looking for to select for survival is a minimum of one (or one pair male/female fertile enough to procreate) N=1 to survive and continue the "adapted" species to the future. The junk DNA is so complex with so many potential variations, that perhaps each of us is a "special case" of epigenetic change based on our own individual environmental pressures. I am an identical twin, but my brother and I still have our differences. Look at Wim Hof. He is certainly his own N=1. Mother Nature's hedge on the future is to always have as many options available as possible because you never know what Black Swan event is coming next or when! The more I get into this evolutionary biology, the more it is simply brilliant. The wider the trade space to select from, the more likely at least one set is adapted/adaptable for the uncertain future. Life goes on--it finds a way! I was thinking a lot about these types of thoughts too...I had three siblings that died when they were children of a very rare genetic disorder (similar to a PKU potentially). Genetic counseling showed that my parents had a 1 in 1,000,000 chance of both carrying the recessive genes for this to have occurred. Ridiculous odds, like winning the worst lottery three times over. I now think to myself what purpose could this condition that rendered my brothers and sister helpless have served in an evolutionary way? Does nature just try so many different things so that some will stick to ensure survival? Is this haphazard or brilliant, or both? Factor X tells me it's probably not as haphazard as it looks...

particles are finite, like grains of sand, or atoms.

### chocolate — 2012-05-21T18:48:24+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-the-h-is-factor-x-post-your-guesses.42/post-25999>

You guys are freaking me about about the smell. Our family has a cursed sense of smell and I'm told to be as bad as my grandpa. He used to make my grandmother take other peoples kids outside and hose them off when they visited. She'd make like it was a game. He swore he could smell them. He knocked out the bedroom walls to enlarge his home one night, when he came home and the smell of humanity was too dense. I see people and hold my breath. I stand back several feet and when they come up on me I stop breathing and then take tiny breaths. That forces you to listen to whatever drivel they are emitting. I gag in public restrooms. I can smell when women sit down and straddle the toilet, even to pee. My husband cracks up when I have to use the restroom at Walmart. He figures its fifty fifty I won't be able to hold my breath long enough to fight it. I've run out of the gym gagging when someone with smelly pits started lifting and barfed in a trash can... It horrible, my face turns red and flushes when I try to fight it. My family is always sticking stuff under my nose to see if its fit or foul. But I can turn it off, too. It just takes some mental energy. I just think its a natural sensitivity that went haywire. On the other hand, I must smell about as non threatening as anything... there is no such thing as a vicious animal to me, and my type A makes me at risk for cross infections with animals. Of course, I'm not the Into the Wild type. Is there a positive in this?

### MamaGrok — 2012-05-21T18:54:32+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-the-h-is-factor-x-post-your-guesses.42/post-26001>

chocolate, I just read something about what deficiency or disease or something can cause people to be overly sensitive to smell. It struck me b/c my mother has always been crazy sensitive to smell and I'm sure it's not completely unrelated to her autoimmune disease.

> **Anna K.;25990 wrote:
>
> "The more carbs you eat……the faster the clocks run."

> My previous understanding was that the Inuit diet (mostly fat, some meat, little non-starchy veggies) was the ideal because that is what we are descended from (cold-adapted) and that any intake of carbs was not ideal and would shorten our telomeres. Inuits didn't eat 400 grams of cabs in the summer.
>
>
>
> So if we are cold adapted and taking regular cold baths during the summer, do we HAVE to eat more carbs in the summer, or we simply CAN?
>
> What is optimal for health and longevity? What is the biological sense of carbs in the summer?
>
>
> Almost nowhere on earth is an Inuit level of year-round cold possible today. Even much of Alaska gets fairly warm in the summer. My inclination is that unless we are truly living \*in the cold\* (not just cold for a few minutes a day) day in and day out, all summer long, our carbohydrate intake ought to correspond, UNLESS we still have neolithic disease.

On the other hand, I must smell about as non threatening as anything... there is no such thing as a vicious animal to me, and my type A makes me at risk for cross infections with animals. Of course, I'm not the Into the Wild type. Is there a positive in this?

is there a positive ? absolutely! you have a gift most of us have lost... and you need to learn to embrace it, not turn it off.

Think of how a dog, who's sense of smell is 10000Xs as acute, can "see" with his nose.. picking up the slightest scent miles away. Far more sensitive than your meager abilities..

yet, he will stick that same ultra sensitive nose into a rotting corpse without being 'overpowered' any more than you would be overpowered by the smell of roses, vanilla, or vinegar.

Of course they will all cause olfactory fatigue, but how you choose to define the smell as good or bad... that's entirely choice, unless it's a scent that is acutely toxic, like high levels of ammonia.

if you develop this talent, you could be the worlds greatest wine or "chocolate" taster!

### Jack Kruse — 2012-05-21T19:05:04+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-the-h-is-factor-x-post-your-guesses.42/post-26015>

> **Entelechy;25991 wrote:** Total enthalpy of a system will remain the same, but as organization of matter from chaos occurs as a function of life processes, at a cost of energy differentials, then the entropy gradient must increase to maintain thermodynamic equilibrium....
>
>
>
> or on an even more macro view....life and the expansion of the universe... are one and the same. Therefore,
>
>
>
> does life cause the universe to expand? or is the expansion of the universe the source of life itself?

That is what I thought too but I just found out from a bioastrophysicist that at extreme cold in space close to absolute zero chemistry begins to become very ordered for a time......and then it completely shuts down protons collapse and we get an incredible deep freeze because energy and life all require temperature differentials to work. In absolute zero there is a complete lack of energy. What we do not know is what are the gravitational effects when this happens......or is there a dark matter that draws the disparite masses together to recreate things in a cycle or not. I found it very interesting to say the least. Because if it is a cycle.....as we approach cold life would suspend in animation and then as we contract we would increase energy in the system and eventually as collision happens life would begin once again. No energy or matter would be created or destroyed it would be recreated and we wuld re evolve........like a giant revolving door using the principles of energy transfer and motion.

> **chocolate;26090 wrote:** You guys are freaking me about about the smell. Our family has a cursed sense of smell and I'm told to be as bad as my grandpa. He used to make my grandmother take other peoples kids outside and hose them off when they visited. She'd make like it was a game. He swore he could smell them. He knocked out the bedroom walls to enlarge his home one night, when he came home and the smell of humanity was too dense. I see people and hold my breath. I stand back several feet and when they come up on me I stop breathing and then take tiny breaths. That forces you to listen to whatever drivel they are emitting. I gag in public restrooms. I can smell when women sit down and straddle the toilet, even to pee. My husband cracks up when I have to use the restroom at Walmart. He figures its fifty fifty I won't be able to hold my breath long enough to fight it. I've run out of the gym gagging when someone with smelly pits started lifting and barfed in a trash can... It horrible, my face turns red and flushes when I try to fight it. My family is always sticking stuff under my nose to see if its fit or foul. But I can turn it off, too. It just takes some mental energy. I just think its a natural sensitivity that went haywire. On the other hand, I must smell about as non threatening as anything... there is no such thing as a vicious animal to me, and my type A makes me at risk for cross infections with animals. Of course, I'm not the Into the Wild type. Is there a positive in this?

oh, wow, I'm not nearly as sensitive but feel like as I've been working on my health, this is one of the things happening... very slowly. I used to not be able to smell anything, I even failed a smell test at my nose/ear/throat doctor as a teenager entirely. I could't differentiate sulfur from whatever the heck it was that was supposed to smell "good".

Lately, I can't seem to go into public anymore without holding my breath. I leave the house and the people that pass by me just smell so intense. Perfume, body odor, it doesn't matter. Pollution... terrible. It feels like something is eating away in my airways. A person I was in relationship with a few years ago now has the most horrendous smell to me... I have to hold my breath when we hug.

I live in hippie town and frequent hippie circles (lol, I guess I \*am\* one ) so the problem is contained as most of us don't use products that smell. I also like to spend time with dogs and their smell doesn't bother me a bit. Dogs tend to take positively to me ... I rehab "problem" dogs as a hobby until they're ready to go get another chance at a new family. The other really weird thing is that animals don't appear to smell my human presence. I've had to stop hiking solo in the mountains b/c I kept running into bears, mountain lions, coyotes, you name it. That never happens if I'm with a "smelly" friend.

I am absolutely curious where this is going to go as I continue on my path to health.

### MJ* — 2012-05-21T21:37:25+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-the-h-is-factor-x-post-your-guesses.42/post-26068>

> **Randychaps;26155 wrote:** Is emotional opposition a neolithic thought or good biology? Only time will tell!

Well, if the olfactory concept is indeed correct, it's Neolithic...if Romeo and Juliet (generically speaking any two people from differing backgrounds who want to be together) were attracted by the way they each smelled to ensure better immunity for their offspring, but their parents didn't like where they came from and stopped the union, I'd say that's Neolithic...

I believe this a a fundamental point of Jacks macro hypothesis... the rate of cultural evolution far exceeds epigentic adaptation, and those cultural memes further cause expression of genes, (thought can change DNA)... thereby drastically accelerating the rate of epigentics, and the ensuing biologic mismatches that can't keep up. > **Entelechy;26165 wrote:** I believe this a a fundamental point of Jacks macro hypothesis... the rate of cultural evolution far exceeds epigentic adaptation, and those cultural memes further cause expression of genes, (thought can change DNA)... thereby drastically accelerating the rate of epigentics, and the ensuing biologic mismatches that can't keep up. I definitely get it that we caused the speed to rev up...

Thought leads to changes in DNA...that's what I've been thinking about. As humans we know we can think but what causes adaptations in animals? Environmental pressures, but do animals think? If they don't think what changes their DNA? Adaptations in other creatures and species is something I've been thinking about, things like protective coloration, so amazing to see...they are influenced by environment and serve a purpose or some sort of need the animal has to blend in and stay safe from a predator, to help balance nature. Some adaptations are wild. Have you ever seen the fish that live in the darkest parts of the ocean? Viper fish have developed florescent lures to overcome a lack of light and are used to attract prey...are adaptations purely epigenetic in animals? Can animals problem solve and would that then lead to changes in DNA just at a slower pace, and does that not require some form of thinking? I wasn't sure until I saw this video of a killer whale who "learned" how to hunt differently due to a scarcity of food...she was able to then teach her young and pass on a new practice of luring seals out of save harbor. It is fascinating...\[video\]\[video=youtube\_share;FzHnenjpIQg\]http://www.youtube.com/watch?v=FzHnenjpIQg&feature=related\[/video\]\[/video\] This is not an example of a biologic change but rather a change in behavior, but could this lead to a biologic change over time, maybe to whales that walk, if man was not around to interfere?

Well, I come from selective breeding. My parents were the first not to be paired. I didn't realize it til I was older. I know that in the US, pre Jamestown, a chief paired someone with his wife, they bore a son, and a white woman was kidnapped in a raid and impregnated by that son. The stories got twisted and my brother had a dna test done... sure enough... when I would visit older people of substance when I was younger, they would always do a tortured lineage. It I could never figure out why it mattered. My grandmother was brought home by my grandpa's son and my grandpa, of relative substance, looked her over and made a deal with her dad. She had stepchildren younger than her and don't you know the subsequent wife of the son hated my grandmother. The relationships didn't seem based on affection at all. Apparently my grandmother brought long bones into the mix. It took something to bring the height back. The product of the kidnapped white woman and brokered nooky from the pairing of the sea captain and chief wife was hugely tall and fine boned. And a super linguist, not well liked by his mother. the kidnapped white woman was sent home to her husband preggers and I guess there was some shared custody... I am not surprised they sent her home, if she was related to me. We are all over the map and interbreeding was avoided. And yep, Appalachian.

### ruby — 2012-05-22T02:41:20+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-the-h-is-factor-x-post-your-guesses.42/post-26146>

Originally Posted by Jack Kruse: "cold also stops light from moving at absolute zero.....dead in its tracks. "

erhm, the definition of absolute zero is no movement. At temperatures near 0\*K, nearly all molecular motion ceases. Electromagnetic radiation or light, including the invisible part of the spectrum, is a form of energy emitted and absorbed by charged particles, which exhibits wave-like behavior as it TRAVELS through space. Light is motion. at 0 K there is no motion => no light.

### Jack Kruse — 2012-05-22T03:09:52+0300

### Entelechy — 2012-05-22T04:04:59+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-the-h-is-factor-x-post-your-guesses.42/post-26169>

> **ruby;26237 wrote: Originally Posted by Jack Kruse: "cold also stops light from moving at absolute zero.....dead in its tracks. "
>
> erhm, the definition of absolute zero is no movement. At temperatures near 0\*K, nearly all molecular motion ceases. Electromagnetic radiation or light, including the invisible part of the spectrum, is a form of energy emitted and absorbed by charged particles, which exhibits wave-like behavior as it TRAVELS through space. Light is motion. at 0 K there is no motion => no light.**

**

definitions are important. Temperature is defined as kinetic energy of matter,,,the average velocity of its molecules. the faster they move, the warmer the substance.

At absolute zero molecules have minimal** kinetic energy. The molecules are not motionless, due to Heisenberg's uncertainty principle of quantum mechanics, which entails that the atoms cannot have both a fixed position and zero momentum at the same time.

At this point the matter appears to smear out.

When a light beam/particle/ take your pick is sent through this bose-einstein smear (modified to be translucent) it does slow down due to refraction to an incredible 38mph.

with another trick, we can make it come to a complete stop... if only for a second... before instantly accelerating back to 186kmps

electromagnetic radiation, whether defined as a particle, or wave, is not defined as motion simply because motion is relative.

### jeanie@truelongmont.com — 2012-05-22T04:45:46+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-the-h-is-factor-x-post-your-guesses.42/post-26182>

> **MJ\*;26068 wrote:** This could help explain some of what Jack discussed in terms of the survival of our species through the sense of smell in attraction to others with "different" smells due to differences in gene pool to create more potential genetic possibilities.

For the record, I was spouting my theory on this for years prior to factor x. . but

There is a role for "cultural traditions" even when they seemingly go against good ol fashioned natural selection. Don't dismiss the role of social groups as a necessary component of hominid evolution. It all ties in with ho's, cuckolding and thoughts changing your DNA. Consider the following:

1\. We are driven to mate with good smelling hotties (to us) for the physical advantages they provide our offspring.

2\. The same reasons that make that mate beneficial genetically, may not make that same mate beneficial in the immediate and current environment. In fact, rarely is the one we are most physically attracted to our best 'mate' in our existing environment.

3\. It's been hypothesized that (bound by the 80/20 repeating ratio?) around 20% off all human offspring are reared by a male other than their true father.

Marry both real time cultural influences with the reproductive advantage of "sexy mates" and you get the ability to utilize thoughts to determine the here and now, and you get 20% of the offspring moving forward with the physical epigenetic/evolutionary advantages.

\*\*I use 80/20 ratio for point of communication. I'm almost sure that if analyzed with accuracy most of the things we call 80/20 are probably more accurately expressed the golden ratio (fibonacci)

### jeanie@truelongmont.com — 2012-05-22T05:15:22+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-the-h-is-factor-x-post-your-guesses.42/post-26201>

> **Jack Kruse;26184 wrote:** culture creates lots of mismatches......even the ancient ones.......

Culture protects and rears in the immediate. A primary threat to survival for hominids is predators, mechanical injury, and unplanned sudden environmental changes. By nature of a socialized animal, we need the group to protect us NOW in the current environment. That group provides an advantage over an individual. So we use our ability to THINK to find our place and belong with the others right now.

Could autism's role be providing a hominid that isn't so bound by their emotions - as that may be a trait needed to move forward.

Taking a 30,000 ft view. . . instead of a tunnel vision trained on who is not working out in our neolithic now and why they are failing - I wonder who IS thriving? Who is reproducing willy nilly and spreadin their genes? It appears that the natural cycle is that the parent die off after reproducing.

Evolution may well be insisting we die YOUNGER for the optimal chance of furthering life (planetary resources etc). Of course, that thought really came to me most often while watching TV and being on the computer all night while trying to nurse my baby that would not sleep more than a couple of hours at a time till 18months old. The sleep deprivation alone started a cascade of mismatches that I'm sure were designed to make sure I stayed alive just long enough for my baby to reproduce but no more.

Are we trying to outsmart nature? I'm a major poker player so I'm gonna stay in. I'm pot invested.

### ruby — 2012-05-22T05:25:30+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-the-h-is-factor-x-post-your-guesses.42/post-26212>

"The temperate parts of the planet are endowed with rather more resilience. This is primarily due to the vicissitudes of life in the temperate zone. Tropical climatic stability has nurtured the evolution of species over tens of millions of years in virtually unchanged conditions (save for variations in geographic range). (...)Because of enormous environmental variations seen there, animals living in the temperate zone have had to rely on crucial adaptations to keep themselves alive: investment and migration. (...) No tropical mammal would ever evolve the behaviour of building up fat reserves to prepare for times of famine, but most temperate species do it as a matter of course. During the Arctic summer, reindeer add as much as a third to their body weight, storing resources for the long dark winter. This allows them to survive the period of dearth that comprises 70% of the year. It also makes them tempting targets for carnivores".(source: Spencer Wells, The Journey of Man, Princeton University Press 2002)

I think the more we accumulate the more risky it becomes for oncogenesis due to histones and acetylation issues.

### jeanie@truelongmont.com — 2012-05-22T05:34:32+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-the-h-is-factor-x-post-your-guesses.42/post-26219>

I think the line is drawn in practicality. Since you can't really live in a cold dark world, and at best can approximate a winter - that you have to go with the flow of circadian rhythms. I think UNLESS you are in that theoretical "cold dark place" as an environment you probably are better off with veggies in your system to feed your gut cooties as that does seem to confer some symbiosis in operations in processing the warm/hybrid world.

What hasn't been defined in any clear way I think is "cold adapted". Surely an ice bath taker living a normalish life in Florida is simply a different animal than the human living in the dark for weeks on end in the arctic. So optimal MUST take into account your here and now environment.

Right now I don't think there is a clear answer on "how much cold, how much dark" one needs to run optimally. So far things are focused on day trading in the ancient pathway as a theraputic device. Begs the question, if one lives in a warm place - would living 24/7 in the ancient pathway be a mismatch too? Would it come with it's own versions of neolithic disease.

I think one has to evaluate their own pendulum arm.

Since I'm being rather obtuse tonight. . .

I think if YOU are cold adapted, and you are going to do CT, even during the summer, then the optimal thing IS low carb.

If your pendulum quit swinging, and you are close to optimal, then I think you want to transition to fitting in best with your current environment - whatever that is. Let nature decide for you!

> There is a role for "cultural traditions" even when they seemingly go against good ol fashioned natural selection. Don't dismiss the role of social groups as a necessary component of hominid evolution. It all ties in with ho's, cuckolding and thoughts changing your DNA. Consider the following:
>
>
>
> 1\. We are driven to mate with good smelling hotties (to us) for the physical advantages they provide our offspring.
>
>
>
> 2\. The same reasons that make that mate beneficial genetically, may not make that same mate beneficial in the immediate and current environment. In fact, rarely is the one we are most physically attracted to our best 'mate' in our existing environment.
>
>
>
> 3\. It's been hypothesized that (bound by the 80/20 repeating ratio?) around 20% off all human offspring are reared by a male other than their true father.
>
>
>
> Marry both real time cultural influences with the reproductive advantage of "sexy mates" and you get the ability to utilize thoughts to determine the here and now, and you get 20% of the offspring moving forward with the physical epigenetic/evolutionary advantages.
>
>
>
> \*\*I use 80/20 ratio for point of communication. I'm almost sure that if analyzed with accuracy most of the things we call 80/20 are probably more accurately expressed the golden ratio (fibonacci)

### Jack Kruse — 2012-05-22T14:42:04+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-the-h-is-factor-x-post-your-guesses.42/post-26295>

> **MJ\*;26364 wrote:** So is there a way to clean out our DNA, and if so, how would you even know what you might need, keep the HIV for tomorrow just in case, ditch the tail genes?

More RNA/DNA we have the more adaptable we become.......it is the fuel for a sped up epigentics. The trade off is that it allows for rapid expansion of our immune system and of our genome that makes us more susceptible to Cancer from mismatches.......but it appears long term the trade off must be worth it.......to evolution. It sped the rapid developmental changes in our gut and immune system to set the tone for encephalization as one great example.

I think you understand a lot more than you give yourself credit for! Good job! The idea is that things that we consider to be diseases today will likely be found to have had an evolutionary purpose - this is a huge area of science that is yet to be explored. But I think the short answer is that the things we see expressed as, and are considered to be, disease today like diabetes, may once have served an important purpose in life's quest to further life. In addition there are new viruses that crop up and make changes to our DNA that we have yet to understand just what the biological meaning of will be to our future as a species, and AIDS would be a good example of that...it comes on as a killer, and it's difficult to see such as thing as ever being good, but for those who survive it, what doesn't kill you makes you stronger as a species, and the DNA change from AIDS might be called upon at some point in our future to keep us stronger against a similar pressure.

### MJ* — 2012-05-22T19:17:26+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-the-h-is-factor-x-post-your-guesses.42/post-26392>

> **Anna K.;26476 wrote:** MJ, thanks for commenting. I think I'm healthy, but either way my goal is to slow my biological clock anyway and stay healthy and live long.
>
> So you are saying for that " you will need to maintain a real winter experience". What does that mean? Just keep dong CT and be low carb year round even though I live in FL? Moving to Alaska is not really an option for me.
>
> Also what I'm saying is that I really don't want to eat carbs, I'm functioning fine on LC of 30-60. The question is do I HAVE to go high carb occasionally to avoid mismatches? or LC in FL long term is just fine if I'm cold adapted and keep doing CT?
>
>
>
> thanks.

since this experience is relatively new for everyone, I'm not sure there is a direct answer, other than follow your health with your labs...if you are doing CT and LC and your labs are stellar, you keep doing what you are doing...if your labs, or how you are feeling health-wise begin to show some degredation you may have to look to make some changes. One thing to keep in mind is that carbs and winter don't mix...so if you are creating an extended winter for yourself to get any sort of inflammation under control then avoid the carbs while you are doing that...personally I think I could not live without some seasonal eating, I'd just die of boredom with the choices...I like the idea of get it all under control, then follow the seasons, CT in fall and winter, taper off and enjoy the summer and then start over...does that make sense?

### youthfuliz — 2012-05-22T19:33:09+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-the-h-is-factor-x-post-your-guesses.42/post-26394>

> **Jack Kruse;26184 wrote:** culture creates lots of mismatches......even the ancient ones.......

Diet and mecicine are just the tip of the iceberg. There are many other things that we do and take for granted (since it's what everyont is doing and have been for generations) are mismatches. Our big brain creates the morden "civilized" world that our body just doesn't buy. It's easy to do CT when everyone else is laughing, but harder to go against social, cultural institutions... the values and ethics our brain created to regulate ourselves, before it could understand itself biologically. Going against the CW is much more than diet, exercise and medicine.

### Jack Kruse — 2012-05-22T20:01:21+0300

> **Anna K.;26477 wrote:** What did the test results made you do differently that you weren't doing already? thanks.

altered my version of the paleo diet to one that better approximated my risk and that of my family. It showed me that my epigenetics were far more important than my genetic and family history and I made some radical lifestyle changes because of it.

### shilohman — 2012-03-16T17:52:27+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/glasses-to-block-blue-light.43/post-1529>

Cavemam, I use amber shooting glasses for reading. I also replaced all of the lights that I have on at night with yellow bug lights.

Since doing this 1)drop off to sleep almost immediately 2) no more waking up at 3:30am or later and unable to go back to sleep 3) much easier getting up in the morning.

When I heard about doing this I was very skeptical but for $15 (I already owned the shooting glasses) in light bulbs I figured to test it out.

Am I glad I did.

### Lyndra — 2012-03-19T19:18:31+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/glasses-to-block-blue-light.43/post-2821>

Is there a way to verify that the glasses really do block blue light? A google search turned a statement saying that when you wear real blue-blockers, the color blue looks black and white looks orange. I just tried that with some old amber ski goggles and saw some color differences but not a lot. Then I pulled out some blue LED Christmas lights and looked at them through the goggles. There was no difference at all - the blue lights still look blue so I'm thinking those won't work.

### L8F — 2012-03-19T23:22:07+0200

### Patty Cakes — 2012-03-19T23:29:34+0200

### jenn — 2012-03-25T00:23:43+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/glasses-to-block-blue-light.43/post-5588>

I have [these glasses](http://http://www.amazon.com/Cocoons-Vision-Absorptive-Filters-Sunglasses/dp/B002ACNSX2/ref=sr_1_4?ie=UTF8&qid=1332627399&sr=8-4), and I found out quite by accident how well they filter out blue light. I have a "hunter's flashlight" that has a blue setting (supposedly for tracking blood trails!), and when taking a walk one night with my amber glasses on, I switched the flashlight to the blue setting and thought it was defective because I could see no hint of light whatsoever!

They also fit over my prescription glasses well, and are especially handy at night, when family members insist on keeping the lights on!

### L8F — 2012-03-25T01:38:11+0200

### DarleenMB — 2012-03-25T22:43:16+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/glasses-to-block-blue-light.43/post-5990>

So glad I checked back in here. I had completely forgotten I have a pair of Serengetti sunglasses. They were out in the car. I checked their website and they are indeed blue blocking lenses. YAY! Now to find those stick-on magnifiers so I can read with them on.

### chocolate — 2012-03-26T03:02:23+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/glasses-to-block-blue-light.43/post-6165>

[https://www.bna.org.uk/news/view.php?permalink=JT46EDZ325](http://https://www.bna.org.uk/news/view.php?permalink=JT46EDZ325)

I was watching the video from the Cambridge Science Festival and understand wearing the blue blockers is cumulative. The little flashes of light aren't supposed to disrupt the circadian rhythm so much, either. Just like lightening wouldn't. Somehow, circadian vision accounts for the sum total of light. The lecture was engaging and easy to understand.

### co-caveman — 2012-04-12T06:37:47+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/glasses-to-block-blue-light.43/post-14097>

> **Cavemam;13924 wrote:** I got the big wraparound ones from lowbluelights. Love em. Use them every night when the sun goes down. Worth the $.

I picked these up a few weeks ago, they fit well over my prescription glasses and work great. Descent price too and I like the Kim Jong Il look

[http://shop.blublocker.com/collections/frontpage/products/starshield-blublocker](http://http://shop.blublocker.com/collections/frontpage/products/starshield-blublocker)

"BluBlockers originated from the space program back in 1986. Astronauts needed eye protection from the strong radiation of outer space so a pair of sunglasses was designed that blocked not only all the Ultra Violet rays but all the blue rays as well. Hereâ€™s why.

New research was being released indicating that there were many eye diseases caused by the blue spectrum light waves. Blocking the blue light decreased the danger from blue light and as a result several things were discovered.

First, by blocking blue light, squinting seemed to disappear. Objects seemed to appear clearer and sharper and eyes of the wearer were more relaxed yet the scene appeared to be brighter.

BluBlocker Corporation saw an opportunity to introduce these sunglasses to the mass market by obtaining patent rights from the manufacturer, lowering its cost, and providing the same protection the astronauts had.

Now, 25 years later, BluBlocker is back with the finest protection and both the old retro BluBlocker style and the new styles of the decade. No longer do you have to sacrifice protection for style.

Look through our selection of some of the advanced sunglasses styles available and know that you are getting value, style and protectionâ€”all in one package."

Cavemam, unless you jailbreak your iPad you cannot install fLux and I have been too chicken to do that to mine so I went on Amazon and searched for overhead projector sheets and bought 2 amber ones. At night I use my blue blockers and the overhead amber clear overlay on top of my iPad although it is slightly larger than the iPad you can still tap and read fairly easily through it and I also have an anti glare screen protector in it too. Hope this helps. They were dirt cheap, like 2 bucks each.

What makes vibration in a lattice in physics? Light does. Light always becomes a vibration in a liquid crystal first. So this is clue why the major coherent frequency in the brain is at 40 Hz and why a mitochondria needs to vibrate at 100Hz to fat burn. The MCU calcium micropore is the key to the transofmration of light to vibration. It also tells us that a Warburg shift occurs when the mitochondria cannot vibrate at this range due to the stretched out respiratory proteins where light energy is harvested by NAD+/NADH couple at 340nm. This is why a Warburg shift reverts to glycolysis alone. Fat burning requires a coherance among all mitochondrion in a tissue. It is not a function of the fuel we give a mitochondria. It is a function of how light energy is transformed into a physio-mechanical vibration in the mitochondria. This is why Volkow and Frey both found nnEMF always leads to glucose redox shift. The Q cycle cuts that light energy up into the optical wwindow frequencies (600-1400nm) which is the absorption spectrum of cytochrome c oxidase. That optimizes ATP production without the need for massive electrons to do it. When the respiratory proteins are stretched out the deli slicer between cyto 1 and 2 does not make IR light in the 600-1400 nm optical window.

### Souldanzer — 2012-03-22T23:31:17+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/optimal-labs.46/post-4659>

OPTIMAL LABS AND OPTIMAL RANGES

INSULIN RESISTANCE MARKERS

Fasting Insulin ---> less than 2

Fasting Glucose ---> under 85

HbA1c ---> this should be Free T3 and s/b in the top of the range for most people

reverse T3

FT3/rT3 ---> >20

Total T3/rT3 ---> >10

\*\*\*\* ratios must use same units!!\*\*\*\*

LIPIDS

total Cholesterol ---> >200

HDL

LDL

Triglycerides

Total/HDL Ratio

Iranian Formula

STEROID/SEX HORMONES

Plain DHEA (no S)

Estrogens, total

Estradiol

Estrone

Testosterone, Serum ---> 60 - 76ng/ml (I \*\*think\*\* this is for women but it seems a little high?)

Testosterone, Free, Direct

Progesterone ---> 1.5 day 3/4 of cycle

Progesterone, postmenopausal ---> 6-28ng/ml optimal 13-15

Pregnenolone ---> 200 - 230 ng/dl

DHEAs ---> 275 - 400ug/ml

CARDIAC RISK/INFLAMMATION MARKERS

CRP, cardiac (C-reactive protein, highly sensitive) ---> as close to 0.0 as possible

VITAMINS/MINERALS

Vitamin D ---> close to 100

Vitamin B12 ---> higher than 1000

Omega 6/Omega 3 (also aa/EPA ratio) ---> 4 : 1

Iron, saturation ---> above 35%

BONE HEALTH

Osteocalcin ---> in the teens at least

OTHER

alpha MSH ---> between 35 - 81

IGF-1 ---> 300-350ng/ml

CORTISOL/ADRENAL STRESS

1) Chronically elevated cortisol:

a. Phosphatidylserene — 2g a day in divided doses

b. Adaptogenic herbs — panax ginseng, rhodiola, ashwaganda, eleutherococcus

c. Cytokine support — resveratrol, pycnogenol, green tea extract, pine bark extract

d. Neurotransmitter GABA support — taurine, valerian root, passion flower, L-theanine

2) Chronically depressed cortisol:

a. Licorice root extract — Dosages depend on the type of licorice root extract used

b. Adaptogenic herbs — panax ginseng, rhodiola, ashwaganda, eleutherococcus

c. Cytokine support — Echinacea, astralagus, shiitake mushroom, beta-glucan, beta sitosterol

d. Excitatory neurotransmitter support — acetylcholine (Alpha-GPC, huperzine, galantamine), serotonin (5-HTP), tryptophan, St. John's wort

3) Abnormal circadian rhythm:

a. Acetylcholine support — Alpha GPC, huperzine, galantamine

b. Phopshytidylseriene — 2g a day in divided doses for minimum of 6 months

_**\*\*\* this information has been randomly collected from JanSz posts, Colleen's posts, Dr. K's posts, and other random readings.... use at your own risk!!\*\*\***

Please add and correct. I have it saved as a Pages Document that can be edited more easily._

### finnite@dccnet.com — 2012-03-23T00:40:08+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/optimal-labs.46/post-4687>

> **Souldanzer;4687 wrote:** OPTIMAL LABS AND OPTIMAL RANGES
>
>
>
> THYROID
>
>
>
> TSH
>
> T4
>
> T3 ---> this should be Free T3 and s/b in the top of the range for most people
>
> reverse T3
>
> FT3/rT3 ---> >20
>
> Total T3/rT3 ---> >10
>
> \*\*\*\* ratios must use same units!!\*\*\*\*

TSH, unmedicated 1-1.5 is optimal (functional ranges show up to 3 but I have seen women literally falling asleep at the wheel with a 3 TSH) it should be noted that TSH can vary up to 3 points in a day so watch the trend.

for men unmedicated TSH under 1.5 and women under 0.8 can be indicative of pituitary issues

FT4 should be 50% of the range for optimal

FT3 should be 75% of the range _or higher_ for optimal

(note to this, many labs are now narrowing their ranges, lab ranges are based on bell curves and as more and more hypothyroid people are being tested those ranges are narrowing greatly. if your lab has a narrow range then you may want to have FT3 at the top of the range provided that there are no hyper symptoms accompanying such as rapid pulse, temp spike, heart palps etc)

when testing FT3 there are 2 shools of thought.

1) take a morning dose of T3 meds and test at the 4-5 hour mark, no earlier or later. this window will show you how the incoming T3 is working in your body. T3 (cytomel) has a half life of between 12 and 48 hours (references vary)

2) ensure that you have at least 12 hours between dosing and testing because of the half life this will ensure that your FT3 reading is halfway or lower in the range. this is particularly useful if you are taking thyroid meds with a nervous doctor who will only go by predetermined lab test ranges and not symptoms or is not used to how exogenous T3 shows on tests

note Ft4 has a much longer half life and the lab results may not be accurate until the 6 week mark. However, I have had an experience when taking T3 only and then switching to NDT for 2 weeks prior to testing and had my FT4 reading jump from below range to low in range. as always YMMV

for a FT3/RT3 ratio to be valid both must be tested from the same blood draw.

**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/optimal-labs.46/post-24656>

# Thread: Hello from down under
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hello-from-down-under.47/>
**Subforum:** Meet and Greet
**Posts:** 14
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Rook — 2012-03-15T01:05:57+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hello-from-down-under.47/post-140>

hi all,

i'm rook from new zealand. looking forward to seeing what great info gets posted up on this forum. i do a bit of a mish mashed approach comprising of a diet pretty close to an archevore prescription with calorie cycling from lean gains and some cold themogenesis from jack kruse/tim ferriss....seems to be working ok at the moment and i'm about 6-8% off my ideal body fat percentage so not too far to go.

### Grottenolm — 2012-03-15T01:09:45+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hello-from-down-under.47/post-144>

Hi Rook! Glad to see another kiwi around these parts  What city are you in? I'm in Auckland ~ been eating paleo for about 2 years, now introducing cold therapy. Looking forward to seeing more kiwis come out of the woodwork!

### Rook — 2012-03-15T01:15:03+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hello-from-down-under.47/post-155>

I'm in Hamilton at the moment. Good work on the consistent paleo. I've been pretty strict with diet for the last 3-4 months at best. Before that I lost weight the traditional way, by completely overdoing exercise. It worked but I felt like crap and was eating next to nothing to maintain it...I much prefer eating properly and doing very sparse but intense exercise

Yeah, hopefully there'll be a few more kiwis around that we can share info with...

### Birdy — 2012-03-15T01:16:03+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hello-from-down-under.47/post-156>

Welcome!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!

### KiwiLauren — 2012-03-15T09:59:15+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hello-from-down-under.47/post-635>

Kia ora Rook and Grottenolm, I'm in Christchurch. I've been strict keto paleo for a few months now, doing the CT almost every day and trying to do the circadian cycles, too. I'd LOVE to share info about best places to source supplements, etc. There seem to be so many resources in the USA. Anyone know where you can get 99% chocolate that Dr K recommends so highly here in NZ?

### Rook — 2012-03-15T21:22:03+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hello-from-down-under.47/post-998>

Ah, the 99% stuff. I have a bit of 85% donovans chocolate (made right here in Hamilton) or I get 85% Lindt dark from places like Nosh (not sure if Nosh is down in Christchurch). We have a specialist chocolate shop in a few of the westfield malls up here that also have 100% chocolate but at $12 for 80g it's rather pricey.

### KiwiLauren — 2012-03-15T22:35:40+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hello-from-down-under.47/post-1054>

> **Rook;999 wrote:** Ah, the 99% stuff. I have a bit of 85% donovans chocolate (made right here in Hamilton) or I get 85% Lindt dark from places like Nosh (not sure if Nosh is down in Christchurch). We have a specialist chocolate shop in a few of the westfield malls up here that also have 100% chocolate but at $12 for 80g it's rather pricey.

Thanks! I used to live in Hamilton years ago. No Nosh in Chch (not much of anything here at the moment!), but I can get 85% Lindt at our supermarket. I'm still in search of the holy grail 99% stuff though....

Where are you buying supplements (assuming you use them)? I've used Health Post online but wonder if there are other/better/cheaper places? (Health Post is pretty great, though.)

### Grottenolm — 2012-03-16T10:09:41+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hello-from-down-under.47/post-1341>

Ah chocolate, how I love thee! I mostly drink 100% coco powder dissolved in water as a treat - the trade aid brand. Sometimes I add a dash of coconut milk. For chocolate, I sometimes eat the green and blacks 85%, available at countdown. However, my sister in melbourne sometimes sends me 100% Koko Black chocolate from Oz. Also, my in laws are German and I ask them to bring 99% chocolate when they come to visit  there will always be a way to get hold of addictive substances, lol!

For supplements I use iherb.com - no hassles with them yet, and postage costs are reasonable.

### KiwiLauren — 2012-03-19T08:39:19+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hello-from-down-under.47/post-2657>

Interesting that you use iherb.com without issue. A few years ago I ordered from the US and it was picked up by customs. They then added a $100 (yes, that's right, $100) fee for bringing in vitamins, which was more than what they'd cost me to buy. So I've been wary ever since (and when I saw that warning on iherb's site I wondered). On the chocolate front, I think I need my overseas relatives to get on board!

### Rook — 2012-03-20T20:48:07+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hello-from-down-under.47/post-3416>

I can't say that I take a large amount of supplements yet. I take D3 just because I don't get a chance to absorb a large amount of it naturally (doesn't help that i have darker skin) and that's about it at the moment. Since I don't really have any other problems I'm not too worried about supplementing yet but might look into it a bit later on.

### jumcc — 2012-03-24T09:37:05+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hello-from-down-under.47/post-5348>

hi guys, kiwi here too from qtown. I am not too far off my body fat goals but my hormones are stuffed, adrenals and thyroid. I also have high levels of mercury and lead.

I have been eating paleo low carb for about 6 months. Started weights a few months ago and have toned up and now adding CT in is stripping some fat off me.

I found the stuff interesting about the importance on leaving 3-4 hours between eating and sleep for hormones and fat loss so have really nailed that down as i a bit of late night snacker! For CT i have been sitting in the lake here. it's bloody freezing and i have had quite a few funny looks ha. I am not following the leptin reset guidelines. I train in a fasted state in the morning and eat and lunch and dinner, sometimes something in between. I get the best results when i train fasted.

I'm taking what i can from this site and applying it to me, doing one thing at a time. I find when you throw the kitchen sink at things it gets a bit overwhelming. I did go pretty hard, pretty fast with the CT and felt like crap but now feeling much better.

I also order from iherb and have had no problems. i eat the 85% lindt when i want chocy and i also do the cocoa in water trick. All thes chocolate around for easter is not helping my old chocolate habit!!!

### KiwiLauren — 2012-03-26T02:29:43+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hello-from-down-under.47/post-6133>

Sitting in the Queenstown Lake... I take my hat off to you (though I hope you've kept yours on!!).

### Rook — 2012-03-26T23:13:39+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hello-from-down-under.47/post-6581>

Wow, that's impressive! I'll bet you get some looks for that.

### LVK — 2012-03-29T00:43:40+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hello-from-down-under.47/post-7979>

Hi All,

I've been eating low carb for about three months now. Lost over 10kg before grinding to a halt. Been sitting about the same weight for a few weeks now. But all good, half way there and feeling loads better.

I live on 10 acres just out of Crofton, NZ. We eat about 30% meat off our own land. Goat, pig, & lamb.

Don't know anyone else besides my family eating low carb. People look at me funny when I tell them what we eat (or don't eat).

Louise
