While I am not in your situation, I do want to let you know that you will see results from this journey. I have been primal since Feb/2010, and lost 35 lbs. Have been at a plateau for 2 years now. I still need to lose another 30. I have thyroid issues, so my individual body issues are different than yours. But I know you will see results. One thing I thought of after reading your posts, is to not only give you encouragement - keep at it!!!, but to suggest you drink more filtered water. This should help "flush" your system. Please post if this helps. I've recently (Mar 4) started Lrx, and have just started cold adaptation. I'll keep you posted. I hope to hear from you again.

### Clabbergirl — 2012-03-22T20:09:20+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-benefits-of-cold-thermogenesis-thread.111/post-4570>

> **emmythom;4588 wrote:** While I am not in your situation, I do want to let you know that you will see results from this journey. I have been primal since Feb/2010, and lost 35 lbs. Have been at a plateau for 2 years now. I still need to lose another 30. I have thyroid issues, so my individual body issues are different than yours. But I know you will see results. One thing I thought of after reading your posts, is to not only give you encouragement - keep at it!!!, but to suggest you drink more filtered water. This should help "flush" your system. Please post if this helps. I've recently (Mar 4) started Lrx, and have just started cold adaptation. I'll keep you posted. I hope to hear from you again.

Thank you, emmythom. I am drinking a lot of herbal tea in between caffeine (in the form of iced or hot tea mostly) to try and flush toxins or just keep hydrated. I've been napping every day after work. Congratulations on your weight loss. I hope that is me one day, sooner than later. I feel quite foolish with so much invested in this for 3 months and actually gaining weight and feeling worse. Even the skeptic in me is starting to raise an eyebrow when I know this is a much healthier way of eating than I've ever done before. I don't want to go back to wheat or anything, but it's certainly triggering old feelings of 'I'm not like everyone else in this weight loss game', and that kind of victimized thinking is counterproductive to what I'm trying to do. I am also hypothryoid, have been since 2002, although I suspect my adrenals were going under back then and went unchecked.

Really struggling to hang on but what other options do I have. Keepin' on keeping on.

### emmythom — 2012-03-22T20:12:00+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-benefits-of-cold-thermogenesis-thread.111/post-4573>

Have you eliminated dairy, corn, rice, oats, (all grains)?? No potatoes, no alcohol?? No sugar?? - not even sugar substitutes, no honey, maple syrup, agave?? Are you taking a multivitamin, or some B12, or D3??

### MamaGrok — 2012-03-22T20:15:04+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-benefits-of-cold-thermogenesis-thread.111/post-4576>

Don't get the feeling that we all have had rapid progress. It took me 6 months just to be able to complete 8 weeks of the LR without a binge. It's taken me two full years to lose 45 pounds. Others have had weight increase before decrease. Check out quelson's journey, too - holy moly!

We're all broken, just in different ways. I've been fortunate to have weight loss (albeit slow) since the beginning, but I'm so tired every day I can hardly get out of my chair and feeling like I hardly know my children anymore b/c I just send them off to play most of the time so I can rest & research. Different brokens, but we'll all get there with patience & perseverance!

### differentstory — 2012-03-22T20:33:27+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-benefits-of-cold-thermogenesis-thread.111/post-4589>

Have any of you had your significant other walk into the room and test the water while you are in the tub???

Last night, after I got out and had been out for 2 minutes, I had to reach my hand back into the water and I was SHOCKED at how cold it was!!!!  I couldn't believe I had just sat in that for an hour. And it was only 62 degrees - not the 40 and 50 others of you have been doing.

I'm interested to ask DH what he thinks of the water temp. tonight. I think he's going to be shocked!

### MamaGrok — 2012-03-22T23:16:50+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-benefits-of-cold-thermogenesis-thread.111/post-4651>

I started cold air exposure in December, not realizing Dr. Kruse was going to make CT part of the regimen ... I was just doing it to get some morning sunshine, then figured that exposure to natural temps would be good for me, too.

I started spot-icing about 4 weeks ago, and mostly get it done every other day. I started baths that week, too, but the water probably doesn't get below 60, ever, even with ice, and I only am able to fit it in around 2-3x/week. I'm definitely still cold (especially when dumb enough to forget gloves/socks, and do it 6 hours after my last meal), but also able to do it for an hour now instead of just 30m.

I had crazy pimples last week, and low energy this week, but the fat just keeps coming off. I have added a dietary thyroid supplement and have continued the totally dairy- and gluten-free diet I started on 1/1/12, and continued the leptin reset, but I do think the CT is doing the weight benefits at this time. (Also, I think eliminating dairy & FODMAPs have all but killed my long-time bloating problem, which is another weight drop, but at least several pounds of the 5+ I've lost in the last two months are fat loss, not water.)

ETA It started by the belly & thigh fat feeling loose and like I could almost pull it off, instead of tightly bound to me and very wide, as it had been. Those have shrunk most, and now the fat on the back of my upper arms, and on my forearms, is feeling the same way those did a few weeks ago!

### finnite@dccnet.com — 2012-03-22T23:59:05+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-benefits-of-cold-thermogenesis-thread.111/post-4670>

Congrats MamaG! that is so exciting for you!!

Well earned

### Susan M. — 2012-03-23T01:13:10+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-benefits-of-cold-thermogenesis-thread.111/post-4715>

I have had to put my hand back in the tub after forgetting to drain it right after I get out, and yes, it amazes me that I am able to sit in that kind of cold for an hour at a time now. It's crazy! My husband isn't interested in how cold it is yet. He just says "don't look at me for sympathy!". He really is supportive of me though.

>
>
>
>
>
>
>
> ETA It started by the belly & thigh fat feeling loose and like I could almost pull it off, instead of tightly bound to me and very wide, as it had been. Those have shrunk most, and now the fat on the back of my upper arms, and on my forearms, is feeling the same way those did a few weeks ago!

Congrats. It must feel wonderful. Thank you for the details, btw. Very helpful. So in 1 month of CT, you've lost approx. 10 lbs? And you were 90% to your goal weight when you began?

### Kpcst — 2012-03-23T01:44:00+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-benefits-of-cold-thermogenesis-thread.111/post-4733>

I was 225 when I started paleo a yr n half ago. I dropped to 205 easy, and have been there for a year. 2 weeks ago I started CT and a strict keto paleo. I am 197 today. I also did 12.5 cross fit open workout and felt fine without having had many carbs the last two weeks. Not sure if it's the CT or the More regimented diet. Either way I'm happy.

KP

### Evo Mama — 2012-03-23T01:45:22+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-benefits-of-cold-thermogenesis-thread.111/post-4734>

Yay, MamaGrok!!! Congrats on that milestone. Your perseverence and successes are such great inspiration!

### MamaGrok — 2012-03-23T01:50:03+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-benefits-of-cold-thermogenesis-thread.111/post-4738>

Thank you! It feels awesome! I got \*to\* 140 after babe#3, \*almost\* to 140 after babe#4, and again almost to 140 last year before I went on a binge spree and gained 10lb it took me a year to lose, so \*yes\*, it feels AWESOME!

> **Clabbergirl;4757 wrote:** Congrats. It must feel wonderful. Thank you for the details, btw. Very helpful. So in 1 month of CT, you've lost approx. 10 lbs? And you were 90% to your goal weight when you began?

I lost 5lb in the last two months. Well, almost three months, now. I keep forgetting how far we are into March! 2.5 of those (or so) have been since I started CT. Some of the losses are certainly water weight from losing the accursed bloating, and some are most definitely fat loss. My pinch is much less than an inch these days!

(And BTW, my goal isn't really 135. I just put that b/c that's what I was in high school & college & early marriage, and b/c when I started this primal journey, I didn't really believe I could ever be as thin as I was at 24 (120lb, which is "ideal" for my height) again ... my belief is changed now, but we'll see where this journey takes me!)

### Ane — 2012-03-23T04:04:37+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-benefits-of-cold-thermogenesis-thread.111/post-4803>

### Evo Mama — 2012-03-23T15:01:15+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-benefits-of-cold-thermogenesis-thread.111/post-4926>

I just mentioned this on the CT8 blog comments, but I thought I'd mention it here too. Prior to starting LRx and CT on 3/6/12, my knee was giving me trouble, following weekly sprinting and coaching/playing youth intramural basketball (I can't tell you how much fun it is to play basketball with youngsters on lowered nets!). My knee would start getting sore about 30 minutes after playing and would be stiff and sore throughout the next day, and fading soreness for a day or two after than. By the time I recovered, it was time to sprint or play b-ball again. When I started LRx and CT, I stopped sprinting altogether and had a break in the bball schedule, so last night was the first time I played basketball since starting. And, guess what? No knee soreness whatsoever! Truly amazing. I feel great today.

To add some TMI - Prior to LRx and CT, DH and I were lucky if we got intimate 1x per week. Sometimes, it was 1x per month. This week, 5x. Actually, tonight will most likely make 6. It would likely be even more often if I wasn't sitting in a cold tub so much.

In addition, as I've mentioned before, my body comp has improved, along with my mood, brain function, hair, nails, and recently sleep. I wake up ready to tackle the day and greatly look forward to my CT. Life is good, and it's only getting better!!!

### Zorica Vuletic — 2012-03-23T19:17:54+0200

That's how my ass is right now...loose.   I'm thinking it will 'melt' now. Btw, I've already had some 'melting' already...but am getting more now. Yay!

### Adria — 2012-03-24T00:49:47+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-benefits-of-cold-thermogenesis-thread.111/post-5206>

I'm not cold adapted yet cause I'm going slow. But already seeing benefits. The first week I had increased arthritis in all joints, wondering if I should quit. Kept going and slowly increased the cold. I lost 5# the 2nd week. The 3rd week my arthritis was gone. Never could I allow my feet, hands, knees to get cold without pain, been this way 20+yrs. All GONE. I can walk outside in freezing temps without jacket and with no shoes. If I tried that before I'd be in a world of hurt.

Feeling calm and happy and sleeping like a rock. Eating only 2x per day, no snacks and no cravings. Can't wait to see what changes will be next! No pre-menopause, no signs or symptoms at all, which I attribute to the keto-paleo diet.

### Myree67 — 2012-03-25T08:30:17+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-benefits-of-cold-thermogenesis-thread.111/post-5725>

Ok I had to lower my sleep meds and so far am still sleeping 8-9.5 hours a night am working on tapering off my pain meds to see how I do... pain specialist suggested trying that... increased activity... this is a slow work in progress but I'm willing to do the work... I noticed after my cold shower yesterday I felt energized and didn't hurt nearly as bad.. today was a lazy day and I didn't do the shower and wasn't as active and pain level is pretty high so just finished icing back,hips and tummy... I'm taking my freaking shower tomorrow... I'm sleeping sooooo much better and I increased my ct just last Monday... I'll increase again this Monday... I hope this works for my chronic pain! I just saw a pain specialist but she threw so many terms my way that I can't remember all of them... she advised icing... DUH... and increased activity slowly walking... an hour of meditation a day for pain control and taper off pain meds... I hope and pray I can get off all these meds!!!

I have new hope now that I'm sleeping again!!!

### LinD — 2012-03-25T18:21:04+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-benefits-of-cold-thermogenesis-thread.111/post-5845>

I instantly believed CT is legit after my husband used ice packs starting Nov 2011 for his back pain and when he got to the point of an ESI, the nurse commented he had no love handles. ! Eureka! Indeed they were gone. Wow.

DH did have L4/L5 surgery in Feb (to remove the c-clamp like bone spur, plus a cyst was found in the spinal column), but I couldn't get him to start CT, much less ice packs since his surgeon seemed to frown on the use of ice packs after the surgery. (too bad?)

Now, after my CT experiences, DH is noticing my flat tummy area.... Guess who now wants to start CT? We are so good for each other. ;-)

### debbie98055 — 2012-03-27T02:57:21+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-benefits-of-cold-thermogenesis-thread.111/post-6713>

I really feel relaxed and have more energy. I feel like doing more home decorating, which is a good sign for me since a lot of the time I struggle with low energy and brain fog. I also have a new favorite color, pale yellow : ). Weird, I've never been a person who likes yellow and now I'm dying to buy clothes and sheets in my new favorite color?

I've only been doing ct for 2 weeks, and I'm going REALLY slowly compared to most. I do not have weight to lose but my thighs seem to be less flabby.

### hellojtm — 2012-03-27T03:39:20+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-benefits-of-cold-thermogenesis-thread.111/post-6730>

So it seems like there are a lot of people reporting a lot of things. One that really stands out to me is improved sleep. I know that having proper sleep hygiene is the most important paleo thing I have ever done. It improves body composition, energy, libido, reduces hunger, etc... so there are a lot of connections here. However, off the top of my head, here are the things that I thought were supposed to be benefits of CT:

\- better hormone sensitivity

\- perfect thyroid regulation

\- decreased appetite

\- increased brown adipose tissue = less load on the leaky muscular mitochondria ATP generation

\- increase in catabolism when used on top of good sleep hygiene and caloric (or macronutrient) restriction = longer telomeres

\- uncoupling intake from body weight due to BAT wasting of calories

\- better adrenal function

\- improved recovery time from all exertions

\- lower inflammation

\- reduced fasting blood glucose

\- less oxidation

\- increased RER

\- increased v02max

I may have missed something.

jesse

### Kpcst — 2012-03-27T04:49:38+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-benefits-of-cold-thermogenesis-thread.111/post-6767>

I have been doing CT for 2 1/2 weeks. Steady cold baths for 30+ min a day. Yesterday I played golf,mowed the front and back yard, weedeated, washed the driveway.... All in 82 degree weather. I hardly broke a sweat. My wife was in the AC and cleaning the house. She was sweating a lot. Today I xfit and the WOD was brutal, and outside. I hardly sweated at all. I drink plenty of ice water all day long, and would say I'm an average "sweater". Does being cold adapted mean your body doesn't need to sweat as much bc we control temp internally or were the last two days my imagination?

KP

I've noticed this same thing. Less sweating/body heat produced by the same exercise. Really apparent in the last week. Just seem less inflamed overall, although maybe it's psychosomatic. I'm about 3 weeks in. Cold baths and cold showers, no heat in the car with windows down (still in the 30s and 40s here), occasional ice pack, nothing but ice water for drinking.

### omlh@todmi.com — 2012-03-27T12:03:09+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-benefits-of-cold-thermogenesis-thread.111/post-6896>

Yesterday was a bad day as a family - my youngest daughter injured herself badly, and we headed to hospital (she will be OK, but it will take some time, lots of stock/bone broth and good foods, rest and some pain meds as she has a winged scapula following a bad shoulder dislocation). Whilst there, I ended up several times having to run fast and hard to the car for varying items she needed. It wasn't until the evening, when we were home again, that I realised that (a) my knee didn't hurt (I am trying to avoid knee replacement surgery), and (b) I hadn't been out of breath at any point, which is unheard of for me! Missed my cold soak yesterday, for obvious reasons, but will get back on track today as I can really see some major benefits.

### finnite@dccnet.com — 2012-03-27T15:45:48+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-benefits-of-cold-thermogenesis-thread.111/post-6950>

Olivia,

glad your daughter will be okay! and yeah you on the physical improvements

### TheKid — 2012-03-30T16:02:26+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-benefits-of-cold-thermogenesis-thread.111/post-8726>

> **MamaGrok;4604 wrote:** Don't get the feeling that we all have had rapid progress. It took me 6 months just to be able to complete 8 weeks of the LR without a binge. It's taken me two full years to lose 45 pounds. Others have had weight increase before decrease. Check out quelson's journey, too - holy moly!
>
>
>
> We're all broken, just in different ways. I've been fortunate to have weight loss (albeit slow) since the beginning, but I'm so tired every day I can hardly get out of my chair and feeling like I hardly know my children anymore b/c I just send them off to play most of the time so I can rest & research. Different brokens, but we'll all get there with patience & perseverance!

You know something, you've just hit on something here. You mentioned being so tired and sending your kids off to play so that you can rest and research. I have been thinking about something I heard on a podcast recently in which the guy was explaining the trials travails of his health journey and the fact that when one is as broken as some of us are, the road to health can become like a part-time or even full-time job. I don't know if such single-minded focus is necessarily healthy.

You see, stress can undo so much of what we are trying to achieve. And I have noticed that sometimes I will be at home with my head buried in a blog or forum or some research paper, while the rest of my actual life passes me by. For myself, I am trying to get to a point where I can build sufficient knowledge about the protocols I need so that I can just implement them and then put the plane on autopilot for a while. As Ron Popeil would say, "Set it and forget get!"

This is why I am looking forward to the book from Jack, in the hopes that there will be a section(s) where he lays out in bullet points exactly what to do, when to do it, when to test, when to stop, when to start, etc.

For me, this journey can be an obsession, and while we need to be proactive about our health, there is a difference between being proactive and being neurotic. Some people can be like Tim Ferris and record everything every day and not become a head case. For others, constantly watching the scale or the blood glucose meter or anything else may become a significant stressor. So, again, I am attempting to reach a point where I am not applying a protocol(s), but rather I am living my life in a way that allows me to obtain optimal health.

Anyways, just my two cents.

### MamaToMyMunkins — 2012-03-30T16:40:51+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-benefits-of-cold-thermogenesis-thread.111/post-8760>

TheKid- EXCELLENT post! Thanks for that perspective!  I am on the same path right now, and noticing that everything is going much smoother than it was when I was obsessing on every little detail. I appreciate the reminder this morning!

Some people can be like Tim Ferris and record everything every day and not become a head case. For others, constantly watching the scale or the blood glucose meter or anything else may become a significant stressor. So, again, I am attempting to reach a point where I am not applying a protocol(s), but rather I am living my life in a way that allows me to obtain optimal health.
>
>
>
> Anyways, just my two cents.

Very good post and so true! I was just thinking the same thing this last few days. I have adrenal fatigue and am wondering if I just start doing things that I really enjoy, might I actually help myself in the long run instead of always just reading all the probs we all have out here and further stressing over how to fix it all. There probably should be some balance and I know I have been obsessed with reading and not necessarily having any fun like even watching tv programs that make me laugh or intrigues me. Might just watch some telly tonight afterall and put the laptop down for a break!

### omlh@todmi.com — 2012-03-30T21:17:57+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-benefits-of-cold-thermogenesis-thread.111/post-8904>

Had some really good news today, which for me really underlines the benefits of CT. A couple of years ago, I dislocated my patella, and have had recurrent dislocations and subluxations (several a month) since. An MRI scan showed severe damage, and I have been seeing an orthopaedic surgeon to discuss knee replacement surgery to help solve this. My life has been very restricted because of this, spending half my time on crutches, so I was obviously keen to resolve the problem. I had another appointment today with the surgeon and he was very pleasantly surprised at how effective the physio I've been having has been - much more effective than he's seen before. I was asked whether I had been taking collagen supplements (stock/bone broth for me!) as the ligaments and tendons are much tighter than they were, and the knee is significantly more stable. I explained that I had been having cold baths and he reckons that this has helped immensely - in fact, if it's even better in another couple of months, he wants to do another MRI scan to track the changes.

I really have to say a very big thank you to Dr. Kruse for this, as it appears to be healing something I was told could never properly heal, and should mean that I can avoid major surgery - which is a huge health benefit, and I am so grateful for this chance.

### Marsha — 2012-03-30T21:34:11+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-benefits-of-cold-thermogenesis-thread.111/post-8910>

> **oliviascotland;6933 wrote:** Yesterday was a bad day as a family - my youngest daughter injured herself badly, and we headed to hospital (she will be OK, but it will take some time, lots of stock/bone broth and good foods, rest and some pain meds as she has a winged scapula following a bad shoulder dislocation). Whilst there, I ended up several times having to run fast and hard to the car for varying items she needed. It wasn't until the evening, when we were home again, that I realised that (a) my knee didn't hurt (I am trying to avoid knee replacement surgery), and (b) I hadn't been out of breath at any point, which is unheard of for me! Missed my cold soak yesterday, for obvious reasons, but will get back on track today as I can really see some major benefits.

I hope they are using ICE on her shoulder!

### sd13 — 2012-03-30T23:33:13+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-benefits-of-cold-thermogenesis-thread.111/post-8985>

Here is my story for those worried about not getting ct results in a short period of time:

For perspective--I started low carb diet in Jan. 2009 at 345lbs--lost 33lbs fairly rapidly then thing ground to a halt. The following 2 and a half years I managed to lose another 32. I tried lower carbs, higher carbs, lower cals, higher cals in vaying combos and nothing worked. Jan 2 0f this year I started the leptin reset and no weight loss--started ct baths 6.5 weeks ago. No wieght loss, no change in measurments, no reduction in appetite and sleep improved very slightly. I so wanted all the wonderful changes other seemed to be enjoying and I assumed I was really, really broken. Well something amazing happened this week, on Monday I experiened substantial appetite reduction, and the seemingly impossible happened too--I have lost 5 pounds in the past 3 days! That hasn't happened to me since the first week of going low carb, over 3 years ago! So any who doubt, keep the faith

>

When I get my health back to that point, there will be no wistful moments of life passing me by. ### TheKid — 2012-03-31T10:16:19+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-benefits-of-cold-thermogenesis-thread.111/post-9140>

Miraculous healing powers! Could it be true?!?

So I don't know if I'm just really searching for something, or if what I am noticing is really a result of Dr. Jack's protocols, especially the CT.

One thing I've notived over the past few years is that I am slow to heal whenever I get a cut or scrape or whatever. Just a week ago I took a nice gouge out of my finger while working on something. After one week, this thing is really just about closed up and healed. I haven't healed this quickly since I was a child.

Now there is a confounding factor, because I did use a new 3M Nextcare medicated bandage on it. It was the first time I ever used this product; so one might attribute the healing to this bandage. But just in case it was the CT, I wanted to at least share the experience and see if anyone else has had anything similar happen to them.

I have used Tegaderm for a couple of years. In my experience, it speeds up the healing in that you don't have a scab to deal with. A week before Christmas I cut my finger almost to the bone. Not bad enough for stitches, but almost. I used gauze + tape + finger splint for a week so the edges could begin to knit. Then two weeks with Tegaderm and it still had a very red scar but was nicely knitted. I don't bruise easily, and I figure my healing times are about average.

\]I'm not sure if it's the CT or the one time a day Jarrow 5000 mcg B12, or the occasional iodine supplementation, but this odd bump on my palate has greatly diminished the last two days. It appeared Dec 2011 with no pain... Saw DDS in Jan and then again early Feb and he determined it was an abscess and I should have a root canal. Timing was bad with DH's back surgery, so I postponed the dental procedure. Besides, I had no pain and DH certainly did!

It's April now and still no mouth/tooth pain... And this 'bump might be disappearing. Now if the feet numbness (MS-related?) would go away, too, I will be dancing quite a jig! Actually, quite thrilled if the 'abscess' is gone for good.

### MartiD — 2012-04-09T17:26:05+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-benefits-of-cold-thermogenesis-thread.111/post-12997>

I made an interesting discovery this morning. As many of us have said our skin is different and I have mentioned my "fat" tissue in legs doesn't feel as marbled.

I have a scar on my belly (belly button to public bone) from a spinal fusion two years ago. The scar has always been hard and bumpy from scar tissue and it bugs me but I have learned to not obsess over it for quite a while now. This morning in the shower I noticed as a rubbed my hand over it with soap that the tissue is very soft now, almost non-detectable by touch! I applied a bit of pressure and that hard scar tissue is soft! I even think that visually it is lighter in color ( I know my stretch marks are lighter)! Granted I will never wear a 2 piece again but very excited that this scar is disappearing! I have been doing cold baths since mid-February and started spot CT'ing my belly for a few weeks ago...probably only done the spotting 3 times! I also have a scar on my lower back from a herniated disc operation 13 years ago and it is still hard to the touch but I haven't spotted that area and it is a much older scar so may not ever change.

### Evo Mama — 2012-04-09T22:12:54+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-benefits-of-cold-thermogenesis-thread.111/post-13115>

> **MamaGrok;9296 wrote:** I am healing much better over the last few years of primal eating. I wouldn't be surprised if diet & CT both contribute. I have burned myself on the oven and seen the scar disappear entirely in a few months (similar burns in the past and on others last years or a lifetime), and I'm pretty sure that a scar I've had since I hit a brick on a slip n slide as a child is disappearing.

I burned myself fairly bad on Saturday on my oven (baking kale chips and playing with toddler not always a good mix - toddler is completely safe - mommy got burned, well, then toddler was subjected to four-letter-word, but other than that, he's fine  ). Oh, and the kale chips were fine, too, for those who might be concerned.

After cursing quite loudly, I ran my arm under cold water, and NO PAIN!!! And, it's Monday, less than 2 days later and the mark is almost completely gone. I've never had a burn mark disappear so quickly.

I'm one who occasionally needs motivation to jump in that cold tub. This thread is a god-send for motivation. I love hearing all of the interesting little ways our lives are improving, folks.

PS - Mamagrok - I just read your goal of running a 10k without training. I'll be doing something similar in May (yikes!). I signed up for a 5k mud run (with all of the little obstacles along the way) before I started the Leptin Rx and CT. I've decided to run the mudder without really training (I'm 5 weeks into the reset, showing signs of LS, so I am going to start doing a little bit more workout activity, just because my body really WANTS to move, but I'm not going to be doing any running, other than some sprints). Not sure when you are planning to meet this goal, but I look forward to comparing notes.

### MamaGrok — 2012-04-09T22:45:14+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-benefits-of-cold-thermogenesis-thread.111/post-13128>

I have a scar on my finger from sealing up after I slammed my finger horrible into a door close to ten years ago. (This was while on Celebrex for back pain (which did nothing, btw, and physical therapy took care of in a jiffy) - during those two weeks, I thought I was pregnant because of constant mind slips and clumsiness. Went off it; it all stopped.)

The scar still hurts. I wonder if I should occasionally dip my finger in the cold water (I've been keeping hands entirely out, but my water no longer gets below 63, anyway, and I have no circulation problems), or if that's unnecessary.

**

Does CT'ing heal skin by directly putting that part in the water, or does it cause allover increase in skin healing even if that part is not in the water?**

Evo Mama - that is a fantastic healing story, and yes, one day I'm TOTALLY doing a mudder. I always loved obstacle courses. I'm so tired of EVERYONE around me 'training' for their 10K's, and worse, needing knee replacements, back surgery, etc., etc., b/c of their hobby. I heartily believe a healthy person should not need any training for these silly things (and shouldn't be injured by them if done rarely). My body is still in "rest" mode, so I'm not ready yet, but my training will solely be "walk slow a lot," "sprint occasionally," and LHT in the form of SimpleFit. I can't wait to hear your results.

(BTW, dh did one a few years ago. We went to a bowl game with our tailgating friends and they all wanted to do it. They trained and they trained. He just showed up (he's naturally eaten near paleo all his life and is very lean and strong despite not having time to exercise at all anymore except a few softball & flag football games a year). They ranged from walk/job to "I trained and daggumit it, I'm FINISHING this beast!" (then being exhausted when done), whereas he just started jogging, kept up with the fastest of them, and at the end, I asked him whether he was ready to go again and he (quietly) told me yes, he could easily get up and go again right that second.)

### Dali Dula — 2012-04-16T15:01:12+0300

Although started LR back in Nov. and have been CTing since Feb all with great results somehow I missed a really important point regarding the circadian rhythms. DO NOT EAT OR DRINK ANY CALORIES FOR 4 HOURS BEFORE SLEEP AT NIGHT. I started doing this about 4 weeks ago and it really kicked it up. Muscle gain, weight loss, feelings of wellness, no hunger, all with less exercise. I know this is clearly stated in the blogs, but I missed this point for months so maybe others have also.

funny this is mention. my beard grows used to grow in at a normal once a day shave pace..now my 5 o'clock shadow resembles Baby Finster and I'm pushing 50...

### Birdy — 2012-04-18T17:55:20+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-benefits-of-cold-thermogenesis-thread.111/post-16217>

I have had a dry and scaly elbow for perhaps forever, at least most of my adult life. Just one elbow, not the other. It is now completely gone. For the first time in years, I can actually see my elbow, and make out the scars left from bicycle accidents I had as a kid. My skin on my feet is changing too, though I expected that since my feet have been experiencing the cold water. But, the elbow is a total surprise, since it was only exposed to cold air and brief showers. I'll be really interested in seeing what my hba1c is this fall, when I get my yearly tests.

### Larry — 2012-04-26T21:10:56+0300

1\. Wart (that I have had for about 10 years) on left elbow - gone.

3\. Weight loss since March 7th - 7.5 lbs.

4\. My bodies' amazing biology at work with cold - priceless!

### LisaAPB — 2012-04-27T16:56:04+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-benefits-of-cold-thermogenesis-thread.111/post-19326>

I started eatng Paleo about a year ago, Leptin Rx last fall, full CT in February of this year. I'd inadvertently been cold adapting myself in a half ass way, for much longer. Someone had told me my arms, legs and face in very cold water after my shower was good for the circulation and skin, so I started doing that almost 2 years ago. Prior to starting any of this, I was hypothyroid, had fatty liver disease, IBS, anemic, and prediabetic with a FBG of 130. After a year of eating right, I got the FBG down into the 90's. By the end of February after 3 weeks of full CT, my **FBG is **consistently 80 or less. My **liver enzymes are all in the **normal range again too. Anemia** is gone**. My TSH level, unmedicated, prior to starting was 15 (normal range is 1-5), unmedicated TSH at the end of February was 5... so the thyroid** is getting better** too. I do muscle/strength building workout once a week for 20 to 30 minutes and I am seeing some good muscle definition. The only other physical test was I did a fast 10 mile bike ride after not riding for a year, and I didn't get winded or tired at all, even riding up the hills didn't cause any pain!

### Jack Kruse — 2012-04-27T17:21:05+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-benefits-of-cold-thermogenesis-thread.111/post-19336>

**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-benefits-of-cold-thermogenesis-thread.111/post-19351>

I haven't even attempted to do any kind of running since my spinal fusion two years ago but don't have any soreness after two trys. I am also doing some weights but just started so not seeing much yet but looking forward to it! Can't wait for our local pool to open Memorial Day weekend. That water will be nice and cold and I'll pretty much have it to my self cause nobody can stand to get in the first few weeks!

I did my longest CB last night with added ice to tap water. Temp was somewhere between 55-60 degrees and I stayed in almost 45 minutes. I usually struggle with evening baths but this one seems a bit easier even though it took me awhile to get warmed up afterwards. Slept like a rock though! This mornings CB was super easy and didn't seem cold at all!

### janellestg — 2012-04-29T03:21:10+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-benefits-of-cold-thermogenesis-thread.111/post-19708>

I had gastric bypass surgery 10 years ago. When I had complications another surgeon inadvertently ruined my surgery while trying to help me. Later a third surgeon tried to help me by lengthening my bypass and further decreasing my absorption. Long story short I ended up not losing much weight and having severe malabsorption issues. I have had cancer, scurvy, anemia, and chronic diarrhea. Because my vagus nerve was severed it was going to be very difficult to become LS. For the last 2 months I have incorporated CT. I realized today that my leaky gut is so much better. I actually thought I was constipated because I had become so used to the diarrhea. I had lab work done last week. Can't wait for the results. I know they are going to be much improved.

### LisaAPB — 2012-04-29T08:14:53+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-benefits-of-cold-thermogenesis-thread.111/post-19737>

> I assume you're following the AI paleo diet w/ no dairy, nuts, eggs, nightshades? I've been eating a few raw egg yolks here and there and just hoping it isn't causing any problems, but I don't notice any reactions at all.

No, I do not totally avoid those foods, except certain dairy, like milk, which has too high lactose content for me. I eat very small amounts of hard aged cheese, use it more like a seasoning. I use heavy cream in my coffee, and I eat 8 to 10+ eggs a week. I eat macadamia nuts, some almonds and some brazil nuts now and then. I use heavy cream in my coffee, and I eat 8 to 10+ eggs a week. Occasionally I have a slice of tomato (BLT - bacon & tomato in a lettuce boat), and I use different kinds of peppers in cooking. In summer and fall, I eat fruit daily. About once a week I have greek yogurt with berries & chopped nuts as a treat (BG testing does not show a rise with this combo). I eat dark chocolate or cocoa crack once or twice a week too.

### vkiernan — 2012-04-29T14:46:24+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-benefits-of-cold-thermogenesis-thread.111/post-19750>

I have to report my sleep is finally getting better and I am able to sleep in the am longer. I am also not waking in the middle of the night unless DH wakes me as he doesn't sleep well but I am also going right back to sleep. This has only been the last 4 nights but that is 4 in a row. Big improvement. Now whether it's due to CT or dhea, I'm not sure. They both are probably playing a role. I am feeling better in the day too. I believe the CT really detoxed me. It was rough but well worth it. I seem to be having less mental (mood) issues lately and this should be pms week. Yeh me on that. Sleep and better mood - CT here I come.

I think my gut is getting better internally. My stomach makes lots of noise now when I eat and when I CT. Are any of you experiencing the noise thing? I have to believe that means it's doing it's thing. Last night though I had salmon, lettuce salad and cauliflower-sweet potato-coconut milk soup and I did my CT shortly after eating (I was very full) and by the time I was out of the bath, I was on the verge of getting hungry. I didn't like that cuz no more eating after dinner. I did wake up hungry this morning. That usually never happens either. Maybe things are starting to work.

Did some one say cellulite disappearance??? Still waiting on that.

Keratosis pilaris (chicken skin) is usually a vitamin A deficiency. Mine decreased significantly with fermented cod liver oil supplementation, and increased when I ran out (even though I'd been supp'ing for two years ... I later learned i have severe fat malabsorption, so that explains why I developed a deficiency so soon after stopping it). So it sounds like CT is increasing your fat absorption, perhaps? Is it the only change you've made since the KP stopped?

### MamaGrok — 2012-05-04T16:25:15+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-benefits-of-cold-thermogenesis-thread.111/post-21306>

> **Krusing\_tptimal\_in\_CA;21327 wrote:** Nope the KP is still alive and well and I'm hoping it will go. Thou I'll definitely check my vitamin a supplements... Til now ive never been a good supplement taker but after reading so much on the blog I'm convinced to keep at it! Vitamin A is one of those you can OD on so I'll have to be careful, right? ..... Off to go find fermented cod liver oil! Mama grok do you get yours local or online?

I get vitamin A from pastured liver and from fermented cod liver oil (that just means it's made the traditional way, but there is only one producer known in the world still making it that way: Green Pastures, and they're only online, so far as I know. I buy in bulk from their website. It looks very expensive, but the serving size is tiny, so it's really not). SYNTHETIC vitamin A is toxic in not-so-high amounts, but naturally occurring vit A in foods, naturally balanced with vitamin D, is not even close.

### Souldanzer — 2012-05-04T19:08:31+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-benefits-of-cold-thermogenesis-thread.111/post-21359>

Don't be scared of natural vitamin A!! I was doing the WAPF PMS recommendations for quite a few months so I was doing between 20,000 and 50,000 IU of natural vitamin A a day.... never got a sign of toxicity. I think this is one of those vitamins we're all really deficient in.

And while I was obviously deficient in vitamin A, I was also bright orange from too much beta-carotene. It's just not the same thing even though CW keeps telling us that it is.

I'd say eat plenty of liver and make sure your D is up to speed. Or you can drink cod liver oil like I did, yuck.

### Guest — 2012-05-05T14:43:34+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-benefits-of-cold-thermogenesis-thread.111/post-21590>

I wondered why when showering it is much harder to have cold water hit my back then my front, but I might have the answer now. My water runs at 59 and that is excruciating when it hits my back, but okay when hitting my tummy.

Could it be inflammation and CT is fixing it? Went to see my chiro yesterday and he was quite taken back because he couldn't find anything to adjust. I have had upper back problems for 20 years. Rolfing has helped, much, but never took the problem completely away. Then I talked to him about CT and how hard it was to have the cold hit my back. He said that possibly the reason it felt much colder was the inflammation I have dealt with for years and that it was probably why I didn't need an adjustment.

For me, this is just a small indication of what CT can do and gives me the courage to keep going. The first time I heard about CT I intuitively knew this was going to be the key to healing myself--finally. Though, I do feel that when CTing is working it's charm, that it's much like homeopathy and Rolfing and takes us back through emotional trauma while healing. Don't know where to go with this, except, that knowledge is power and I know there is much to presume with both physical and mental/spiritual healing.

### Souldanzer — 2012-05-05T18:04:20+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-benefits-of-cold-thermogenesis-thread.111/post-21622>

I had to teach myself to eat oysters (gag reflex ) so I'm still with the tiny little smoked ones that come in cans in olive oil. I've made progress though and actually like them now, or at least my body keeps asking for them  I do go through 4-6 cans a week right now. Pretty sure it's the zinc but that has no scientific basis, lol. Zinc is known to help with acne and "chicken skin" is actually a form of acne.

Other than that I have 2 1/2lbs of a huge sockeye salmon fillet sitting in the fridge right now waiting to be chowed down on. I also eat 1-2lbs of shrimp a week. Every once in a while mahi mahi or sole fillets, canned sardines, kippered herring, When I went to visit my parents in Europe a few months ago I ate pounds of little smoked fish (Sprotten - anybody know what they're called in English? I love them and would like to find them in the U.S.). I went ahead and ate the whole thing head to tail to the horror of my mother.

All of that would be supplemented by offal. Not much muscle meat right now. I'm really doing this for mTBI recovery but it had this nice little side effect....

Lol, more of an answer than you really cared for

### AKMan — 2012-05-12T01:49:15+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-benefits-of-cold-thermogenesis-thread.111/post-23302>

I CT'd HARD from Feb - April, then quit as it got into spring. I've lately been doing strenuous sprints on a gravel road, jogging/sprinting 2 miles twice a week. My legs have really been stiff and sore the next few days.

Did a mammoth sprint session last night, legs very sore this morning. Didn't want to hobble all weekend, and thought, 'let's try CT'.

Filled the tub with 35 degree water, stood in it as it filled. Ankles screamed in protest! I ignored them as water got to mid-calf, then I knelt and quickly sat in the cold water. Even though it had been a full month, it was no problem. Breathing normal and pain in ankles quickly went away.

Spent 30 minutes in tub, splashing water on face and chest. Got out and was so stiff I could barely walk. Since I didn't want to shiver, I took a hot shower right away. Warmed up and dried off.

Immediately, upon drying off and getting dressed, I realized there was ZERO stiffness or soreness in my legs. I drove to town and did a massive search for building materials at Home Depot, Lowe's , Sam's, and WalMart--lots of walking, stooping, carrying, standing. Several hours later as I sit here typing, my legs feel better than ever. No hint of stiffness. I'm even trying to manipulate them into a position that should provoke a stiff/sore reaction but nada.

Another thread in the Fitness Section talked about CT blunting the effect of exercise--a bad thing--but the effect on recovery a full 12 hours after the exercise is astonishing. So if you exercise a lot, don't CT right away, wait til the next morning. Give it a try!

### TerrierMom — 2012-05-13T08:23:35+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-benefits-of-cold-thermogenesis-thread.111/post-23617>

Interesting.... When I used to do half marathons (not any more of course!!) we used to ice our legs in the hotel tubs after the event, which killed the soreness etc,

. In this case we were no longer trying to train and get stronger but just aid recovery. The articles posted (and AKmans anecdote) suggests that icing after exercise blunts the adaptation (I.e. when exercise breaks down tissue and it rebuilds stronger as it repairs.... Which is why we lift heavy things)... But icing 12 hrs after the exercise allows adaptation to occur but still speeds recovery!

Adaptation sweet spot? Very interesting!!

### rjconser@swbell.net — 2012-05-13T13:54:59+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-benefits-of-cold-thermogenesis-thread.111/post-23631>

Don't see that anyone has yet mentioned another very simply benefit for CT - jet lag.

For 15 years I have travelled regularly from the US to northern Europe. No matter what I have tried, my regular experience is that I wake up at ~2am and stare at the ceiling most of the rest of the night. I usually get my first full night's sleep right before I come home.

From following Jack's posts on effect of CT on stimulating cortisol, it seemed reasonable to me that CT would give a strong 'wake up' shot of cortisol to my body that was stronger even than sunlight - i.e. telling me that 'now' is morning.

Last month, rather than going straight into the office upon arrival in the morning, I checked in at my hotel and took a 20 min bath with coldest tap water (which I guessed to be about 50 deg based on my CT experience at home). You guessed it - slept through the night that night. As an experiment, skipped CT bath the next morning and woke up in the middle of the night again. CT'd again and back to sleeping through the night.

Back again this week. Jumped in the tub first thing again when I arrived in the morning - and slept right on through that night. 20 min CT session every day and slept well each night. Of course, also important to note that daytime altertness was much improved as well.

Even though only 2 experiments in, overall, CT has so far had a truly profound impact on my ability to maintain best possible performance faced with too much long distance travel. By CT'ing first thing on the morning of arrival, I am more alert and sleep well at night and now largely immune to the effects of jet lag. I've shared this with all of my colleagues and the one who has already tried has already reported a similarly strong effect.

Might be hard to do conveniently if you travel to the tropics, but if there's cold water where you're going, I would suggest you might think about a cold bath upon arrival to reset your clock for a more productive trip.

Russ

### Inger — 2012-05-25T07:40:10+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-benefits-of-cold-thermogenesis-thread.111/post-27280>

The Kruse-protocol works!!! After a few Months of following it, I weigh the same as in the before-pics (taken about 3 years ago, eating a keto-diet, a very healthy kind of keto with organs etc. but having it warm 7/24 and lots of artificial lights..)

The left pictures are "before", at the right side the actual shots.

{ "lightbox\_close": "Close", "lightbox\_next": "Next", "lightbox\_previous": "Previous", "lightbox\_error": "The requested content cannot be loaded. Please try again later.", "lightbox\_start\_slideshow": "Start slideshow", "lightbox\_stop\_slideshow": "Stop slideshow", "lightbox\_full\_screen": "Full screen", "lightbox\_thumbnails": "Thumbnails", "lightbox\_download": "Download", "lightbox\_share": "Share", "lightbox\_zoom": "Zoom", "lightbox\_new\_window": "New window", "lightbox\_toggle\_sidebar": "Toggle sidebar" }

.

.

I cannot believe really, that I weigh the same in both pictures, and I am doing no sports and living quite sedentary!

I eat a lot.. I got such a huge appetit from CT! Especially in the beginning. It is better now, but I just ate as much as I wanted when I had the huge appetit. I have started to add in carbs a few weeks ago - I eat them as organic or wild fruit like dried Figs, Berries, Mangos.., wild raw honey / honeycomb, occasionally a little vegtables like carrots and cucumber. I eat wild greens/herbs every day now. Lots of raw coconut oil, and also fresh coconut. No dairy, or very very occasional. It makes me swell up.. uh. Lots of seafood, wildcaught, often raw, as sashimi - I love sashimi, my favorit food. I eat a lot raw, maybe 80% of my food. Some days 100%. Only one cup organic coffee with coconut oil and eggyolk in the morning, more makes me feel... not good. Very occasionally organic red wine.

I try to focus on wild foods as much as I can.

My CT these days (as the summer starts) is mostly in the river by the house, sometimes I sit in there for 15-20 minutes, maybe 1-2 times a week, and otherwise I just stay in a few minutes. Every morning I go there for swimming (very shorthly, just a few minutes), and also evening if I feel like it. The water temp is about 52-55 degrees C right now.

Other benefits to me so far,

\-nicer skin!!! The pores on my nose and face are smaller, nicer skin overall! I always had ugly skin..

\-good sleep.. I always read the news from my computer in bed before sleeping.. about at 9 PM. With sunglasses on and f.lux and the computerscreen on the darkest setting that's possible. It works well, because after about 30 minutes I get soooo sleepy I can barely keep my eyes open! Somedays I sleep really short, like 5-6 hours.. wake up at 4 AM. I am not really tired eather so I guess it does not matter? Somedays I sleep long, like 9 hours. I have the feeling, it is when I have done more/longer CT that I sleep more. But I am not tired in the daytime at all even with little sleep. Nice.. nice.. nice....!!!!!

\-my TOM is.. a little different! Before it was nice and very normal too, but now I have about 30% less bleeding, and overall totally easy and perfect!

I LOVE the protocol!!!!! I am so very thankful to Jack, **he has given me such a great gift**. And I am so thankful to all the members in this forum, that so generousely are sharing their experiences!

Inger

### bigknitwit — 2012-05-25T16:24:46+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-benefits-of-cold-thermogenesis-thread.111/post-27321>

Cting seems to normalize my blood pressure. I am dealing with adrenal issues giving me low blood pressure. Since I also have autoimmune thyroid disease, I am forever trying to balance adrenal supplements with thyroid hormone replacement.

This morning, before CTing (only 20 minutes before my 3 year old needed me), my blood pressure was 91/64, heartrate 94bpm. I wasn't feeling well. Heartrate was compensating for low blood pressure. After the brief CT session, blood pressure was 111/65, with heartrate of 62 bpm. I feel much better! Now to figure out how to get this result first thing in the morning...

### angieonthegogo — 2012-05-26T03:12:28+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-benefits-of-cold-thermogenesis-thread.111/post-27516>

> **Inger;27378 wrote: The Kruse-protocol works!!! After a few Months of following it, I weigh the same as in the before-pics (taken about 3 years ago, eating a keto-diet, a very healthy kind of keto with organs etc. but having it warm 7/24 and lots of artificial lights..)
>
>
>
> The left pictures are "before", at the right side the actual shots.
>
>
>
>
> .
>
>
>
>
>
>
>
>
> .
>
>
>
>
>
> I cannot believe really, that I weigh the same in both pictures, and I am doing no sports and living quite sedentary!
>
>
>
> I eat a lot.. I got such a huge appetit from CT! Especially in the beginning. It is better now, but I just ate as much as I wanted when I had the huge appetit. I have started to add in carbs a few weeks ago - I eat them as organic or wild fruit like dried Figs, Berries, Mangos.., wild raw honey / honeycomb, occasionally a little vegtables like carrots and cucumber. I eat wild greens/herbs every day now. Lots of raw coconut oil, and also fresh coconut. No dairy, or very very occasional. It makes me swell up.. uh. Lots of seafood, wildcaught, often raw, as sashimi - I love sashimi, my favorit food. I eat a lot raw, maybe 80% of my food. Some days 100%. Only one cup organic coffee with coconut oil and eggyolk in the morning, more makes me feel... not good. Very occasionally organic red wine.
>
> I try to focus on wild foods as much as I can.
>
>
>
> My CT these days (as the summer starts) is mostly in the river by the house, sometimes I sit in there for 15-20 minutes, maybe 1-2 times a week, and otherwise I just stay in a few minutes. Every morning I go there for swimming (very shorthly, just a few minutes), and also evening if I feel like it. The water temp is about 52-55 degrees C right now.
>
>
>
> Other benefits to me so far,
>
> \-nicer skin!!! The pores on my nose and face are smaller, nicer skin overall! I always had ugly skin..
>
> \-good sleep.. I always read the news from my computer in bed before sleeping.. about at 9 PM. With sunglasses on and f.lux and the computerscreen on the darkest setting that's possible. It works well, because after about 30 minutes I get soooo sleepy I can barely keep my eyes open! Somedays I sleep really short, like 5-6 hours.. wake up at 4 AM. I am not really tired eather so I guess it does not matter? Somedays I sleep long, like 9 hours. I have the feeling, it is when I have done more/longer CT that I sleep more. But I am not tired in the daytime at all even with little sleep. Nice.. nice.. nice....!!!!!
>
> \-my TOM is.. a little different! Before it was nice and very normal too, but now I have about 30% less bleeding, and overall totally easy and perfect!
>
>
>
> I LOVE the protocol!!!!! I am so very thankful to Jack, he has given me such a great gift**. And I am so thankful to all the members in this forum, that so generousely are sharing their experiences!
>
>
>
> Inger

Excellent job! You have re-motivated a lot of us women...I'm spot CTing right now.

What else did you do for your knee besides CT?

### omlh@todmi.com — 2012-05-26T11:49:14+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-benefits-of-cold-thermogenesis-thread.111/post-27603>

> **indigogirl;27652 wrote:** Great news Olivia!
>
> What else did you do for your knee besides CT?

History was that I dislocated my right patella in October 2010 on my way to the US (ironically for a hiking holiday). Thankfully an orthopaedic surgeon was on board the flight, and put the patella back, and ordered that the crew make sure that I iced it for the remaining six hours of the flight as there were no painkillers available. It was checked out in the US, and at the end of the holiday I returned to the UK, and was put on a waiting list for physio, which started in June 2011. I'm continuing this every day still. The additional extras on my side were to start eating Primally to lose weight (72lbs lost so far), see a Consultant again as since the original injury I have had 18 further dislocations and an MRI scan showed severe ligament and tendon damage as well as a wrecked patella, and I could dislocate the patella with actions as minor as standing up from a chair or going up or downstairs. The second time I saw a consultant, he told me that he would like to replace the patella with an artificial joint, and feed the hamstring tendon through it to stop any further dislocations. I asked for a second opinion, and the Consultant I saw said that he'd like to give physio one more shot as the operation proposed was very radical. He also told me to stop eating grains (which I'd already done) and either supplement with glucosamine or make and consume my own stock - a man after my own heart! This was in January of this year. I saw a new physio who gave ulstrasound treatment, and told me to continue doing the exercises I was doing, but to add weights to my ankle. This really didn't seem to improve things much.

In March I started reading about CT via Glamorama's posts on MDA, and then signed up here and started slowly with CT in mid-March. The improvements health-wise have been dramatic. Not only has my knee gone from needing major surgery to being fine, but I have regained full feeling in a finger which I damaged (severed a nerve, had microsurgery, but never regained proper feeling) 15 years ago, and have regained full lung function (damaged right lung after a severe bout of pneumonia 20 years ago). I have not lost weight following the CT protocol, but I have regained so many other things that I will make sure this is a part of my life from here on in! And maybe some weight loss will follow once the rest of my issues have healed?

### Dali Dula — 2012-05-27T07:02:37+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-benefits-of-cold-thermogenesis-thread.111/post-27805>

> **CoolingWeb;27780 wrote:** Could you please talk about the fat cells apoptosis that you went through with daily CT for 4 months? Detoxing signs?
>
>
>
> How much fat loss total and how much since CT?
>
>
>
> Stay coool,
>
>
>
> Cooling

I had very little noticeable detox symptoms except after a few weeks into the protocol i had a four or five day bout with constipation. These were the hardest \*\*\*\*s ever. Thought my head was going to pop off. They were these super dense long things. i know this is tmi but it was crazy. Then I started pounding Mg. Everything cleared up and i have had no other problems. I do a lot of Mg in the form of nigari which is basically Mg sulfide with trace minerals. My understanding is there is basically no toxicity with Mg. If you start to get loose stools you're taking more than you need. Back off. Mg is the perfect laxative.

As for apoptosis I'm not sure, but as soon as I was doing full immersion ct and not eating 4 hours before bed I became a slomo incredible hulk. I actually started gaining weight while losing bf%. In a 4-6 week period I gained 8 lbs and while losing fat. I'm an active guy and I hiit and sprint, but honestly I was not working hard to make this happen. Someone asked me tonight if there was one thing I would recommend from all of this what would it be. It would be not to eat 4 hours before bed. (Then you should do everything else all of Jack's protocols)

So I don't think you could always recommend CT for weight loss, but it works for fat loss. (and life gain)

### kathylu — 2012-05-27T21:34:01+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-benefits-of-cold-thermogenesis-thread.111/post-27907>

I was having a lot of problems with my left knee. No diagnosis, I was referred to an orthopedist but didn't go. Since starting CT at the end of March, the knee has been slowly improving. No more pain, though I find I am still hesitant to bend this knee when descending stairs. I think it's just psychological though...it's been so painful in the past I am afraid that one wrong step will put me back where I was. I'm really enjoying moving almost normally now. That's enough inspiration for me to keep up the cold treatments!

### vkiernan — 2012-05-29T05:52:28+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-benefits-of-cold-thermogenesis-thread.111/post-28134>

Not sure where to post this but I am thinking the end result is from CT'ing. Starting last Saturday, a week ago, I have not been hungry at lunch time; it was more like eat just a little at 2 just in case I can't make it til 5. All week this has been going on. Yesterday I made it from BAB at 7ish to supper at 4:30 and today BAB at 7 and supper at 5. With my adrenal fatigue I have always been the gotta eat now type when hunger strikes but that has not been the case for a whole week. Got to be more than a fluke since it isn't 1 or 2 days only. I have been doing the reset since July 3rd, 2011.

Maybe TMI, but for those who suffer like I did I want you to now that since my last really bad detox (estrogen dump) that was quite awhile ago, I have had no more breast tenderness. I have had this most of my whole life and it has interferred greatly. It was so bad that 2 weeks out of every month I was plagued. I hope I haven't jinxed myself by reporting but it appears enough time has passed that this is now a bennie for me from CT'ing.

Great signs!

### Souldanzer — 2012-05-29T17:35:26+0300

Starting on primal and adding a lot of magnesium made me able to take fewer painkillers. But last month after doing CT for 2 month, was my first time in 15 years or more when I had NO pain at all! I didn't have to take anything! It's just one month, so no definite conclusion yet, but it's encouraging.

Strangely, it's been getting worse for me. I was able to control it a few years ago with GLA and fish oil but that doesn't work anymore.

The severe pain never fully came back yesterday, which is interesting. I did keep an ice pack on most of the day. My sis has this, too, she used to faint for real regularly... now she takes meds at the first sign of pain.

### angieonthegogo — 2012-05-29T20:20:20+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-benefits-of-cold-thermogenesis-thread.111/post-28211>

> **Souldanzer;28273 wrote:** Strangely, it's been getting worse for me. I was able to control it a few years ago with GLA and fish oil but that doesn't work anymore.

Mine are no longer as long, intense and as debilitating as they used to be. There have been lots of times I couldn't even leave the house or had to call in sick because it was so bad or the headaches or the pain or just the proximity to a bathroom was an issue. I've had a few where I could have passed out from either the pain or from the amount of blood lost. I will get a 'bad' month it seems every few months but in general they have become shorter and less intense. Due to CTing and the detox/ED they aren't always timely...but CTing during generally helps too. There are some months though that during I can't mentally get anywhere near that tub...those months I can't seem to tolerate CT during. I guess overall I'd say my hormones are normalizing but with the CT/detox that can throw a monkey wrench into it but we all know how non-linear this process is. There is significant trending to the correct destination so that's enough to keep me motivated.

### hazyjane — 2012-05-30T20:40:40+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-benefits-of-cold-thermogenesis-thread.111/post-28391>

> **angieonthegogo;28372 wrote:** I just had a random thought: If CT increases testosterone and testosterone can cause thinning hair...am I going to lose all of the lovely new hair growth that I have doing CT? Temporarily? Permanently?  Or is it more about the ratio of the testosterone with the estrogen, progesterone and...what's the other one, pregnenolone...since progesterone is made from pregnenolone? Wondering if there are any long-term side-effects of CT to any of this if we are trying to reverse disease and not following the seasons.
>
>
>
> Ok, back to your regularly scheduled program...

It's not really testosterone but the DHT form that causes hair loss (in men and in some women). Most women lose hair from anemia and/or thyroid issues rather than DHT, unless they have PCOS and have female pattern baldness, which occurs on the top of the head, not all over like thyroid/anemia hair shedding.

### bigknitwit — 2012-06-18T02:08:55+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-benefits-of-cold-thermogenesis-thread.111/post-31844>

I have been using CT lately to control my heartrate, and sometimes my blood pressure. I have been trying to get off synthroid (large dose - 200mcg, hashimoto's for 13 years), and onto Armour, for the past year. It is difficult! My adrenals are struggling to handle the dose to manage my hypothyroid symptoms, and I really don't want to go back on hydrocortisone. When my adrenals struggle, my blood pressure drops, and my heartrate climbs to make up for the lack of needed blood pressure, and I feel awful. I have learned to get in the cold tub, and come out after 25-30 minutes feeling like a new person! My heartrate slows to normal, and blood pressure is raised to normal. I do this even when blood pressure seems "okay", but heart is racing. It is a fix that lasts for several hours. In the mean time, I keep trying to get the Armour where it needs to be. I have started DHEA to hopefully support my adrenals, and not make the cold tubs imperative, but rather something refreshing. I bought the ice vest and will start wearing it at times when I can't walk away from the kids for the tub!

### snapper — 2012-07-01T14:02:29+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-benefits-of-cold-thermogenesis-thread.111/post-34756>

> **Birdy;917 wrote:** Every time I do CT, even just a face dunk, I sleep like a rock that night. People need to know that even if they don't lose a pound or an inch, good sleep is worth its weight in gold. 20 minutes shivering in a tub is much better than 6 hours tossing and turning, and the 8 groggy hours that follow.

I am sleeping the best I have in my adult life since starting CT. I'm only following the protocol loosely, and I've looked at a range of stuff on cold adaptation. I do 5+ mins of face dunking morning and evening. I place a hand in the water (~50 F but often a bit lower) for 5 mins. I am increasingly having cold showers (~ 59 F) for 10 mins. I've tried ice on torso with compression shirt multiple times, but always get mild burns where the pressure is greatest.

But the sleep. Just with face and hand dunks, sleep is wonderful!!
